  - `~/Development/lichess-source/lila/ui/puzzle/src/ctrl.ts`
  - `~/Development/lichess-source/lila/ui/analyse/src/view/main.ts`

Product rules for this phase:
- `Practice Against Them` is a board-led tool, not a dashboard-only surface.
- The opponent should follow imported repertoire only while the underlying data is confident enough to justify it.
- Once repertoire certainty is exhausted, the UI must clearly signal that the engine has taken over and Patzer no longer knows what the opponent would really play.
- Use strong design judgment, but keep the experience honest and operationally clear.

Execution rules:
- This is a grounding/audit step first, not a bundled implementation prompt.
- Do not make code changes unless a tiny source-of-truth doc correction is clearly required to keep the sprint grounded in the current repo reality.
- Be explicit about what is source-confirmed, what is inference, and what remains unknown.

Deliverable:
- identify the exact smallest current board/control seam for a practice state machine inside openings
- identify the closest valid Lichess references for board-led training flow, and say clearly where there is no direct opponent-practice equivalent
- identify the exact files most likely to own practice session state, repertoire move choice, and engine handoff
- call out the highest architectural risk if this tool is bolted directly into `view.ts` without a dedicated owner seam

If you do not edit files, say explicitly that no build was required for this audit step.

Task: audit and extend the existing move-selection service with confidence gating and sparse-branch handling so Practice Against Them can make honest decisions about when repertoire data is strong enough to use

NOTE (2026-03-31): `buildPracticeCandidates()` and `pickWeightedMove()` already exist
in `src/openings/analytics.ts` (lines ~979-1023). They provide weighted random move
selection from opponent frequency distributions. This prompt should NOT recreate them.
Instead, focus on: (1) adding a confidence/sample-size threshold so the selector knows
when data is too sparse, (2) returning a confidence signal alongside the selected move
so the caller can decide when to hand off to the engine, (3) handling edge cases like
zero candidates or very low-frequency branches.

Inspect first:
- Patzer:
  - `src/openings/analytics.ts` — existing `buildPracticeCandidates()`, `pickWeightedMove()`
  - `src/openings/tree.ts`
  - `src/openings/ctrl.ts`
  - `docs/mini-sprints/OPENINGS_OPPONENT_TOOL_SUITE_SPRINT_2026-03-29.md`

Product rules for this phase:
- `Practice Against Them` is a board-led tool, not a dashboard-only surface.
- The opponent should follow imported repertoire only while the underlying data is confident enough to justify it.
- Once repertoire certainty is exhausted, the UI must clearly signal that the engine has taken over and Patzer no longer knows what the opponent would really play.
- Use strong design judgment, but keep the experience honest and operationally clear.

Constraints:
- keep this prompt focused on repertoire-based move selection only
- be explicit about branch confidence, sample-size thresholds, or whatever honest gating logic you choose from the audited data
- do not wire engine takeover in this prompt
- do not hide uncertainty; the selector must know when it no longer has a strong repertoire-backed move

Task: add the honest handoff policy that decides when `Practice Against Them` stops pretending to know the opponent’s repertoire and transitions into engine-driven opponent play instead

Inspect first:
- Patzer:
  - `src/openings/ctrl.ts`
  - `src/engine/ctrl.ts`
  - `src/openings/types.ts`
  - `docs/mini-sprints/OPENINGS_OPPONENT_TOOL_SUITE_SPRINT_2026-03-29.md`

Product rules for this phase:
- `Practice Against Them` is a board-led tool, not a dashboard-only surface.
- The opponent should follow imported repertoire only while the underlying data is confident enough to justify it.
- Once repertoire certainty is exhausted, the UI must clearly signal that the engine has taken over and Patzer no longer knows what the opponent would really play.
- Use strong design judgment, but keep the experience honest and operationally clear.

Constraints:
- keep this prompt focused on handoff policy and ownership, not the full UI shell
- the policy must make it clear when the opponent is still playing from imported repertoire history and when the engine has taken over
- do not fake certainty past the actual repertoire evidence
- if a separate practice helper owner is the cleanest place for this policy, prefer that over burying it in `view.ts`

- `CCP-484` (Phase 1 — Critical Quick Fixes: 9 child prompts)
- `CCP-490` (Phase 2 — Game Library Restructure: 5 child prompts)
- `CCP-496` (Phase 3 — Puzzle Subsystem Scaling: 5 child prompts)
- `CCP-502` (Phase 4 — Server & Sync Hardening: 5 child prompts)
- `CCP-507` (Phase 5 — UI Responsiveness: 4 child prompts)

Manager-prompt rule:
- `CCP-474` is the manager prompt id only
- do not execute or recurse into `CCP-474` as if it were one of the child prompts

Startup coordination step:
- Before editing, check whether any other tool, agent, Claude Code session, or Codex thread is actively touching the same subsystem area this batch needs.
- If overlapping work exists, stop and report it before editing.

Execution order rationale:
Phases MUST be executed in order. Each phase builds on the previous:
- Phase 1 (CCP-484): Quick fixes with no dependencies — safe to run first
- Phase 2 (CCP-490): Game library restructure — foundational architecture change
- Phase 3 (CCP-496): Puzzle subsystem scaling — independent of Phase 2 but ordered after for stability
- Phase 4 (CCP-502): Server & sync hardening — requires Phase 2 per-game records for differential sync
- Phase 5 (CCP-507): UI responsiveness — requires Phase 2 per-game records for pagination

Total: 33 prompts across 5 phases.

For each phase manager:
1. Read the phase manager prompt file from docs/prompts/items/
2. Run `npm run prompt:start -- <phase-manager-CCP-ID>`
3. Execute the phase manager (which will execute its children in order)
4. After phase completes, run `npm run build` for full validation
5. Report phase results before moving to next phase

If a phase manager fails:
- Report what failed and why
- If Phase 1 fails partially, continue to Phase 2 (Phase 1 children are independent)
- If Phase 2 fails, STOP. Phases 4 and 5 depend on it.
- If Phase 3 fails, continue to Phase 4 (independent)

After all phases complete:
- Run `npm run build` for final validation
- Summarize per-phase: which prompts succeeded, which failed
- Update docs/PERFORMANCE_DEBT.md to mark resolved items

Completion step:
- Produce a manual verification checklist covering the most important user-visible behaviors this batch changed.
- After the final summary, run: `npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-474 --checklist "- [ ] item one|- [ ] item two|- [ ] item three"`
- If any children failed, use: `npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-474 --errors "N children failed: CCP-xxx, ..." --checklist "- [ ] item one|- [ ] item two"`

Execution order rationale:
- CCP-475: Fix sync IDB version mismatch (Critical bug, 1-line fix)
- CCP-476: Add 200ms engine throttle (Critical, high impact)
- CCP-477: rAF-gate redraw (High, foundational rendering fix)

- CCP-481: Request persistent storage (quick win, independent)
- CCP-482: Cache filteredSummaries (quick win, independent)
- CCP-483: Cache game summaries in stats controller (medium, independent)

All prompts in this phase are independent (no cross-dependencies). Order is
by severity: critical fixes first, then high-impact, then quick wins.

For each child prompt:
1. Read the child prompt file from docs/prompts/items/
2. Run `npm run prompt:start -- <child-CCP-ID>`
3. Execute the task as described in the child prompt
4. Validate with `npm run build` and `npx tsc --noEmit` (both must pass)
5. Mark child as completed: `npm run prompt:complete -- <child-CCP-ID>` (or with `--errors "..."` if validation failed)
6. Report results before moving to next child

If a child prompt fails:
- Report what failed and why
- Continue to the next child prompt unless the failure blocks it
- Track failures for the final summary

After all children complete:
- Run `npm run build` for a final full validation
- Summarize: which prompts succeeded, which failed, and what remains

Batch prompt IDs to execute in order:
- `CCP-485`
- `CCP-486`
- `CCP-487`
- `CCP-488`
- `CCP-489`

Manager-prompt rule:
- `CCP-490` is the manager prompt id only
- do not execute or recurse into `CCP-490` as if it were one of the child prompts

Startup coordination step:
- Before editing, check whether any other tool, agent, Claude Code session, or Codex thread is actively touching the same subsystem area this batch needs.
- If overlapping work exists, stop and report it before editing.

Execution order rationale:
This phase MUST be executed in strict order. Each step depends on the previous:
- CCP-485: Add per-game IDB store schema (foundation)
- CCP-486: Migrate save path to per-game records (writes to new store)
- CCP-487: Migrate load path to on-demand reads (reads from new store)
- CCP-488: Update deduplication to composite keys (uses new store)
- CCP-489: Update sync client for per-game records (uses new store)

This is the most important architectural change in the performance roadmap.
It unblocks Phase 4 (differential sync) and Phase 5 (game list pagination).

For each child prompt:
1. Read the child prompt file from docs/prompts/items/
2. Run `npm run prompt:start -- <child-CCP-ID>`
3. Execute the task as described in the child prompt
4. Validate with `npm run build` and `npx tsc --noEmit` (both must pass)
5. Mark child as completed: `npm run prompt:complete -- <child-CCP-ID>` (or with `--errors "..."` if validation failed)
6. Report results before moving to next child

If a child prompt fails:
- STOP. Do not continue to subsequent prompts in this phase.
- Report what failed and why.
- Each step is a prerequisite for the next.

After all children complete:
- Run `npm run build` for a final full validation
- Verify: games can be imported, selected, analyzed, and synced
- Summarize: which prompts succeeded, which failed, and what remains

Completion step:
- Produce a manual verification checklist covering the most important user-visible behaviors this batch changed.
- After the final summary, run: `npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-490 --checklist "- [ ] item one|- [ ] item two|- [ ] item three"`
- If any children failed, use: `npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-490 --errors "N children failed: CCP-xxx, ..." --checklist "- [ ] item one|- [ ] item two"`

Task: Update the sync client for differential push and pull using timestamps

Depends on: CCP-499, CCP-500

Inspect first:
- `src/sync/client.ts` — pushToServer and pullFromServer

In pushToServer(): read lastSyncedAt from localStorage, query IDB for records with updatedAt > lastSyncedAt, send only those. In pullFromServer(): include ?since=lastSyncedAt in GET requests, write only returned records. Update lastSyncedAt on success. First sync (no lastSyncedAt) syncs everything. Max 1 file.

- If CCP-499 fails, do not attempt CCP-500 or CCP-501.

- Test sync push/pull if server is available

# CCP-510 — Rename "Repertoire" tool to "Opening Tree"; move prep-zone to Repertoire tool

## Context

The left tool rail on the opponent research session page has four tools:
`repertoire`, `prep-report`, `style`, `practice`.

The `repertoire` tool currently renders the board, opening tree move list, and a
`.openings__prep-zone` analytics block (overview, summary modules, line insight
cards). The prep-zone content belongs on a dedicated Repertoire dashboard, not
embedded in the tree navigation view.

The goal of this prompt is to:
1. Rename the top tool from "Repertoire" to "Opening Tree" with a relevant icon
2. Strip the prep-zone block out of the Opening Tree panel (board + move list only)
3. Add "Repertoire" as the second tool in the rail — rendering the prep-zone content
   that was removed from Opening Tree

## Changes

### `src/openings/types.ts`

Change the `OpeningsTool` type:

```typescript
// Before:
export type OpeningsTool = 'repertoire' | 'prep-report' | 'style' | 'practice';

// After:
export type OpeningsTool = 'opening-tree' | 'repertoire' | 'prep-report' | 'style' | 'practice';
```

### `src/openings/ctrl.ts`

- Replace all `'repertoire'` default/reset values with `'opening-tree'` in:
  `openCollection()`, `closeSession()`, and any `initOpeningsPage` call.
- In the session restore block, add `'opening-tree'` to `validTools` and keep
  `'repertoire'` for backwards compatibility with saved sessions that stored
  `'repertoire'` as the active tool (restore those as `'opening-tree'`).

### `src/openings/view.ts`

**`TOOL_DEFS` array** — update to:
```typescript
const TOOL_DEFS: ToolDef[] = [
  { id: 'opening-tree', label: 'Opening Tree', icon: ICON_BRANCH }, // '\ue003' licon.Branch
  { id: 'repertoire',   label: 'Repertoire',   icon: ICON_BOOK },
  { id: 'prep-report',  label: 'Prep Report',  icon: ICON_BAR_GRAPH },
  { id: 'style',        label: 'Style',        icon: ICON_EYE },
  { id: 'practice',     label: 'Practice',     icon: ICON_SWORDS },
];
```

For the Opening Tree icon, use `'\ue003'` (licon.Branch). If that doesn't render,
fall back to `'\ue004'` (licon.AnalBoard).

**`TOOL_NAMES` map** — add `'opening-tree': 'Opening Tree'` entry.

**`renderSessionPage` dispatch block** — update:
```typescript
...(activeTool() === 'opening-tree'
  ? renderOpeningTreeTool(collection, node, path, redraw)
  : activeTool() === 'repertoire'
    ? [renderRepertoireDashboard(collection, redraw)]
    : activeTool() === 'prep-report'
      ? [renderPrepReportTool(redraw)]
      : [renderToolPlaceholder(activeTool())]),
```

**Rename `renderRepertoireTool` → `renderOpeningTreeTool`** and strip the
following from the panel (move them, not delete them):

Remove from `renderOpeningTreeTool`:
- The `openings__repertoire-header` block (label + game count)
- The `openings__prep-zone` block (overview + summary modules + line insight cards)
- The `openings__zone-divider` that follows the prep-zone

The Opening Tree panel should then be: action menu → color toggle → tree build bar
→ played lines → deviation panel → sample games → move list → explorer → engine.

**Add `renderRepertoireDashboard(collection, redraw)`** — a new function that
renders the prep-zone content as a standalone full-width tool view:

```typescript
function renderRepertoireDashboard(
  collection: ResearchCollection | null,
  redraw: () => void,
): VNode {
  return h('div.openings__tool-content', [
    h('div.openings__prep-zone', treeBuilding()
      ? [h('div.openings__prep-zone-loading', 'Building tree\u2026')]
      : [
          collection ? renderRepertoireOverview(collection) : null,
          renderRepertoireSummaryModules(redraw),
          renderLineInsightCards(redraw),
        ]
    ),
  ]);
}
```

Note: `renderRepertoireDashboard` returns a single `VNode` (not `VNode[]`) because
it spans the full content area like `renderPrepReportTool`. The CSS class
`openings__tool-content` already handles this layout.

**Update `setActiveTool` calls** that previously sent the user back to repertoire:
- In `navToLine` inside `renderPrepReportTool`: `setActiveTool('repertoire')` →
  `setActiveTool('opening-tree')`
- In the trap card click handler: same swap

## Files

- `src/openings/types.ts`
- `src/openings/ctrl.ts`
- `src/openings/view.ts`

## Verification

- Open Opponent Research, open a collection.
- Confirm the top tool rail button shows "Opening Tree" with a branching/tree icon.
- Confirm clicking "Opening Tree" shows the board, move list, explorer, and engine —
  with no overview/summary/insight cards above the move list.
- Confirm clicking "Repertoire" shows the overview, summary modules, and line insight
  cards (the prep-zone content) without a board.
- Confirm Prep Report, Style, and Practice tools are unaffected.
- Confirm navigating to a line from Prep Report switches to "Opening Tree".

# CCP-509 — Fix stuck "importing games" dialogue after returning to library

## Context

When the user completes an import and the collection opens in session view, then
clicks "← Library" to return to the library page, the page shows a stuck
"Importing games…" dialogue instead of the collection list.

## Root cause

In `src/openings/import.ts`, the success path calls `openCollection(collection, redraw)`
which transitions `_currentPage` to `'session'`. However `_importStep` is **never reset**
from `'importing'` to `'idle'` on success.

`closeSession()` in `ctrl.ts` resets `_currentPage = 'library'` but does not touch
`_importStep`. When `renderLibraryPage` runs, it reads `importStep() === 'importing'`
and renders the import workflow panel instead of the collection list.

## Fix

In `src/openings/ctrl.ts`, inside `openCollection()`, reset `_importStep = 'idle'`
before setting `_currentPage = 'session'`. This is semantically correct: entering a
session always means the import workflow is complete.

Specifically, add the reset at line ~350 in `openCollection()`, before or alongside
`_currentPage = 'session'`.

Touch only `src/openings/ctrl.ts` (1 file).

## Verification

- Click "New Research", fill in details, click Import.
- Wait for the import to complete and the session board to appear.
- Click "← Library".
- Confirm the library card list appears, not the "Importing games…" dialogue.
- Confirm "New Research" button is present in the header.

# CCP-508 — Fix time control classification thresholds in timeClassFromTimeControl

## Context

In `src/import/types.ts`, the `timeClassFromTimeControl` function parses a PGN
`TimeControl` header value (e.g. `"300+0"`) and returns a time class string.

The current thresholds are wrong:

```typescript
if (secs < 180)    return 'ultrabullet';  // treats 1+0, 2+1 as ultrabullet
if (secs < 600)    return 'bullet';       // treats 3+0, 5+0 (blitz) as bullet
if (secs < 1800)   return 'blitz';        // treats 10+0, 15+0 (rapid) as blitz
```

This causes blitz games to show up in the bullet column on the opponent research
library cards, and actual bullet games (1+0, 2+1) to vanish entirely because they
are classified as `'ultrabullet'`, which has no display column.

## Correct Lichess thresholds (based on base time only)

| Class       | Base time |
|-------------|-----------|
| ultrabullet | < 30s     |
| bullet      | < 180s    |
| blitz       | < 480s    |
| rapid       | < 1500s   |
| classical   | >= 1500s  |

Note: Lichess uses estimated total time (base + 40 × increment) for its canonical
classification, but a simple base-time approximation is sufficient here.

## Task

Fix `timeClassFromTimeControl` in `src/import/types.ts` to use the correct
thresholds:

```typescript
if (secs < 30)     return 'ultrabullet';
if (secs < 180)    return 'bullet';
if (secs < 480)    return 'blitz';
if (secs < 1500)   return 'rapid';
return 'classical';
```

Touch only `src/import/types.ts`. No other changes needed.

## Files

- `src/import/types.ts` — fix `timeClassFromTimeControl` thresholds (1 file)

## Verification

- Import a known set of Lichess games including 1+0 (bullet), 3+2 (blitz), 10+0 (rapid).
- Open Opponent Research, create a collection with those games.
- Confirm each speed column shows the correct games and ratings.
- Confirm 1+0 games appear under Bullet, not missing entirely.
- Confirm 3+0 / 5+0 games appear under Blitz, not Bullet.

---
id: CCP-514
title: Fix dead recency toggle in Prep Report
type: bugfix
priority: high
category: bugfix
fixes: CCP-362
---

# Fix dead recency toggle in Prep Report

## Problem

`recencyMode()` state exists in `src/openings/ctrl.ts` and is rendered as a toggle in the
Prep Report UI, but toggling "Recent" / "All time" has no visible effect. Two root causes:

1. `setRecencyMode()` does not invalidate `_prepReportCache`, so the view always returns
   the previously cached result.
2. `computeLikelyLineModule()` in `src/openings/analytics.ts` ignores recency mode entirely —
   it always applies recency boosts regardless of which mode is active.

## Acceptance criteria

- Toggling the recency control in Prep Report causes the lines to visibly reorder / change
  frequency display.
- "All time" mode: lines sorted by raw cumulative frequency; no recency boost applied.
- "Recent" mode: lines sorted by recency-boosted score (existing behavior).
- Switching modes re-renders immediately without a page reload.

## Files to touch (max 2)

- `src/openings/ctrl.ts` — invalidate `_prepReportCache` in `setRecencyMode()`
- `src/openings/analytics.ts` — add `recencyMode` parameter to `computeLikelyLineModule()`
  and `computePrepReportLines()`; when mode is `'all-time'` return 1.0 from `recencyBoostFor`
  and sort by raw frequency

## Implementation notes

### ctrl.ts

In `setRecencyMode(mode)`, add `_prepReportCache = null` before or after the existing
state assignment so the next call to `getPrepReport()` recomputes.

Also update `getPrepReport()` (or wherever `computePrepReportLines` is called) to pass
`recencyMode()` as an argument.

### analytics.ts

Change the signature of `computeLikelyLineModule` to accept a `recencyMode: 'recent' | 'all-time'`
parameter (default `'recent'` to preserve existing behavior).

Inside `computeLikelyLineModule`, when `recencyMode === 'all-time'`:
- Replace all `recencyBoostFor(...)` calls (or the inner scorer) with a constant `1.0`
- Sort the final candidates by raw `frequency` (or `cumulativeFreq`) descending instead
  of by the boosted score

Propagate the parameter up through `computePrepReportLines` → caller in `ctrl.ts`.

## Verification steps

1. Navigate to `/openings`, load a collection with enough games.
2. Open the Prep Report tab.
3. With "Recent" selected, note the top lines and their order.
4. Click "All time" — lines should reorder, recency-specific boosts should disappear.
5. Click "Recent" again — order should return to original.
6. Confirm no console errors.

- its `onupgradeneeded` handler is currently a no-op

Task: Create `src/engine/types.ts` with shared engine strength types.

Define and export:
- `EngineMode = 'analysis' | 'play'`
- `EngineStrengthConfig` interface with fields: `level: number`, `label: string`, `uciElo: number`, `maxDepth: number`, `description: string`
- `STRENGTH_LEVELS` constant array with 8 entries:

| Level | Label | UCI_Elo | Max Depth | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beginner | 1320 | 1 | Frequent blunders |
| 2 | Casual | 1500 | 3 | Weak tactics |
| 3 | Club Novice | 1650 | 5 | Basic tactics |
| 4 | Club Player | 1800 | 8 | Solid tactics |
| 5 | Tournament | 2000 | 12 | Strong tactics |
| 6 | Expert | 2200 | 16 | Few mistakes |
| 7 | Master | 2500 | 22 | Near-optimal |
| 8 | Full Strength | 3190 | 30 | UCI_LimitStrength off |

- `DEFAULT_STRENGTH_LEVEL = 4`

Files: `src/engine/types.ts` (new, 1 file)

Acceptance criteria:
- File compiles with `npx tsc --noEmit`
- Types and constants are exported
- `npm run build` passes

Final report requirements:
- Echo `Prompt ID: CCP-567` and `Task ID: CCP-567`.
- Confirm file created and compiles.

- If overlapping work exists, stop and report it before continuing.

Task: Add play-mode UCI option methods to `StockfishProtocol` in `src/ceval/protocol.ts`.

Import `EngineStrengthConfig` from `../engine/types`.

Add three public methods:

1. `setPlayStrength(config: EngineStrengthConfig): void`
   - If config.level < 8: send `setoption name UCI_LimitStrength value true`, `setoption name UCI_Elo value {config.uciElo}`
   - If config.level === 8: send `setoption name UCI_LimitStrength value false`
   - Always send: `setoption name UCI_AnalyseMode value false`, `setoption name Analysis Contempt value Both`

2. `setAnalysisMode(): void`
   - Send `setoption name UCI_LimitStrength value false`
   - Send `setoption name UCI_AnalyseMode value true`
   - Send `setoption name Analysis Contempt value Off`

3. `goPlay(depth: number): void`
   - Send `setoption name MultiPV value 1`
   - Send `go depth {depth}`

Files: `src/ceval/protocol.ts` (1 file)

Acceptance criteria:
- `npm run build` and `npx tsc --noEmit` pass
- Existing analysis flow unchanged (new methods are additive)

Final report requirements:
- Echo `Prompt ID: CCP-568` and `Task ID: CCP-568`.
- List methods added and confirm build passes.

Task: Add engine mode awareness to `src/engine/ctrl.ts`.

Import `EngineMode`, `EngineStrengthConfig` from `./types`.

Add:
- `export let engineMode: EngineMode = 'analysis'`
- `export let playStrengthConfig: EngineStrengthConfig | null = null`
- `export function enterPlayMode(config: EngineStrengthConfig): void`
  - Sets `engineMode = 'play'`, `playStrengthConfig = config`
  - If engine search is active, stops it (send `protocol.stop()`)
  - Calls `protocol.setPlayStrength(config)`
- `export function exitPlayMode(): void`
  - Sets `engineMode = 'analysis'`, `playStrengthConfig = null`
  - Calls `protocol.setAnalysisMode()`
  - If engine was enabled and ready, resumes `evalCurrentPosition()`
- Guard `evalCurrentPosition()` to early-return if `engineMode === 'play'`
- Guard `evalThreatPosition()` to early-return if `engineMode === 'play'`

Files: `src/engine/ctrl.ts` (1 file)

- `npm run build` and `npx tsc --noEmit` pass
- Analysis mode unaffected (guards only activate when mode is 'play')

Task: Design the bestmove routing for play mode.

Inspect first:
- `src/engine/ctrl.ts` lines 538-707 (parseEngineLine and bestmove handling)
- The current bestmove handling path: stale search handling, threat eval, live eval, cache promotion

The current `parseEngineLine()` updates `currentEval`, triggers UI redraws, and interacts with batch/retro. We need to route bestmove responses for play-mode searches without contaminating this path.

Evaluate two options:
A. Add a `_playMoveCallback` slot in ctrl.ts that `parseEngineLine` checks when `engineMode === 'play'`
B. Register a temporary raw `onMessage` listener on the protocol

Document:
- Which approach is cleaner and safer
- Exactly where the guard would go in parseEngineLine
- What happens to `info` lines during play mode (should they be ignored?)
- Any edge cases (what if user navigates during a play-move request?)

Output: design decision — no code changes.

Final report requirements:
- Echo `Prompt ID: CCP-578` and `Task ID: CCP-578`.
- State the chosen approach and rationale.

Source Document: docs/mini-sprints/ENGINE_STRENGTH_LEVELS_SPRINT_2026-03-30.md
Source Step: Phase 3 — Play Move Service / Task 3.2
Execution Target: Claude Code

You are working in `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer`.

Queue execution marker step:
- As the first task before startup coordination or implementation work, run:
  - `npm run prompt:start -- CCP-579`
- Only continue implementation work after that command succeeds.

Startup coordination step:
- Before editing, check whether any other session is actively touching `src/engine/ctrl.ts` or creating `src/engine/playMove.ts`.
- If overlapping work exists, stop and report it before continuing.

Task: Create the shared play-move service.

Create `src/engine/playMove.ts` with:

```typescript
export interface PlayMoveRequest {
  fen: string;
  strength: EngineStrengthConfig;
  onMove: (uci: string) => void;
  onError?: (reason: string) => void;
}
```

Implement:
- `export function requestPlayMove(req: PlayMoveRequest): void`
  - Calls `enterPlayMode(req.strength)` if not already in play mode at this strength
  - Sends `protocol.setPosition(req.fen)`
  - Sends `protocol.goPlay(req.strength.maxDepth)`
  - Installs a one-shot callback via `setPlayMoveCallback()` in ctrl.ts
  - On bestmove: calls `req.onMove(uci)`
- `export function cancelPlayMove(): void`
  - Sends `protocol.stop()`, clears pending callback

Wire into `src/engine/ctrl.ts`:
- Add `export let _playMoveCallback: ((uci: string) => void) | null = null`
- Add `export function setPlayMoveCallback(cb: ((uci: string) => void) | null): void`
- In `parseEngineLine`, at the bestmove branch: if `engineMode === 'play'` and `_playMoveCallback` exists, call `_playMoveCallback(bestmove)`, clear callback, and return early (do NOT update currentEval or evalCache)
- Ignore `info` lines when `engineMode === 'play'` (no eval updates during play mode)

Files: `src/engine/playMove.ts` (new), `src/engine/ctrl.ts` (add callback routing)

Acceptance criteria:
- `npm run build` and `npx tsc --noEmit` pass
- Play-mode bestmove does not contaminate analysis eval state

Final report requirements:
- Echo `Prompt ID: CCP-579` and `Task ID: CCP-579`.
- Confirm files created/modified and build passes.

Task: Add move delay utility to `src/engine/playMove.ts`.

Add:
- `export function playMoveWithDelay(req: PlayMoveRequest, baseDelayMs?: number): void`
  - Default `baseDelayMs = 400` (matches existing `PRACTICE_OPPONENT_DELAY_MS` in openings view)
  - Compute delay: `baseDelayMs + randomInt(100, 400)` scaled by `(strength.level / 8)` — weaker levels "think" less
  - The delay happens BEFORE sending the engine request (simulates thinking time)
  - After the delay, calls `requestPlayMove(req)` which gets the engine response quickly at low depth
- Helper: `function randomInt(min: number, max: number): number` (private)

Files: `src/engine/playMove.ts` (1 file)

Acceptance criteria:
- `npm run build` and `npx tsc --noEmit` pass
- Function exported and callable

Final report requirements:
- Echo `Prompt ID: CCP-580` and `Task ID: CCP-580`.
- Confirm delay logic implemented.

Task: Wire the play-move service into the Practice Against Them engine handoff.

In `src/openings/view.ts`:
- Import `playMoveWithDelay`, `cancelPlayMove` from `../../engine/playMove`
- Import `STRENGTH_LEVELS` from `../../engine/types`
- In `schedulePracticeOpponentResponse()`, at the `plan.action === 'request-engine'` branch (around line 2320):
  - Get `session.strengthLevel`, look up `STRENGTH_LEVELS[session.strengthLevel - 1]`
  - Get the current FEN from the session/board state
  - Call `playMoveWithDelay({ fen, strength, onMove: (uci) => { navigateToMove(uci); recordPracticeMove(uci); syncOpeningsBoard(redraw); redraw(); } })`
  - Remove the existing stub comment about "engine auto-play deferred to follow-up prompt"

In `src/openings/ctrl.ts`:
- Import `cancelPlayMove` from `../engine/playMove` and `exitPlayMode` from `../engine/ctrl`
- In `stopPractice()` or session cleanup: call `cancelPlayMove()` then `exitPlayMode()` to restore analysis mode
- In `closeSession()`: same cleanup if practice was running

Files: `src/openings/view.ts`, `src/openings/ctrl.ts` (2 files)

Acceptance criteria:
- `npm run build` and `npx tsc --noEmit` pass
- Engine now plays a move when repertoire is exhausted during practice
- Stopping practice restores analysis mode

Final report requirements:
- Echo `Prompt ID: CCP-584` and `Task ID: CCP-584`.
- Confirm wiring complete and build passes.

Task: End-to-end validation of Practice Against Them with engine strength levels.

Run:
- `npm run build`
- `npx tsc --noEmit`

Manual test checklist (document for reviewer):
1. Open Opponents page, load a research collection
2. Start Practice Against Them
3. Play moves until repertoire confidence is exhausted
4. Verify the engine responds with a move (not just a banner)
5. Verify the banner shows the strength level info (e.g. "Engine playing at Level 4")
6. Stop practice — verify analysis eval resumes normally
7. Navigate to another page and back — verify no stale state or engine mode leak
8. Start practice again at a different strength level (if UI supports it yet) — verify different play quality

Final report requirements:
- Echo `Prompt ID: CCP-586` and `Task ID: CCP-586`.
- Report test results for each checklist item.
```

```
---
id: CCP-593
title: Fix retro move feedback lost after offTrack state
type: bugfix
priority: high
category: bugfix
---

# Fix retro move feedback lost after offTrack state

## Problem

When the user makes a move during "Learn from your mistakes", instead of receiving
feedback (good move / not best / etc.) the UI immediately shows:

> "You browsed away — Resume learning"

## Root cause

`src/analyse/retroMoveHandler.ts` — the after-move hook gates entry on
`ctrl.retro?.isSolving()`. The `isSolving()` method returns true only when
`_feedback === 'find' || _feedback === 'fail'`.

The timing sequence when a user plays a move from the exercise position:

1. **Before-hook fires** — `feedback = 'find'`, `isSolving()` = true.
   If the move is correct (`uci === bestMove`): `onWin()` sets `feedback = 'win'`.
   If wrong: nothing happens here.
2. **navigate() runs** — path moves from `parentPath` to `parentPath + childId`.
3. **`onJump()` fires inside navigate** — sees `feedback === 'find'` and
   `path !== parentPath` → sets `feedback = 'offTrack'`.
4. **After-hook fires** — `isSolving()` is now **false** (feedback = 'offTrack') →
   hook returns early → no eval state is entered → UI shows offTrack banner.

## Fix

In the after-move hook in `src/analyse/retroMoveHandler.ts`, do not gate on
`isSolving()`. Instead:

- Get `ctrl.retro` directly (not gated on isSolving).
- Still guard: retro must exist, current candidate must exist.
- Skip if `feedback() === 'win'` (correct move already handled by before-hook).
- Perform the `atRetroExercise` path check as before.
- If `atRetroExercise` and `feedback()` is `'offTrack'` (or `'find'`/`'fail'`):
  - If move is wrong: call `retro.setFeedback('eval')` then `retro.onCeval()`.

The before-hook guard on `isSolving()` can remain — it only needs to run during
`'find'` state to pre-set `'win'` before navigate.

## Files to touch (1 file)

- `src/analyse/retroMoveHandler.ts`

## Verification steps

1. Open the analysis board, start Learn from your mistakes.
2. At an exercise position, play a wrong move.
3. Confirm feedback renders (e.g. near-best acceptance or fail state) — NOT "You browsed away".
4. At an exercise position, play the correct move.
5. Confirm win feedback renders as before.
6. Navigate away manually (click a different move in the move list).
7. Confirm "You browsed away / Resume learning" still shows correctly for genuine navigation.
8. No console errors.
```

```
---
id: CCP-594
title: Audit Learn from your mistakes moment selection against Lichess
type: research
priority: normal
category: research
---

# Audit: Learn from your mistakes moment selection vs Lichess

## Context

Users have reported that the moments chosen by "Learn from your mistakes" are
sometimes questionable — in particular: a position is flagged as a mistake, but
when the solution is revealed, it shows the same move the user actually played.
This suggests either the candidate selection thresholds, the solution-move
assignment, or the near-best acceptance logic may be off.

## Goal

Produce a written audit comparing Patzer Pro's retrospection implementation
against the Lichess reference, identifying any divergences that could cause
wrong or misleading moments to surface.

## Reference files

**Lichess source** (at `~/Development/lichess-source/lila`):
- `ui/analyse/src/retrospect/retroCtrl.ts` — candidate selection, win/fail logic
- `ui/analyse/src/retrospect/retroView.ts` — feedback rendering

**Patzer Pro source**:
- `src/analyse/retro.ts` — moment candidate extraction, scoring, thresholds
- `src/analyse/retroCtrl.ts` — feedback state machine, win/fail/eval logic
- `src/analyse/retroMoveHandler.ts` — board move interception
- `src/main.ts` — lines around `strictSolutionMove` assignment (search for that term)

## Questions to answer

1. **Solution move assignment** — Patzer sets `strictSolutionMove: node.uci` in
   at least one path in `src/main.ts`. Is `node.uci` the engine's best move or
   the move the user actually played? If it is the user's move, that directly
   explains "solution = move I played."

2. **Candidate scoring** — Does Patzer's win-chance loss calculation in `retro.ts`
   match Lichess's thresholds? What is the minimum WC loss to flag a moment?
   Does Lichess use a different floor?

3. **Near-best acceptance** — `onCeval()` in `retroCtrl.ts` accepts moves above a
   povDiff threshold. What is that threshold, and does it match Lichess's equivalent?

4. **False-positive candidates** — Are there cases where the classification logic
   in `retro.ts` flags a moment where the user's move was actually equal or better?
   (Timing: evaluated at a low depth vs. deeper cached result?)

5. **Depth dependency** — Does Patzer use analysis depth consistently when selecting
   candidates, or could shallow evaluations cause noise?

## Deliverable

A written report covering:
- Each question above: source-confirmed answer, Patzer value, Lichess value, match/diverge.
- A ranked list of divergences by severity (blocks correct behavior / degrades UX / cosmetic).
- Recommended fix prompts for any confirmed bugs (do not implement — log as separate prompts).

## Note

Do not change any code as part of this audit. Research and report only.

---
id: CCP-595
title: Eval diff for retro move quality — batch manager
type: feature
priority: normal
category: manager
---

# Eval diff for retro move quality — batch manager

## Purpose

Implement the data plumbing and display infrastructure to show the evaluation
difference between the engine's best move and the move the user actually played,
surfaced in the "Learn From Your Mistakes" interface.

The goal: when a user solves (or fails) a retro exercise, the UI can show
something like "the best move gains +2.0 over what was played in the game."
The exact copy/wording is deferred — this sprint builds the backend correctly
so messaging can be layered on top without re-architecting.

## Design principles

- **Engine in secret**: background eval requests for best-move positions that
  are not yet in the eval cache run silently, without affecting the analysis
  board, eval graph, or any visible engine output.
- **Reusable from day one**: the eval-diff utility lives in a shared module
  (`src/analyse/evalDiff.ts`) so puzzles and other tools can import it later
  without duplication.
- **No messaging decisions yet**: CCP-598 adds a placeholder value display.
  The exact copy ("X better than what was played", "You gained X", etc.) is
  a separate future polish task.

## Child prompts — run in order

1. **CCP-596** — Create `src/analyse/evalDiff.ts`: shared pure utility for
   computing and formatting the eval difference between two positions.

2. **CCP-597** — Extend `RetroCandidate` with an `evalDiff` field; populate
   it at session-build time from the existing eval cache; wire a silent
   background engine evaluation pass for candidates where best-move position
   data is missing or shallow.

3. **CCP-598** — Read `cand.evalDiff` in `src/analyse/retroView.ts` and
   render a placeholder diff value in the feedback panel and solution reveal.

## Out of scope for this sprint

- Exact display copy ("X better than what was played")
- Puzzle interface integration (transfer to puzzles is a separate later task)
- EvalDiff display in the move list or eval graph
- Per-move diff history across the whole game

---
id: CCP-596
title: Create shared eval diff utility — src/analyse/evalDiff.ts
type: feature
priority: normal
category: feature
---

# Create shared eval diff utility — `src/analyse/evalDiff.ts`

## Context

Part of CCP-595 (eval diff for retro move quality). This is child prompt 1/3.

"Learn From Your Mistakes" needs to display how much better the engine's best
move was vs. the move actually played. Before wiring this into any view, we
need a clean, reusable utility that computes the diff from eval cache data.

The utility lives in `src/analyse/` (not `src/engine/`) because it is an
analysis-domain concept — it relates positions to each other, not to UCI
protocol. It will be importable by puzzles and other tools later.

## Task

Create `src/analyse/evalDiff.ts`. No other files are touched.

## Interface to implement

```typescript
/**
 * The computed difference between the engine best move and the played move,
 * expressed in both win-chance and centipawn terms.
 *
 * cpDiff and wcDiff are from the mover's perspective:
 *   positive = engine best is better for the mover than what was played.
 *   negative = the played move was actually better (rare; indicates eval noise).
 */
export interface EvalDiff {
  /** Win-chance diff, 0–0.5 scale. Mirrors the existing `loss` field on RetroCandidate. */
  wcDiff: number;
  /**
   * Approximate centipawn diff (full 100cp = 1 pawn scale).
   * Derived from win-chance diff via inverse sigmoid approximation.
   * May be null if the source eval has no cp data (mate-only eval).
   */
  cpDiff: number | null;
  /**
   * Human-readable pawn string, e.g. "+2.0", "+0.5", "mate".
   * Formatted for display; caller decides surrounding copy.
   */
  formatted: string;
  /** Whether the source is high-confidence (derived from cp) or approximate (from wc only). */
  confidence: 'cp' | 'wc-approx';
}

/**
 * Input evals for the diff computation.
 * All values are stored in Patzer's evalCache format.
 */
export interface EvalDiffInput {
  /**
   * Eval at the parent position (the exercise start).
   * cp, if present, is from the side to move at the parent position.
   */
  parentEval: { cp?: number; mate?: number; loss?: number };
  /**
   * Eval at the child position (after the played move).
   * cp, if present, is from the side to move at the child position
   * (i.e., the OPPONENT of the original mover).
   */
  nodeEval: { cp?: number; mate?: number; loss?: number };
  /** Which color played the move being evaluated. */
  playerColor: 'white' | 'black';
}

/**
 * Compute the eval diff between the engine best and the played move.
 *
 * Primary source: nodeEval.loss (precomputed mover-perspective win-chance loss
 * from batch analysis). This is the preferred source because it was computed
 * from both evals in a single analysis pass, avoiding depth-inconsistency.
 *
 * Secondary source: parentEval.cp + nodeEval.cp — used only when loss is absent.
 * In Patzer's eval cache, cp values are stored from the side to move's
 * perspective (Stockfish UCI convention). The child node has the opponent
 * to move, so its cp must be negated to convert to the original mover's
 * perspective.
 *
 * Returns null if neither source yields a usable value.
 */
export function computeEvalDiff(input: EvalDiffInput): EvalDiff | null

/**
 * Format a pawn value for display.
 * Examples: 0.5 → "+0.5", 2.0 → "+2.0", 0.0 → "0.0"
 * Negative values (played move better than engine best) formatted as-is.
 */
export function formatPawns(pawns: number): string
```

## Implementation notes

### Win-chance to approximate centipawn conversion

The `loss` field is on the 0–0.5 win-chance scale (halved). To get an approximate
centipawn equivalent, invert the logistic function used by `evalWinChances`:

```
wcDiff (on full 0–1 scale) = nodeEval.loss * 2
cpApprox = ln(wcFull / (1 - wcFull)) / 0.005   (inverse of the standard Stockfish sigmoid)
```

Cap the output at ±1500 cp (= ±15 pawns) to avoid astronomical numbers from
near-decisive evaluations. For positions where `parentEval.mate` or
`nodeEval.mate` is set, return `formatted: 'mate'` and `cpDiff: null`.

### cp-based path (secondary)

If `nodeEval.loss` is undefined but both cp values exist:

```
// Convert both to original mover's perspective:
moverParentCp = playerColor === 'white' ? parentEval.cp : -parentEval.cp
moverNodeCp   = playerColor === 'white' ? -nodeEval.cp  : nodeEval.cp
cpDiff = moverParentCp - moverNodeCp
```

Note: this path is less reliable because parent and child evals may have been
computed at different depths. Prefer `loss`-based when available.

### Negative diffs

A negative diff means the played move was evaluated as better than `parentEval`
predicted. This can happen due to depth noise. Clamp to 0.0 for display
(show "0.0" rather than "-0.1") but preserve the raw value in the struct.

## File to create

- `src/analyse/evalDiff.ts` — 1 new file, ~60–80 lines

## Validation

- `npm run build` passes
- `npx tsc --noEmit` passes
- No changes to any existing file

---
id: CCP-597
title: Wire eval diff into RetroCandidate + silent background eval for best-move positions
type: feature
priority: normal
category: feature
---

# Wire eval diff into RetroCandidate + silent background eval for best-move positions

## Context

Part of CCP-595 (eval diff for retro move quality). This is child prompt 2/3.
Depends on CCP-596 (`src/analyse/evalDiff.ts`) being complete.

The eval diff utility exists. Now we need to:
1. Add `evalDiff` to `RetroCandidate` so the data travels with each exercise.
2. Populate it at session-build time from existing cache data.
3. For candidates where the played-move eval is missing or too shallow, silently
   request a background engine evaluation — invisible to the user, no arrows,
   no board updates, no ceval display.

## Files to touch (2)

- `src/analyse/retro.ts` — add `evalDiff` field to `RetroCandidate`; populate
  it inside `buildRetroCandidates` using `computeEvalDiff` from CCP-596.
- `src/analyse/retroMoveHandler.ts` — after the session resolves a candidate
  (win or fail), trigger the silent background eval pass for the next
  candidate if its diff is not yet computed.

## Task 1 — Extend `RetroCandidate`

Add to the `RetroCandidate` interface in `src/analyse/retro.ts`:

```typescript
/**
 * Eval difference between the engine best move and the played move.
 * Populated at session-build time when both parentEval and nodeEval are
 * in the cache. May be absent for candidates built before analysis completed
 * or where the played position was never evaluated.
 */
evalDiff?: EvalDiff;   // import from '../analyse/evalDiff'
```

In `buildRetroCandidates`, after creating each candidate, attempt to compute
the diff:

```typescript
const diff = computeEvalDiff({
  parentEval: parentEval,
  nodeEval:   nodeEval,
  playerColor: candidate.playerColor,
});
if (diff) candidate.evalDiff = diff;
```

`parentEval` and `nodeEval` are already in scope from the existing candidate
logic. `nodeEval.loss` is the preferred source (see CCP-596); no new lookups
are needed for the common case.

## Task 2 — Silent background eval for missing/shallow diffs

Some candidates will have `evalDiff === undefined` at session start — either
because the played position is not in the cache (game not yet fully analyzed),
or because the cached eval is too shallow to be reliable.

When these candidates are encountered (either at session start or when advancing
to the next candidate), a one-shot silent engine evaluation of the played-move
position should be requested.

### What "silent" means

- The engine runs on the played-move FEN at a fixed depth (use `retroConfig.missedMateDistance * 6`
  or a hardcoded depth of 18 — whichever is greater, capped at 20).
- The result updates `evalCache` for the played position's path.
- No arrows are drawn, no ceval panel updates, no `redraw()` is triggered.
- The eval is stored by calling `evalCache.set(candidate.path, result)` and then
  `candidate.evalDiff = computeEvalDiff(...)`.
- The engine must be restored to analysis mode (or its previous state) after the
  one-shot completes.

### Triggering the background eval

Add an exported function:

```typescript
/**
 * Request a silent background evaluation of the played-move position for
 * a retro candidate. Used when `candidate.evalDiff` is absent or the
 * source eval is too shallow.
 *
 * The function is a no-op if:
 *   - the engine is currently in analysis or play mode (do not interrupt)
 *   - evalDiff is already populated with confidence === 'cp'
 *   - a background eval for this path is already in flight
 *
 * @param candidate - the candidate whose played position needs evaluating
 * @param getEval   - evalCache getter
 * @param setEval   - evalCache setter (called when result arrives)
 * @param onDone    - called after diff is computed; caller responsible for redraw
 */
export function requestRetroBackgroundEval(
  candidate: RetroCandidate,
  getEval: (path: string) => PositionEval | undefined,
  setEval: (path: string, ev: PositionEval) => void,
  onDone: (diff: EvalDiff) => void,
): void
```

This function lives in `src/analyse/retro.ts` (it is analysis-domain logic,
not board/engine wiring). It should:
- Check that `engineMode === 'analysis'` and engine is idle before proceeding
- Use `enterPlayMode` / `exitPlayMode` is NOT appropriate here — this is
  analysis-mode eval, not play mode. Use the existing UCI path directly
  via `protocol.setPosition(candidate.fenBefore)` + `protocol.goDepth(depth)`,
  treating the result as an analysis eval.
- Actually: since this is a one-off analysis eval, prefer using `evalCurrentPosition`
  seam if it can be directed to a specific FEN, or simply call the existing
  batch eval infrastructure with a single-item queue.
- If the engine is not idle, skip silently (the candidate will display without
  a diff until the next session rebuild).

### Where it is called from

In `src/analyse/retroMoveHandler.ts`, in the after-move hook, after the
current candidate's outcome is resolved (feedback === 'win' or has transitioned
to 'fail'), call `requestRetroBackgroundEval` on the NEXT candidate (not the
current one — the current candidate's diff should already be computed from cache).
This way the eval is ready before the user gets to the next exercise.

Also call it once when the retro session is first built (for the first candidate),
if its diff is absent.

## What this does NOT do

- Does not change the analysis board's eval display
- Does not show any engine output or arrows for the background eval
- Does not interrupt live analysis if the engine is running
- Does not introduce a second engine instance

## Validation

- `npm run build` passes
- `npx tsc --noEmit` passes
- Starting a retro session with a reviewed game: first candidate has `evalDiff`
  populated (check via `console.log` — diff should be present for positions in cache)
- Engine does not show extra arrows or ceval output during a retro session

# Render eval diff placeholder in retroView feedback and solution panels

## Context

Part of CCP-595 (eval diff for retro move quality). This is child prompt 3/3.
Depends on CCP-596 and CCP-597 being complete (`evalDiff` field populated on
`RetroCandidate`, `EvalDiff` type and `formatPawns` available).

The data is wired. Now surface it in the UI — as a placeholder that shows the
numeric value. Final copy/messaging ("That move gains +2.0 over what was played
in the game", etc.) is a separate future polish task.

## File to touch (1)

- `src/analyse/retroView.ts`

## Where to display

### 1. Win feedback panel (feedback === 'win')

After the existing "Best move" or "Well done" text, append the eval diff if
`cand.evalDiff` is available. Example placeholder:

```
[existing win text]
Diff: +2.0
```

Only show if `cand.evalDiff` exists. If the diff is 0.0 or negative (played
move matched or exceeded best), show nothing — do not show "Diff: 0.0".

### 2. Fail feedback panel (feedback === 'fail')

After the existing "Better, but not the best move" / "That move is even worse"
text, show the diff. Example:

```
[existing fail text]
Diff: +1.5 vs your move
```

Again, only when `cand.evalDiff` is populated.

### 3. Solution reveal panel (feedback === 'view')

After the solution move SAN, show the diff. Example:

```
Best was Nf3
Diff: +2.0
```

Only when `cand.evalDiff` is populated.

## Implementation notes

- Import `formatPawns` from `'./evalDiff'`.
- The diff value to display is `cand.evalDiff.formatted` — already formatted
  by the utility (e.g., "+2.0").
- Wrap in a `h('span.retro-feedback__eval-diff', ...)` or similar minimal
  Snabbdom element so it can be styled separately later.
- Do NOT add any CSS rules yet — the element will inherit existing text styles.
  A dedicated styling task will follow.
- Gate every reference with `cand.evalDiff != null` — never assume it's present.

## Example Snabbdom pattern

```typescript
// In the appropriate feedback section:
...(cand.evalDiff
  ? [h('span.retro-feedback__eval-diff', `Diff: ${cand.evalDiff.formatted}`)]
  : []),
```

## What this does NOT do

- Does not change any logic or data — display only
- Does not add CSS or styling
- Does not change copy/wording of existing feedback text
- Does not display diff for candidates that haven't been resolved yet (find state)

## Validation

- `npm run build` passes
- `npx tsc --noEmit` passes
- Open "Learn from your mistakes" on a fully reviewed game
- Solve an exercise correctly → win panel shows "Diff: +X.X" (if diff available)
- Fail an exercise → fail panel shows "Diff: +X.X" (if diff available)
- Click "Show solution" → solution panel shows "Diff: +X.X" (if diff available)
- Candidates with no eval diff available → no "Diff:" text shown at all

Task: fix the delayed play-move follow-up so canceling during the pre-send thinking window actually prevents the later engine request from firing.

Review-grounded bug to confirm:
- `src/engine/playMove.ts` schedules delayed requests with `setTimeout`
- `cancelPlayMove()` only cancels a request that has already been sent to the engine
- pending timers are not cleared, so a stale delayed callback can still dispatch later

Inspect first:
- Patzer:
  - `src/engine/playMove.ts`
  - call sites that use `playMoveWithDelay()` and `cancelPlayMove()`
  - any related openings practice flow that depends on cancel safety
- Relevant Lichess reference:
  - inspect only if there is a comparable cancellation pattern; otherwise keep the fix local to the current service

- keep the fix scoped to timer cancellation / stale callback safety

- if validation failed or another blocker remained, run:

- close out the review in the registry with the required provenance fields

- then run `npm run prompts:refresh`

# CCP-606 — Dashboard refresh modal with server setup instructions

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

The prompt tracking dashboard (`docs/prompts/dashboard.html`) has a **Refresh Data** button.

When the dashboard is opened via the `file://` protocol (i.e. opened directly in the browser
from the filesystem), clicking Refresh shows a plain `alert()` telling the user to run
`npm run prompts:dashboard` and reload.

The user wants this replaced with a proper modal that gives **two side-by-side options**:

1. **Static rebuild** — run the npm command in terminal, then reload
2. **Live server** — start the dev server and open the dashboard at localhost

Both options should have code boxes with copy-to-clipboard buttons.

## Target file

`docs/prompts/dashboard.html` — this is a **generated file** but the generation script
embeds the HTML/JS directly. The actual source to edit is the **generator script**:

`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

Check which file contains the refresh button logic and modal HTML before editing.
The refresh button click handler is near the bottom of the embedded `<script>` block.

## Requirements

### Modal design

- Triggered when Refresh is clicked while `location.protocol === 'file:'`
- Overlay backdrop (semi-transparent dark)
- Modal card with:
  - Title: "Refresh Options"
  - Subtitle: "Opened via file:// — live refresh is unavailable. Choose an option below."
  - Two side-by-side panels:

**Panel 1 — Static Rebuild**
- Heading: "Rebuild & Reload"
- Description: "Run this command in your terminal, then reload the page."
- Code box: `npm run prompts:dashboard`
- Copy button on the code box
- Note: "Then reload this page."

**Panel 2 — Live Server (Recommended)**
- Heading: "Use Live Server"
- Description: "Start the dev server to get live auto-refresh."
- Code box: `node server.mjs`
- Copy button
- Then a second line: "Then open:"
- Code box: `http://localhost:3001/docs/prompts/dashboard.html`
- Copy button
- Note: "Live mode fetches registry data directly — no rebuild needed."

- Close button (×) in top-right corner of modal
- Clicking the backdrop also closes the modal
- ESC key closes the modal

### Styling

- Match the existing dashboard dark theme (CSS variables already in the file)
- Modal should be centered, max-width ~600px, responsive on narrow viewports
- Code boxes use monospace font, dark background, slightly lighter than the card background
- Copy buttons: small, inline, positioned to the right of the code text
- "Copied!" feedback text replaces button label for 2 seconds after click

### What NOT to change

- Do not change the live-mode refresh behavior (when `location.protocol !== 'file:'`)
- Do not change any other part of the dashboard HTML/JS
- Do not add new npm scripts or server routes
- Touch only the file(s) that contain the refresh button logic

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Then open `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` via `file://` in a browser and:
- Click "Refresh Data"
- Confirm the modal appears with two panels
- Confirm copy buttons work
- Confirm ESC and backdrop click close the modal

No TypeScript compilation needed — this is a pure HTML/JS change inside the generator.

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-606
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-606 --checklist "- [ ] Open docs/prompts/dashboard.html via file:// in a browser|- [ ] Click 'Refresh Data' — confirm modal appears (not an alert)|- [ ] Confirm two side-by-side panels: 'Rebuild & Reload' and 'Use Live Server'|- [ ] Click Copy next to 'npm run prompts:dashboard' — confirm clipboard copy and 'Copied!' feedback|- [ ] Click Copy next to 'node server.mjs' — confirm copy works|- [ ] Click Copy next to the localhost URL — confirm copy works|- [ ] Press ESC — confirm modal closes|- [ ] Click backdrop area outside modal — confirm modal closes|- [ ] Click × button — confirm modal closes|- [ ] Run 'node server.mjs' and open http://localhost:3001/docs/prompts/dashboard.html — confirm Refresh button still works in live mode (no modal)"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-606 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Open docs/prompts/dashboard.html via file:// in a browser|- [ ] Click 'Refresh Data' — confirm modal appears (not an alert)|- [ ] Confirm two side-by-side panels: 'Rebuild & Reload' and 'Use Live Server'|- [ ] Click Copy next to 'npm run prompts:dashboard' — confirm clipboard copy and 'Copied!' feedback|- [ ] Click Copy next to 'node server.mjs' — confirm copy works|- [ ] Click Copy next to the localhost URL — confirm copy works|- [ ] Press ESC — confirm modal closes|- [ ] Click backdrop area outside modal — confirm modal closes|- [ ] Click × button — confirm modal closes|- [ ] Run 'node server.mjs' and open http://localhost:3001/docs/prompts/dashboard.html — confirm Refresh button still works in live mode (no modal)"
```

# CCP-607 — Dashboard next-up urgent block and stable sort

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

The prompt tracking dashboard (`docs/prompts/dashboard.html`) has a "Next Up" sort view.
Currently within `queued-pending`, prompts sort by earliest ID first — so when a prompt
above another gets run, everything below it jumps up a position. This is disorienting.

Two changes are needed:

1. **Stable sort within `queued-pending`**: sort by `createdAt` descending (newest first),
   with priority tier as the primary sort key (`critical → high → normal → low`), then
   `createdAt` descending within each tier. This keeps positions stable regardless of
   which other prompts get run.

2. **Urgent block**: A highlighted callout section pinned above the main prompt table,
   visible only when "Next Up" is the active sort. It shows any `queued-pending` prompt
   that meets at least one of:
   - `priority === 'critical'`
   - `priority === 'high'`
   - `category === 'bugfix'`
   - `category === 'typecheck'`

   The same prompts also appear in their normal position in the main list below.
   No cap on the number of items in the urgent block.

## Target file

`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs` — the generator. Do not hand-edit `dashboard.html`.

## Requirements

### 1. Stable sort for `queued-pending` in "next up"

Update `nextUpRank` and the `next-up` sort case in `sortPrompts()`:

Within rank 0 (`queued-pending`), replace the current `idNum(a) - idNum(b)` tie-break with:
- Primary: priority tier (`critical`=0, `high`=1, `normal`=2, `low`=3, anything else=2)
- Secondary: `createdAt` descending (newest first)
- Tertiary: `idNum` descending as a final stable tie-break

Ranks 1 and 2 (`queued-started`, `queued-run`) and rank 3 (everything else) are unchanged.

### 2. Urgent block HTML

Insert a `<div id="urgent-block">` between the toolbar and the `<table>` element in the
generated HTML body. It should be hidden by default and shown only when:
- `sortPreset === 'next-up'`
- there is at least one qualifying urgent prompt

Structure:
```
div#urgent-block
  div.urgent-block__header
    span  "Needs Attention"
    span.urgent-block__count  "N prompts"
  div.urgent-block__list
    div.urgent-row  (one per urgent prompt, same as a simplified table row)
      span.status-badge  (reuse existing status badge styles)
      span.urgent-row__id   CCP-###
      span.urgent-row__title  prompt title
      span.urgent-row__meta  priority · category
```

Clicking an urgent row should open the detail panel for that prompt (same behavior as
clicking a table row — call the existing `openDetail(p)` or equivalent function).

The urgent block must be updated whenever `renderList()` is called — show/hide and
repopulate based on current sort and filter state.

### 3. Styling

Match the existing dark theme. Use an amber/orange left-border accent:

```css
#urgent-block {
  /* amber left border, subtle warm background */
  border-left: 3px solid #e8a040;
  background: rgba(232, 160, 64, 0.06);
  border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0;
  margin-bottom: 16px;
  padding: 12px 16px;
}
.urgent-block__header {
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  gap: 8px;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
  font-size: 0.8rem;
  font-weight: 700;
  color: #e8a040;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  letter-spacing: 0.05em;
}
.urgent-block__count {
  font-weight: 400;
  color: var(--text-dim);
  text-transform: none;
  letter-spacing: 0;
}
.urgent-row {
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  gap: 10px;
  padding: 6px 0;
  border-top: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.05);
  cursor: pointer;
  font-size: 0.82rem;
}
.urgent-row:hover { background: rgba(255,255,255,0.03); }
.urgent-row:first-child { border-top: none; }
.urgent-row__id {
  font-weight: 600;
  color: var(--text-dim);
  white-space: nowrap;
  min-width: 70px;
}
.urgent-row__title {
  flex: 1;
  color: var(--text);
  white-space: nowrap;
  overflow: hidden;
  text-overflow: ellipsis;
}
.urgent-row__meta {
  font-size: 0.72rem;
  color: var(--text-dim);
  white-space: nowrap;
}
```

### What NOT to change

- Do not change any other sort preset behavior
- Do not change the table rendering logic
- Do not change the filter button behavior
- Do not add new npm scripts

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Open `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` in a browser and verify:

1. With "Next Up" selected:
   - Urgent block appears above the table if any `queued-pending` prompts qualify
   - Amber left border and header are visible
   - Each urgent row shows ID, title, priority · category
   - Clicking an urgent row opens the detail panel
   - Urgent prompts also appear in their normal position in the main table below

2. Switch to any other sort preset — urgent block disappears

3. The `queued-pending` prompts in the main table are now sorted newest-first within
   priority tier, not by ID ascending

No TypeScript compilation needed — pure HTML/JS change in the generator.

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-607
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-607 --checklist "- [ ] Open dashboard.html in browser, select 'Next Up' sort — confirm amber 'Needs Attention' block appears above the table|- [ ] Confirm the block shows only queued-pending prompts that are critical, high, bugfix, or typecheck|- [ ] Confirm each row shows status badge, CCP ID, title, and priority · category meta|- [ ] Click an urgent row — confirm the detail panel opens for that prompt|- [ ] Confirm the same prompts also appear in their normal position in the main table below|- [ ] Switch to any other sort preset (e.g. ID newest first) — confirm urgent block disappears|- [ ] Switch back to Next Up — confirm urgent block reappears|- [ ] In the queued-pending section of the main table, confirm prompts are now sorted by priority tier (critical first, then high, then normal) and newest-first within each tier — not by ID ascending"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-607 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Open dashboard.html in browser, select 'Next Up' sort — confirm amber 'Needs Attention' block appears above the table|- [ ] Confirm the block shows only queued-pending prompts that are critical, high, bugfix, or typecheck|- [ ] Confirm each row shows status badge, CCP ID, title, and priority · category meta|- [ ] Click an urgent row — confirm the detail panel opens for that prompt|- [ ] Confirm the same prompts also appear in their normal position in the main table below|- [ ] Switch to any other sort preset (e.g. ID newest first) — confirm urgent block disappears|- [ ] Switch back to Next Up — confirm urgent block reappears|- [ ] In the queued-pending section of the main table, confirm prompts are now sorted by priority tier (critical first, then high, then normal) and newest-first within each tier — not by ID ascending"
```

# CCP-608 — Dashboard refresh button hard reload after live fetch

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

The prompt tracking dashboard (`docs/prompts/dashboard.html`) has a Refresh Data button.
In live mode (served via HTTP, `location.protocol !== 'file:'`), clicking Refresh calls
`fetchLiveData()` and re-renders the table in place — but does not reload the page.

The user wants the button to also force a hard reload (cache-busted) after the live
fetch completes, so the full page asset (HTML, CSS, JS) is always fresh.

## Target file

`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs` — the generator. Do not hand-edit `dashboard.html`.

The live-mode branch of the refresh button handler looks like this:

```js
} else {
  // Live mode: re-fetch registry data directly
  fetchLiveData().then(() => {
    btn.textContent = 'Refresh Data';
    btn.disabled = false;
  });
}
```

## Change required

After `fetchLiveData()` resolves, trigger a hard reload using:

```js
location.href = location.href.split('?')[0] + '?t=' + Date.now();
```

This strips any existing query string and appends a cache-busting timestamp, forcing
the browser to re-request the page without using a cached copy.

The button state reset (`btn.textContent`, `btn.disabled`) can be removed from the
`.then()` since the page will reload anyway — but leave a brief comment explaining why.

## What NOT to change

- Do not change the `file:` branch (the modal)
- Do not change any other part of the refresh handler
- Do not add new npm scripts or server routes

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Start the dev server (`node server.mjs`) and open
`http://localhost:3001/docs/prompts/dashboard.html`.

- Click Refresh Data
- Confirm the page does a full reload (URL gains `?t=<timestamp>` briefly, then page reloads)
- Confirm the reloaded page shows fresh data

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-608
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:

# CCP-609 — Dashboard next-up flat creation-order sort

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

The "Next Up" sort in the prompt dashboard currently groups prompts by queue state
(queued-pending at top, queued-started next, queued-run below, etc.) and sorts within
each group by priority tier then createdAt. This causes prompts to jump positions when
their status changes.

The user wants the "Next Up" sort to be a flat list of ALL prompts, sorted by `createdAt`
ascending (oldest first, newest at the bottom). No grouping. No rank. A prompt's position
never changes regardless of status updates. New prompts always appear at the bottom.

## Target file

`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

## Changes required

### 1. Replace the `next-up` sort case in `sortPrompts()`

The current `next-up` case uses `nextUpRank()` and `priorityTier()` for multi-tier
grouping. Replace it with a simple `createdAt` ascending sort with `idNum` ascending
as tie-break:

```js
case 'next-up':
  return (a.createdAt || '').localeCompare(b.createdAt || '') || idNum(a) - idNum(b);
```

### 2. Remove `nextUpRank()` and `priorityTier()` functions

These are no longer used by any sort preset after this change. Remove both functions
and their comments to keep the file clean.

Note: `priorityTier()` is also used inside `renderUrgentBlock()`. Before removing it,
inline the priority tier logic directly into `renderUrgentBlock()`'s sort comparator:

```js
const urgent = PROMPTS.filter(isUrgent).sort((a, b) => {
  const TIER = { critical: 0, high: 1, low: 3 };
  const pa = TIER[a.priority] ?? 2, pb = TIER[b.priority] ?? 2;
  if (pa !== pb) return pa - pb;
  return (b.createdAt || '').localeCompare(a.createdAt || '') || idNum(b) - idNum(a);
});
```

### What NOT to change

- Do not change any other sort preset
- Do not change `renderUrgentBlock()` behavior beyond inlining the priority tier logic
- Do not change the urgent block show/hide logic
- Do not change filter behavior

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Open the dashboard and select "Next Up":
- Confirm all prompts appear (not just queued-pending)
- Confirm oldest prompts (lowest CCP numbers) are at the top
- Confirm newest prompts (highest CCP numbers) are at the bottom
- Confirm the order does not change when switching away and back to Next Up
- Confirm the urgent block still appears for qualifying prompts

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-609
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-609 --checklist "- [ ] Open dashboard at localhost, select 'Next Up' sort|- [ ] Confirm all 659 prompts appear (not filtered by queue state)|- [ ] Confirm CCP-1 or earliest prompts are at the top, CCP-609 is near the bottom|- [ ] Switch to a different sort and back to Next Up — confirm order is unchanged|- [ ] Confirm urgent block still appears for any qualifying queued-pending prompts"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-609 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Open dashboard at localhost, select 'Next Up' sort|- [ ] Confirm all 659 prompts appear (not filtered by queue state)|- [ ] Confirm CCP-1 or earliest prompts are at the top, CCP-609 is near the bottom|- [ ] Switch to a different sort and back to Next Up — confirm order is unchanged|- [ ] Confirm urgent block still appears for any qualifying queued-pending prompts"
```

# CCP-614 — Dashboard header bulk-copy buttons for needs-review and issues-found

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

The dashboard header has a tab toggle on the left (Prompts / Sprints). The right side of
the tab bar is currently empty. Add two bulk-copy buttons there:

1. **Copy Needs Review IDs** — copies all prompt IDs where `queueState === 'queued-run'`
2. **Copy Issues Found IDs** — copies all prompt IDs where `reviewOutcome === 'issues found'`
   or `reviewOutcome === 'needs rework'`

## Target file

`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

## Behaviour

Both buttons copy a numbered, comma-separated list of IDs to the clipboard. Format:

```
1. CCP-323, 2. CCP-355, 3. CCP-363, ...
```

If no prompts qualify, the button should flash a brief "None" feedback instead of copying.

After a successful copy, the button label changes to "✓ Copied!" for 1.5 seconds then
reverts to its original label.

## HTML

Add the two buttons to the right side of the existing `.tabs` bar. The tabs bar currently
looks like:

```html
<div class="tabs">
  <button class="tab-btn active" data-tab="prompts">Prompts</button>
  <button class="tab-btn" data-tab="sprints">Sprints</button>
</div>
```

Update it to:

```html
<div class="tabs">
  <button class="tab-btn active" data-tab="prompts">Prompts</button>
  <button class="tab-btn" data-tab="sprints">Sprints</button>
  <div class="tabs__bulk-actions">
    <button class="bulk-copy-btn" id="copy-needs-review">Copy Needs Review IDs</button>
    <button class="bulk-copy-btn" id="copy-issues-found">Copy Issues Found IDs</button>
  </div>
</div>
```

## Styling

The tabs bar already has `display: flex; align-items: center`. Add:

```css
.tabs__bulk-actions {
  margin-left: auto;
  display: flex;
  gap: 8px;
}
.bulk-copy-btn {
  background: var(--bg-card);
  color: var(--text-dim);
  border: 1px solid var(--border);
  border-radius: 4px;
  font-size: 0.75rem;
  padding: 4px 10px;
  cursor: pointer;
  white-space: nowrap;
  transition: background 0.15s, color 0.15s;
}
.bulk-copy-btn:hover { background: #2a2a2a; color: var(--text); }
.bulk-copy-btn.copied { color: var(--accent); border-color: var(--accent); }
.bulk-copy-btn.empty { color: var(--text-dim); border-color: var(--border); }
```

## JS

Add click handlers for both buttons in the `<script>` block:

```js
document.getElementById('copy-needs-review').addEventListener('click', () => {
  const ids = PROMPTS
    .filter(p => p.queueState === 'queued-run')
    .map((p, i) => (i + 1) + '. ' + p.id);
  bulkCopy(document.getElementById('copy-needs-review'), ids);
});

document.getElementById('copy-issues-found').addEventListener('click', () => {
  const ids = PROMPTS
    .filter(p => p.reviewOutcome === 'issues found' || p.reviewOutcome === 'needs rework')
    .map((p, i) => (i + 1) + '. ' + p.id);
  bulkCopy(document.getElementById('copy-issues-found'), ids);
});

function bulkCopy(btn, ids) {
  if (ids.length === 0) {
    const orig = btn.textContent;
    btn.textContent = 'None';
    btn.classList.add('empty');
    setTimeout(() => { btn.textContent = orig; btn.classList.remove('empty'); }, 1500);
    return;
  }
  navigator.clipboard.writeText(ids.join(', ')).then(() => {
    const orig = btn.textContent;
    btn.textContent = '\u2713 Copied!';
    btn.classList.add('copied');
    setTimeout(() => { btn.textContent = orig; btn.classList.remove('copied'); }, 1500);
  });
}
```

## What NOT to change

- Do not change tab switching behavior
- Do not change any other part of the header or toolbar
- Do not add new npm scripts

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Open the dashboard and verify:
- Two buttons appear on the right side of the tabs bar
- "Copy Needs Review IDs" copies a numbered comma-separated list of all `queued-run` prompt IDs
- "Copy Issues Found IDs" copies a numbered comma-separated list of all `issues found` / `needs rework` prompt IDs
- Paste into a text editor to confirm the format: `1. CCP-xxx, 2. CCP-yyy, ...`
- If no prompts qualify, button shows "None" briefly
- Both buttons show "✓ Copied!" feedback for 1.5 seconds on success

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-614
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-614 --checklist "- [ ] Open dashboard, confirm two buttons appear on the right side of the Prompts/Sprints tab bar|- [ ] Click 'Copy Needs Review IDs' — paste into text editor, confirm format '1. CCP-xxx, 2. CCP-yyy, ...'|- [ ] Click 'Copy Issues Found IDs' — paste and confirm only issues found/needs rework prompts are listed|- [ ] Confirm '✓ Copied!' feedback appears for 1.5s then reverts|- [ ] If all prompts of a type are cleared, confirm button shows 'None' briefly"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh

CCP-yyy, ...'|- [ ] Click 'Copy Issues Found IDs' — paste and confirm only issues found/needs rework prompts are listed|- [ ] Confirm '✓ Copied!' feedback appears for 1.5s then reverts|- [ ] If all prompts of a type are cleared, confirm button shows 'None' briefly"
```
