# CCP-615 — Dashboard simplify filter buttons to 7 presets

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

The dashboard toolbar has filter buttons driven by `STATUS_FILTERS` in
`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`. The current set has 15+ filters including
category and priority filters. The user wants this simplified to exactly 7 filters.

## Target file

`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

## New filter set (in order)

| Label | Matches |
|---|---|
| All | all prompts (empty filter) |
| Managers | `p.kind === 'manager'` |
| Ready | `p.queueState === 'queued-pending'` → statusClass `status--ready` |
| Started | `p.queueState === 'queued-started'` → statusClass `status--started` |
| Errors | `statusClass === 'status--run-errors'` OR `reviewOutcome === 'issues found'` OR `reviewOutcome === 'needs rework'` |
| Completed | `statusClass === 'status--run'` (queued-run, no errors) |
| Passed Review | `statusClass === 'status--passed'` |

## Changes required

### 1. Replace `STATUS_FILTERS`

```js
const STATUS_FILTERS = [
  { label: 'All',         value: '' },
  { label: 'Managers',    value: 'kind:manager' },
  { label: 'Ready',       value: 'status--ready' },
  { label: 'Started',     value: 'status--started' },
  { label: 'Errors',      value: 'multi:errors' },
  { label: 'Completed',   value: 'status--run' },
  { label: 'Passed Review', value: 'status--passed' },
];
```

### 2. Update `filterPrompts()` to handle the new filter values

The existing filter logic handles `cat:`, `prio:`, and statusClass matching.
Add two new prefix handlers before the `else` fallback:

```js
} else if (filterStatus === 'kind:manager') {
  list = list.filter(p => p.kind === 'manager');
} else if (filterStatus === 'multi:errors') {
  list = list.filter(p =>
    p.statusClass === 'status--run-errors' ||
    p.reviewOutcome === 'issues found' ||
    p.reviewOutcome === 'needs rework'
  );
} else {
  list = list.filter(p => p.statusClass === filterStatus);
}
```

### What NOT to change

- Do not change the category or priority filter infrastructure (it may be used elsewhere)
- Do not change the filter button rendering logic beyond updating `STATUS_FILTERS`
- Do not change any other toolbar behavior

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Open the dashboard and verify:
- Exactly 7 filter buttons appear: All, Managers, Ready, Started, Errors, Completed, Passed Review
- Each filter correctly narrows the prompt list
- "Errors" shows prompts with run-errors, issues found, OR needs rework
- "Managers" shows only manager-kind prompts
- "All" clears the filter and shows all prompts

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-615
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-615 --checklist "- [ ] Open dashboard and confirm exactly 7 filter buttons appear: All, Managers, Ready, Started, Errors, Completed, Passed Review|- [ ] Click 'Managers' and verify only manager-kind prompts are shown|- [ ] Click 'Errors' and verify prompts with run-errors, issues found, or needs rework are shown|- [ ] Click 'All' and verify full prompt list is restored|- [ ] Click 'Ready', 'Started', 'Completed', 'Passed Review' and verify each narrows correctly"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-615 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Open dashboard and confirm exactly 7 filter buttons appear: All, Managers, Ready, Started, Errors, Completed, Passed Review|- [ ] Click 'Managers' and verify only manager-kind prompts are shown|- [ ] Click 'Errors' and verify prompts with run-errors, issues found, or needs rework are shown|- [ ] Click 'All' and verify full prompt list is restored|- [ ] Click 'Ready', 'Started', 'Completed', 'Passed Review' and verify each narrows correctly"
```

# CCP-616 — AI Guided Questions mode for prompt creation

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

The current prompt creation process is "Quick Create" — Claude infers intent and writes
the prompt body with minimal questioning. This produces prompts that lack precise design
decisions, often leaving ambiguity around placement, behavior, Lichess parity, edge cases,
and acceptance criteria.

The user wants a second mode called **AI Guided Questions** where Claude asks one focused
question at a time to extract a precise, implementation-ready specification before writing
the prompt body. This mode is gated into CLAUDE.md as a behavioral instruction so it
applies automatically in every session.

Sprint creation is handled separately in CCP-617.

The user has defined a companion snippet:

```
<GUIDED_QUESTIONS_MODE>
Your role is to extract a precise, implementation-ready specification — not to validate
assumptions or accept vague answers. Ask one question at a time. Do not proceed until the
current answer is specific enough to write code from. If an answer is ambiguous, restate
what you understood and ask for clarification before continuing. Probe for: exact placement
and behavior, Lichess parity vs conscious deviation, affected files, edge cases, and
acceptance criteria. Do not infer — make the user decide. Ask open questions; do not
suggest answers that lead the user toward a particular choice. When all decisions are
captured, summarize them back before creating the prompt.
</GUIDED_QUESTIONS_MODE>
```

## Step 1 — Research (do this before any implementation)

Before writing any code or docs, research:

1. Search for existing Claude Code skills or community patterns for structured
   requirement elicitation (e.g. "spec mode", "guided questions", "requirement
   extraction" patterns in AI agent workflows)
2. Look for prior art on how AI agents ask clarifying questions one-at-a-time
   vs. upfront batching — what works better in practice?
3. Note any naming conventions or behavioral patterns worth adopting

Summarize findings in 3–5 bullet points before proceeding to implementation.
If a well-established pattern exists, adopt it rather than inventing from scratch.

## Step 2 — Implementation

### Files to update (maximum 3)

1. `CLAUDE.md` — add behavioral gate + GUIDED_QUESTIONS_MODE snippet
2. `docs/prompts/PROMPT_CREATION_PROCESS.md` — describe both modes
3. `docs/prompts/PROMPT_REGISTRY_README.md` — update trigger map

---

### CLAUDE.md changes

Add a new section **`## AI Guided Questions Mode`** with:

**The gate rule (mandatory behavioral instruction):**
> Before creating any prompt — whether triggered by user request, manager
> orchestration, or agent workflow — Claude must ask:
>
> *"AI Guided Questions or Quick Create?"*
>
> - **Quick Create**: proceed with current behavior (infer and write)
> - **AI Guided Questions**: enter guided mode (see below)
>
> This gate applies every time a prompt is being created. No exceptions.
> Sprint creation is gated separately (see CCP-617).

**Guided mode behavior for prompts:**
- Activate `<GUIDED_QUESTIONS_MODE>` (paste the full snippet above)
- Ask questions one at a time — do not batch
- Questions must probe for:
  - Exact UI placement and behavior
  - Lichess parity vs known deviation (and why)
  - Which files will be touched
  - Edge cases and error states
  - Acceptance criteria (how will we know it's done?)
  - Mobile behavior if UI is involved
- If an answer is too vague to write code from, restate what was heard and ask again
- When all decisions are captured, summarize back to the user:
  *"Here's what I'll put in the prompt — does this match your intent?"*
- Only after confirmation: proceed to reserve + write + finalize the prompt

**The GUIDED_QUESTIONS_MODE snippet** (embed in full):
```
<GUIDED_QUESTIONS_MODE>
Your role is to extract a precise, implementation-ready specification — not to validate
assumptions or accept vague answers. Ask one question at a time. Do not proceed until the
current answer is specific enough to write code from. If an answer is ambiguous, restate
what you understood and ask for clarification before continuing. Probe for: exact placement
and behavior, Lichess parity vs conscious deviation, affected files, edge cases, and
acceptance criteria. Do not infer — make the user decide. Ask open questions; do not
suggest answers that lead the user toward a particular choice. When all decisions are
captured, summarize them back before creating the prompt.
</GUIDED_QUESTIONS_MODE>
```

---

### PROMPT_CREATION_PROCESS.md changes

Add a section **`## Creation Modes`** near the top, before the standard flow:

```markdown
## Creation Modes

Before creating any prompt, Claude must ask:

> "AI Guided Questions or Quick Create?"

**Quick Create** — Claude infers intent and writes the prompt body directly.
Use for small, clear, well-understood tasks.

**AI Guided Questions** — Claude asks one targeted question at a time to build
a precise spec. Use for anything involving UI decisions, behavior choices,
Lichess parity questions, or uncertain scope. Answers are baked directly into
the prompt body. Claude summarizes decisions back before finalizing.
```

---

### PROMPT_REGISTRY_README.md changes

Update the **User Language / Trigger Map** section to add:

```
AI Guided Questions triggers:
- "guided"
- "ask me questions"
- "AI guided"
- "guide me through it"
- "let's spec this out"
- "walk me through it"

Quick Create triggers (skip the gate):
- "quick create"
- "just create it"
- "no questions"
- "untracked" (gate does not apply to untracked work)
```

## What NOT to change

- Do not change the prompt registry tooling or scripts
- Do not change sprint creation docs (covered in CCP-617)
- Do not change AGENTS.md unless the research step surfaces a strong reason
- Do not add new npm scripts

## Validation

After implementation:
- [ ] CLAUDE.md contains the gate rule and GUIDED_QUESTIONS_MODE snippet
- [ ] PROMPT_CREATION_PROCESS.md describes both modes clearly
- [ ] PROMPT_REGISTRY_README.md has updated trigger map
- [ ] Manually verify: ask Claude to create a prompt — it should ask
  "AI Guided Questions or Quick Create?" before proceeding

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-616
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-616 --checklist "- [ ] CLAUDE.md contains the gate rule and GUIDED_QUESTIONS_MODE snippet|- [ ] PROMPT_CREATION_PROCESS.md describes both modes clearly|- [ ] PROMPT_REGISTRY_README.md has updated trigger map|- [ ] Manually verify: ask Claude to create a prompt — it should ask"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-616 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] CLAUDE.md contains the gate rule and GUIDED_QUESTIONS_MODE snippet|- [ ] PROMPT_CREATION_PROCESS.md describes both modes clearly|- [ ] PROMPT_REGISTRY_README.md has updated trigger map|- [ ] Manually verify: ask Claude to create a prompt — it should ask"
```

# CCP-617 — AI Guided Questions mode for sprint creation

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

CCP-616 adds AI Guided Questions gating to prompt creation. This prompt does the same
for sprint creation — but sprints are larger in scope and require a different, expanded
question set covering phases, sequencing, dependencies, and milestone validation.

Sprint creation currently has no structured elicitation step. A sprint is created from
a high-level idea and Claude infers the phases and tasks. This leads to sprints that
miss ordering constraints, skip validation gates between phases, and don't reflect the
user's actual priorities.

This prompt must run AFTER CCP-616 is complete and merged, since it builds on the
CLAUDE.md gate rule already established there.

## Step 1 — Research (do this before any implementation)

Before writing any docs, research:

1. How do sprint planning tools (Linear, Jira, etc.) structure their sprint creation
   interview — what questions do they ask upfront?
2. Are there AI agent patterns specifically for sprint/milestone decomposition?
3. What question sequences work best for scoping work that spans multiple sessions?

Summarize findings in 3–5 bullet points before proceeding.

## Step 2 — Implementation

### Files to update (maximum 3)

1. `CLAUDE.md` — extend the AI Guided Questions gate to cover sprint creation
2. `docs/mini-sprints/SPRINT_CREATION_PROCESS.md` — create this file if it doesn't
   exist, or update if it does; describe both sprint creation modes
3. `docs/prompts/PROMPT_REGISTRY_README.md` — update trigger map with sprint triggers

---

### CLAUDE.md changes

In the existing **`## AI Guided Questions Mode`** section added by CCP-616, add a
subsection for sprint creation:

**The gate rule for sprints:**
> Before creating any sprint — whether triggered by user request or agent workflow —
> Claude must ask:
>
> *"AI Guided Questions or Quick Create?"*
>
> - **Quick Create**: infer phases and tasks from the request description
> - **AI Guided Questions**: enter sprint guided mode (see below)

**Guided mode behavior for sprints** (expanded vs prompt mode):
- Ask questions one at a time — do not batch
- Sprint-specific questions must cover:
  - **Goal**: What is the single measurable outcome of this sprint?
  - **Scope boundary**: What is explicitly out of scope?
  - **Phases**: Should this be broken into phases? If so, what are they?
  - **Sequencing**: Does any phase depend on another being validated first?
  - **Validation gates**: How do we know each phase is done before moving on?
  - **Prompt breakdown**: Roughly how many prompts will this sprint generate?
  - **Lichess reference**: Is there a Lichess feature this mirrors? Which source files?
  - **Known deviations**: Any intentional departures from Lichess behavior?
  - **Risk**: What is the most likely thing to go wrong in this sprint?
- If an answer is too vague to sequence work from, ask again before continuing
- When all decisions are captured, summarize the proposed sprint structure back:
  *"Here's the sprint structure I'll create — phases, ordering, and key decisions.
  Does this match your intent?"*
- Only after confirmation: proceed to create the sprint document

---

### SPRINT_CREATION_PROCESS.md

Create or update `docs/mini-sprints/SPRINT_CREATION_PROCESS.md` to document:

```markdown
# Sprint Creation Process

## Creation Modes

Before creating any sprint, Claude must ask:

> "AI Guided Questions or Quick Create?"

**Quick Create** — Claude infers phases and tasks from the request description.
Use only for very small, well-understood sprints.

**AI Guided Questions** — Recommended default for all sprints. Claude asks one
question at a time to establish goal, scope, phases, sequencing, validation gates,
and Lichess reference before writing the sprint document.

## AI Guided Questions — Sprint Question Set

1. What is the single measurable outcome of this sprint?
2. What is explicitly out of scope?
3. Should this be broken into phases? If so, what are they?
4. Does any phase depend on another being validated first?
5. How do we know each phase is done before moving on?
6. Roughly how many prompts will this sprint generate?
7. Is there a Lichess feature this mirrors? Which source files?
8. Any intentional departures from Lichess behavior?
9. What is the most likely thing to go wrong?

Additional questions are asked as needed based on answers.

## Standard Creation Flow

1. Run AI Guided Questions or Quick Create gate
2. Write sprint document in `docs/mini-sprints/`
3. Run `npm run prompts:refresh` to regenerate tracking docs
```

---

### PROMPT_REGISTRY_README.md changes

In the trigger map, add sprint-specific triggers under AI Guided Questions:

```
Sprint AI Guided Questions triggers:
- "create a sprint"
- "new sprint"
- "plan a sprint"
- "spec out a sprint"
- "design a sprint"
```

## What NOT to change

- Do not change prompt creation docs (covered in CCP-616)
- Do not change the prompt registry tooling or scripts
- Do not add new npm scripts

## Validation

After implementation:
- [ ] CLAUDE.md sprint gate rule is present alongside the prompt gate rule
- [ ] SPRINT_CREATION_PROCESS.md exists and documents both modes + question set
- [ ] PROMPT_REGISTRY_README.md has sprint triggers in the trigger map
- [ ] Manually verify: ask Claude to create a sprint — it should ask
  "AI Guided Questions or Quick Create?" before proceeding

```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-617
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:

```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-617 --checklist "- [ ] CLAUDE.md sprint gate rule is present alongside the prompt gate rule|- [ ] SPRINT_CREATION_PROCESS.md exists and documents both modes + question set|- [ ] PROMPT_REGISTRY_README.md has sprint triggers in the trigger map|- [ ] Manually verify: ask Claude to create a sprint — it should ask"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:

```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-617 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] CLAUDE.md sprint gate rule is present alongside the prompt gate rule|- [ ] SPRINT_CREATION_PROCESS.md exists and documents both modes + question set|- [ ] PROMPT_REGISTRY_README.md has sprint triggers in the trigger map|- [ ] Manually verify: ask Claude to create a sprint — it should ask"
```

# CCP-618 — Dashboard: pin status and copy button columns

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

The prompt dashboard table has 8 columns: ID, Title, Priority, Category, Date, Source,
Status, and a copy/actions button. At narrow window widths the Status and actions columns
scroll out of view, making it impossible to see a prompt's status or copy it without
scrolling right.

## Target file

`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

## Goal

Status and the copy/actions column must always be visible at any window width, using
`position: sticky` on those two columns.

## Changes required

### 1. Wrap the table in a scrollable container

Add a wrapper `div` around the `<table>` with `overflow-x: auto`:

```html
<div style="overflow-x: auto;">
  <table>...</table>
</div>
```

### 2. Add sticky CSS for the Status and actions columns

Add to the embedded `<style>` block:

```css
/* Sticky last two columns */
thead th:nth-last-child(1),
thead th:nth-last-child(2),
td:nth-last-child(1),
td:nth-last-child(2) {
  position: sticky;
  background: #1a1a1a;
}
thead th:nth-last-child(1),
td:nth-last-child(1) {
  right: 0;
}
thead th:nth-last-child(2),
td:nth-last-child(2) {
  right: calc(/* actions col width */ 60px);
}
```

Adjust the `right` offset for the second-to-last column to match the actual rendered
width of the actions column (approximately 60px based on the `.row-copy` button width).

Add a subtle left border to the Status column to visually separate it from the
scrolling content:

```css
thead th:nth-last-child(2),
td:nth-last-child(2) {
  border-left: 1px solid #2a2a2a;
}
```

Ensure sticky cells on hovered rows match the hover background:

```css
tbody tr:hover td:nth-last-child(1),
tbody tr:hover td:nth-last-child(2) {
  background: #252525;
}
```

For manager rows:

```css
tr.row--manager td:nth-last-child(1),
tr.row--manager td:nth-last-child(2) {
  background: #1e2228;
}
tr.row--manager:hover td:nth-last-child(1),
tr.row--manager:hover td:nth-last-child(2) {
  background: #252a32;
}
```

### What NOT to change

- Do not change column order or content
- Do not change any other table behavior or filtering logic
- Do not touch the sprint table or urgent block

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Open the dashboard, narrow the browser window, and verify:
- The Status badge and the copy button remain visible on the right as the table scrolls
- The Status column has a subtle left border separator
- Hovered row background is consistent across sticky and non-sticky cells
- Manager row background is consistent across sticky and non-sticky cells

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-618
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-618 --checklist "- [ ] Narrow the browser window and confirm Status badge stays pinned to the right edge|- [ ] Narrow the browser window and confirm the copy button stays pinned to the right edge|- [ ] Hover a row and confirm background is consistent across sticky and non-sticky cells|- [ ] Confirm manager rows have correct background on both sticky and non-sticky cells"
```

# CCP-619 — Dashboard: make refresh button always visible

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

The dashboard header contains the "Refresh Data" button. As the user scrolls down through
a long prompt list, the header scrolls out of view, taking the Refresh button with it.
The button should always be accessible regardless of scroll position.

The header already has `z-index: 10` set, indicating sticky behaviour was always the intent
but was never applied.

## Target file

`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

## Changes required

Add `position: sticky` and `top: 0` to the `.header` rule so the entire header — including
the Refresh Data button — stays pinned to the top of the viewport as the page scrolls.

Find the existing `.header` CSS rule:

```css
.header {
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
```

Add the two sticky properties:

```css
.header {
  position: sticky;
  top: 0;
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
```

No other changes needed. The existing `z-index: 10` already ensures the header sits above
scrolling content.

### What NOT to change

- Do not change header layout, flex behaviour, or responsive styles
- Do not change the Refresh button itself
- Do not touch the table, filters, or any other part of the dashboard

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Open the dashboard and verify:
- Scroll down past the first screenful of prompts
- The header (brand, stats, Refresh Data button) remains pinned at the top
- The Refresh Data button is clickable at all scroll positions
- No visual overlap or z-index issues with table rows scrolling under the header

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-619
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-619 --checklist "- [ ] Scroll down past the first screenful and confirm the header stays pinned at the top|- [ ] Confirm the Refresh Data button is clickable at all scroll positions|- [ ] Confirm table rows scroll under the header without visual overlap issues"
```

# CCP-619-F1 — Fix: Refresh button hidden at narrow window widths

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

CCP-619 was diagnosed incorrectly. The Refresh Data button disappears at narrow window
widths — this is a responsive layout problem, not a scroll problem.

At ≤700px the header uses `flex-wrap: wrap`. The HTML order is:
  brand → title → stats → generated → refresh

When items wrap, the stats block expands to fill the row and the refresh button gets pushed
to a lower wrapped row where it may be partially or fully hidden.

The fix is to use CSS `order` to keep the refresh button on the first row at narrow widths,
alongside the brand and title, pushed to the right with `margin-left: auto`.

## Target file

`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

## Changes required

In the existing `@media (max-width: 700px)` block, add three rules:

```css
@media (max-width: 700px) {
  .header { flex-wrap: wrap; height: auto; padding: 8px 12px; gap: 6px; }
  .header__stats { margin-left: 0; flex-wrap: wrap; order: 1; width: 100%; }
  .header__generated { order: 2; }
  .header__refresh { order: 0; margin-left: auto; }
  .container { padding: 10px; }
  .detail-meta { grid-template-columns: 1fr; }
  .detail-meta dt { margin-top: 6px; }
  td.title { max-width: none; }
}
```

The three new lines are:
- `.header__stats` gets `order: 1; width: 100%` — wraps to its own full-width row below the first row
- `.header__generated` gets `order: 2` — sits below stats
- `.header__refresh` gets `order: 0; margin-left: auto` — stays on the first row, pushed to the right after title

Result at narrow widths:
- Row 1: brand | title | [spacer] Refresh Data button
- Row 2: stats (full width)
- Row 3: generated timestamp

### What NOT to change

- Do not change the desktop header layout
- Do not change the header HTML
- Do not touch any other styles outside the existing `@media (max-width: 700px)` block

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Open the dashboard and verify:
- At full width: header looks unchanged
- Narrow the browser to ~400px: Refresh Data button remains visible on the first row, right-aligned
- Narrow the browser to ~400px: stats wrap to the row below
- Refresh button is clickable at all widths

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-619-F1
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-619-F1 --fixes CCP-619 --checklist "- [ ] At full width the header looks unchanged|- [ ] Narrow to ~400px and confirm the Refresh Data button is visible on the first row right-aligned|- [ ] Narrow to ~400px and confirm stats wrap below the first row|- [ ] Refresh button is clickable at narrow widths"
```

# CCP-619-F2 — Fix: hide header stats at narrow widths to keep header single-line

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

CCP-619-F1 proposed reordering header items with CSS `order` to fix the Refresh button
disappearing at narrow widths. The user has chosen a simpler approach: hide the stats
block and the generated timestamp when the window is too narrow, so the header stays
on a single line and the Refresh button is always visible.

CCP-619-F1 was never executed and is superseded by this prompt.

## Target file

`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

## Changes required

Replace the existing `@media (max-width: 700px)` block. Remove the `flex-wrap: wrap`
and `height: auto` rules from `.header`, and instead hide `.header__stats` and
`.header__generated` so the header stays one line with only brand, title, and the
Refresh button visible.

Find:

```css
@media (max-width: 700px) {
  .header { flex-wrap: wrap; height: auto; padding: 8px 12px; gap: 6px; }
  .header__stats { margin-left: 0; flex-wrap: wrap; }
  .container { padding: 10px; }
  .detail-meta { grid-template-columns: 1fr; }
  .detail-meta dt { margin-top: 6px; }
  td.title { max-width: none; }
}
```

Replace with:

```css
@media (max-width: 700px) {
  .header__stats { display: none; }
  .header__generated { display: none; }
  .container { padding: 10px; }
  .detail-meta { grid-template-columns: 1fr; }
  .detail-meta dt { margin-top: 6px; }
  td.title { max-width: none; }
}
```

### What NOT to change

- Do not change the header HTML
- Do not change any desktop styles
- Do not touch any other part of the media query block beyond what is shown above

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Open the dashboard and verify:
- At full width: header shows brand, title, stats, generated timestamp, and Refresh button
- Narrow to ~400px: stats and timestamp are hidden, header stays on one line
- Narrow to ~400px: Refresh button is visible and clickable
- No header wrapping or two-line header at any width

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-619-F2
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-619-F2 --checklist "- [ ] At full width header shows all elements including stats and timestamp|- [ ] Narrow to ~400px and confirm stats and timestamp are hidden|- [ ] Narrow to ~400px and confirm header stays on a single line|- [ ] Narrow to ~400px and confirm Refresh button is visible and clickable"
```

If errors occurred:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-619-F2 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] At full width header shows all elements including stats and timestamp|- [ ] Narrow to ~400px and confirm stats and timestamp are hidden|- [ ] Narrow to ~400px and confirm header stays on a single line|- [ ] Narrow to ~400px and confirm Refresh button is visible and clickable"
```

# CCP-621 — Dashboard: remove stats block from header

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

The dashboard header shows a row of stat counters (total, sprints, active/problem sprints,
reviewed, started, run, ready, reserved) and a generated timestamp. The user no longer
wants this information in the header.

## Target file

`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

## Changes required

### 1. Remove the CSS

Remove these two rule blocks:

```css
.header__stats {
  margin-left: auto;
  font-size: 0.8rem;
  color: var(--text-dim);
  display: flex;
  gap: 12px;
  align-items: center;
}
.header__stat { white-space: nowrap; }
.header__stat b { color: var(--text); }
```

And remove the `.header__generated` rule block:

```css
.header__generated {
  font-size: 0.72rem;
  color: #555;
  white-space: nowrap;
}
```

Also remove the two lines from the `@media (max-width: 700px)` block that are now redundant:

```css
  .header__stats { display: none; }
  .header__generated { display: none; }
```

### 2. Remove the HTML elements

Remove the stats div and the generated timestamp span from the header:

```html
  <div class="header__stats" id="stats"></div>
  <span class="header__generated">Updated ${...}</span>
```

### 3. Remove the JS that populates the stats

Remove the `document.getElementById('stats').innerHTML = ...` block (the multi-line
assignment that writes the `header__stat` spans).

Also remove the reference to `.header__generated` in the refresh handler:

```js
    const genEl = document.querySelector('.header__generated');
    if (genEl) genEl.textContent = 'Live data';
```

### 4. Add margin-left: auto to the refresh button

With the stats block gone, the Refresh button needs `margin-left: auto` to stay
right-aligned in the header. Add to the existing `.header__refresh` rule:

```css
.header__refresh {
  margin-left: auto;
  ...
}
```

### What NOT to change

- Do not remove the stats variables themselves if they are used elsewhere in rendering
- Do not change any other header elements (brand, title, refresh button)
- Do not touch the sprint or prompt list rendering

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Open the dashboard and verify:
- Header shows only: brand, title, Refresh button
- Refresh button is right-aligned
- No stat counters or timestamp visible at any window width
- Refresh button still works

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-621
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-621 --checklist "- [ ] Header shows only brand, title, and Refresh button|- [ ] Refresh button is right-aligned|- [ ] No stat counters or timestamp visible at any window width|- [ ] Refresh button still works"
```

If errors occurred:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-621 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] Header shows only brand, title, and Refresh button|- [ ] Refresh button is right-aligned|- [ ] No stat counters or timestamp visible at any window width|- [ ] Refresh button still works"
```

# CCP-622 — Dashboard: Refresh button amber stale timer + visual distinction from bulk-copy buttons

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
## Context

The Refresh Data button in the dashboard header is pressed frequently. After 2 minutes
of inactivity (no refresh), the button should turn amber to remind the user that the
data may be stale.

The timer resets every time the button is clicked. If the button is clicked within
2 minutes, it stays its normal colour. After 2 minutes without a click, it turns amber.

## Target file

`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

## Changes required

### 1. Add amber CSS state

Add a new class to the existing `.header__refresh` rules:

```css
.header__refresh.is-stale {
  border-color: #b8860b;
  color: #f0a500;
}
```

### 2. Add the stale timer JS

After the existing refresh button click handler, add a timer that sets the button stale
after 2 minutes of no refresh:

```js
// --- Stale indicator ---
let staleTimer = null;
function resetStaleTimer() {
  const btn = document.getElementById('refresh-btn');
  btn.classList.remove('is-stale');
  clearTimeout(staleTimer);
  staleTimer = setTimeout(() => {
    btn.classList.add('is-stale');
  }, 2 * 60 * 1000);
}
resetStaleTimer();
document.getElementById('refresh-btn').addEventListener('click', resetStaleTimer);
```

Note: the existing click handler on `refresh-btn` already exists — add a second
`addEventListener` call for `resetStaleTimer`, do not replace the existing handler.

### 3. Make the Refresh button larger and visually distinct from the bulk-copy buttons

The `.header__refresh` and `.bulk-copy-btn` currently share nearly identical styling
(same size, same muted colour scheme). The Refresh button is the primary action in the
header and should look distinct and more prominent.

Update the existing `.header__refresh` rule:

```css
.header__refresh {
  margin-left: auto;
  padding: 6px 16px;
  font-size: 0.85rem;
  font-weight: 600;
  background: var(--bg-card);
  color: var(--text);
  border: 1px solid #444;
  border-radius: 4px;
  cursor: pointer;
  transition: background 0.15s, color 0.15s, border-color 0.15s;
  white-space: nowrap;
}
.header__refresh:hover { background: var(--accent); color: #111; border-color: var(--accent); }
```

The `.bulk-copy-btn` buttons stay visually smaller and muted — do not change them.

### What NOT to change

- Do not change the existing refresh button click handler
- Do not change `.bulk-copy-btn` styles
- Do not change any other header layout

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Open the dashboard and verify:
- Refresh button is visibly larger and bolder than the Copy Needs Review IDs / Copy Issues Found buttons
- Button starts in normal colour on page load
- After 2 minutes without clicking, button turns amber
- Clicking the button resets the timer and returns to normal colour
- Hover state still works correctly

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-622
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-622 --checklist "- [ ] Refresh button is visibly larger and bolder than the bulk-copy buttons|- [ ] Button starts in normal colour on page load|- [ ] After 2 minutes without clicking button turns amber|- [ ] Clicking the button resets the timer and returns to normal colour|- [ ] Hover state still works correctly"
```

If errors occurred:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-622 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] Refresh button is visibly larger and bolder than the bulk-copy buttons|- [ ] Button starts in normal colour on page load|- [ ] After 2 minutes without clicking button turns amber|- [ ] Clicking the button resets the timer and returns to normal colour|- [ ] Hover state still works correctly"
```

You are working in `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer`.

This is a tracked cleanup campaign manager. It exists to run the four backlog re-triage waves in order and keep the registry/history truthful. It is not a feature implementation prompt.

Batch prompt IDs to execute in order:
- `CCP-626`
- `CCP-624`
- `CCP-625`
- `CCP-627`

Manager-prompt rule:
- `CCP-623` is the manager prompt id only
- do not recurse into `CCP-623` as if it were one of the child prompts

Campaign goal:
- re-triage the 109 prompts currently marked `issues found`
- classify each prompt as one of:
  - `fix now`
  - `resolved already`
  - `superseded`
  - `manager-only fallout`
- create follow-up fix prompts only for `fix now`
- re-close prompts through the prompt-review workflow with explicit provenance

Required campaign rules:
- preserve historical truth; do not pretend old prompts passed originally if they did not
- do not create follow-up prompts for superseded work or manager-only fallout
- do not re-close any manager prompt until all relevant child prompts have current dispositions
- use the current prompt workflow commands, never hand-edit generated docs
- run `npm run prompts:refresh` after each coherent wave or subtree

For each child wave manager:
1. read the child prompt exactly as written from `docs/prompts/items/`
2. run its lifecycle commands
3. complete the wave before moving to the next one unless the wave explicitly blocks on a real uncertainty

Before executing any child prompts, mark this manager as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-623
```

After all child prompts are complete, mark the manager as done:
```sh

```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:


Required method for each prompt:
1. inspect the original prompt item and current registry state
2. inspect relevant child prompts and their current review outcomes
3. determine whether the manager is:
   - still blocked by child prompts
   - resolved by later child work
   - independently still broken
   - superseded
4. if children are stale or unresolved, re-triage the children first before re-closing the manager
5. if the manager truly still needs code work, only then create a follow-up fix prompt

Do not:

- create manager follow-up prompts just because the original manager was red historically
- hand-edit queue/log/history files

Validation expectations:
- every manager in this wave must end with a current disposition
- no manager should remain ambiguous about whether its red state is child fallout or a real current problem
- any child follow-up prompts created during this wave must be rooted in an actual current issue

Execution Target: Claude Code

- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Lifecycle

```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-628
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-628 --checklist "- [ ] Open the Mistake Detection modal and switch Minimum Severity between Inaccuracy, Mistake, and Blunder; verify the intended UI feedback is clear|- [ ] Confirm Missed Mate in N still changes value correctly and no unrelated retrospection settings regress"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-628 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Open the Mistake Detection modal and switch Minimum Severity between Inaccuracy, Mistake, and Blunder; verify the intended UI feedback is clear|- [ ] Confirm Missed Mate in N still changes value correctly and no unrelated retrospection settings regress"
```

Startup coordination step:
- Before editing, check whether any other tool, agent, Claude Code session, or Codex thread is actively touching the same retrospection settings, header modal, or prompt-tracking files.
- If overlapping work exists, stop and report it before editing.

Task:
Investigate the `Mistake Detection` modal behavior around `Minimum Severity` and determine whether the current UI is behaving as intended, misleading, or partially implemented.

Problem report:
- In the `Mistake Detection` modal, the user sees `Minimum Severity` with pill buttons for `Inaccuracy`, `Mistake`, and `Blunder`.
- Clicking those options does not reveal any additional sliders or parameter groups.
- The only nearby slider is `Missed Mate in N`.
- The user cannot tell whether the severity buttons are working correctly or if the UI is missing controls.

Inspect first:
- `src/header/index.ts`
- `src/analyse/retroConfig.ts`
- relevant modal styling in `src/styles/main.scss`
- any docs or audits that mention Learn From Your Mistakes candidate selection or mistake-detection settings
- relevant Lichess source only if it actually informs this behavior

Required workflow:
1. Inspect the actual Patzer Pro code first.
2. Identify exactly what changes in state when `Inaccuracy`, `Mistake`, or `Blunder` is clicked.
3. Determine whether the current behavior is:
   - intentional but poorly communicated
   - partially implemented
   - a real bug or regression
4. Compare the actual code behavior against what the UI wording suggests to a user.
5. Choose the smallest safe fix.
6. Implement only if the correct fix is clear and safely scoped.

Constraints:
- do not assume sliders are missing unless the code or design intent actually supports that conclusion
- do not bundle unrelated retrospection or settings work
- do not move substantial new logic into `src/main.ts`
- prefer a clarity fix over a speculative behavior change if the underlying setting is already working as intended

Before coding, provide:
- prompt id
- task id
- source document
- source step
- relevant Patzer Pro files
- relevant Lichess files, if any
- exact diagnosis
- exact small step to implement
- why that step is safely scoped

Questions to answer explicitly:
- What exactly changes in code when the user clicks `Inaccuracy`, `Mistake`, or `Blunder`?
- Is the current UI behavior intentional?
- If intentional, why does it read as if something is missing?
- What is the smallest fix that makes the behavior obvious to users?

Validation after coding:
- run `npm run build` if any code changes are made
- report:
  - build result
  - whether behavior changed intentionally
  - whether runtime or console issues were observed
  - any remaining ambiguity or risk

Manual test checklist with concrete user actions and expected results:
- Open `Mistake Detection`, switch between `Inaccuracy`, `Mistake`, and `Blunder`, and verify the UI clearly communicates what changed.
- Adjust `Missed Mate in N` and verify its value still updates independently from the severity setting.

# CCP-615-F1 — Show selected dashboard filter count in filter label

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

Prompt ID: CCP-615-F1
Task ID: CCP-615
Parent Prompt ID: CCP-615
Short task title: Show selected dashboard filter count in filter label
Source document: User request
Source step: prompt dashboard filter count bug fix

## Context

The prompt dashboard already supports filter buttons for views like `All`, `Managers`,
`Ready`, `Started`, `Errors`, `Completed`, and `Passed Review`.

Current problem:
- when the user clicks one of these filters, the selected filter label does not show how many
  prompts currently match that filter
- the user wants the pressed/active filter label to include the matching count so the filter
  state is more informative at a glance

Expected behavior:
- when a filter is selected, its visible label should include the number of prompts that match
  that filter under the current dataset
- this should apply to the active filter button itself
- the count should update correctly when the selected filter changes
- the feature should not redesign the toolbar or add unrelated dashboard controls

## Target files

- `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- generated output: `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

Do not hand-edit `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`.

## Required workflow

1. Inspect the current implementation in `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`.
2. Confirm how filter buttons are generated and how active-filter state is tracked.
3. Identify the smallest safe fix so only the active filter button shows the matching count.
4. Keep the count logic aligned with the existing filter behavior rather than introducing a
   second inconsistent filtering path.
5. Regenerate the dashboard after the change.

## Implementation guidance

- reuse the existing filter logic if possible when computing the active-filter count
- avoid duplicating the filter-matching rules in multiple places unless absolutely necessary
- if the dashboard already computes a filtered list for the active state, prefer deriving the
  count from that existing path
- preserve the existing unselected filter labels unless there is a clear reason not to
- keep this scoped to the prompt dashboard filter labels only

## Validation

Run:

```sh
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Open the dashboard and verify:
- clicking `All` shows the full list and the active `All` filter displays the full count
- clicking `Managers` shows only manager prompts and the active `Managers` label shows that count
- clicking `Ready`, `Started`, `Errors`, `Completed`, and `Passed Review` updates the active
  filter label to the correct matching count
- switching between filters updates the count correctly each time
- no duplicate count text appears on inactive filter buttons
- no console errors appear in the dashboard

In your final report, echo:

# CCP-629 — Mistake Detection: replace severity pills with gradient slider

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

The Mistake Detection modal currently uses three pill buttons (Inaccuracy, Mistake, Blunder)
to select the minimum severity threshold for Learn From Your Mistakes candidates.

Replace those pills with a 3-step slider that uses a green → amber → red gradient track,
tick labels below the track, and a marker indicating the Lichess default position.

## Target files

- `src/header/index.ts` — replace pill button group with slider vnode
- `src/styles/main.scss` — add gradient track, tick label, and default marker styles

## Changes required

### src/header/index.ts

Remove the `h('div.detection-modal__pills', ...)` block (the three pill buttons).

Replace it with a slider group:

```
h('div.detection-modal__severity-slider', [
  h('input.severity-range', {
    attrs: { type: 'range', min: 0, max: 2, step: 1,
             value: ['inaccuracy','mistake','blunder'].indexOf(cfg.minClassification) },
    on: {
      input: (e: Event) => {
        const idx = parseInt((e.target as HTMLInputElement).value, 10);
        const val = (['inaccuracy','mistake','blunder'] as const)[idx];
        setRetroConfig({ minClassification: val });
        redraw();
      },
    },
  }),
  // Lichess default marker at position 1 (Mistake = 50% along the 0–2 range)
  h('span.severity-range__default-mark', {
    attrs: { title: 'Lichess default: Mistake' },
  }),
  h('div.detection-modal__severity-ticks', [
    h('span', 'Inaccuracy'),
    h('span', 'Mistake'),
    h('span', 'Blunder'),
  ]),
])
```

The existing `detection-modal__value` span (showing `loss ≥ N%`) and the description
paragraph must remain unchanged — only the pill group is replaced.

### src/styles/main.scss

Add styles for the new slider and its supporting elements.

The slider track must use a CSS gradient from green (`#4caf50`) through amber (`#f0a500`)
to red (`#e53935`) to communicate increasing strictness.

The default marker is absolutely positioned at 50% (Mistake position in a 3-step slider)
and should be visually distinct — a small downward triangle or vertical tick above the track.

Tick labels sit below the track in a flex row with `justify-content: space-between`.

The `.detection-modal__severity-slider` wrapper must be `position: relative` to anchor
the default marker.

Cross-browser note: styling `input[type=range]` requires both `::-webkit-slider-runnable-track`
and `::-moz-range-track` rules. The thumb requires both `::-webkit-slider-thumb` and
`::-moz-range-thumb`.

### What NOT to change

- Do not change the `missedMateDistance` slider
- Do not change the Blown Wins, Defensive, or Punish families
- Do not change `detection-modal__value` span logic (already shows `loss ≥ N%`)
- Do not change the description paragraph text

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run build
```

Open the Mistake Detection modal and verify:
- The gradient slider renders with green → amber → red track
- Moving the thumb to Inaccuracy / Mistake / Blunder updates the `loss ≥ N%` value label
- A marker is visible at the Mistake (middle) position labelled as the Lichess default
- Tick labels Inaccuracy, Mistake, Blunder are visible below the track
- Setting persists on page reload (localStorage)
- No visual regression on the Missed Mate in N slider below it

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-629
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-629 --checklist "- [ ] Gradient slider renders green → amber → red|- [ ] Thumb movement updates loss ≥ N% label correctly|- [ ] Lichess default marker visible at Mistake position|- [ ] Tick labels Inaccuracy / Mistake / Blunder visible below track|- [ ] Setting persists on page reload|- [ ] No regression on Missed Mate in N slider"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-629 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Gradient slider renders green → amber → red|- [ ] Thumb movement updates loss ≥ N% label correctly|- [ ] Lichess default marker visible at Mistake position|- [ ] Tick labels Inaccuracy / Mistake / Blunder visible below track|- [ ] Setting persists on page reload|- [ ] No regression on Missed Mate in N slider"
```

# CCP-630 — Mistake Detection: continuous loss-threshold slider with classification markers

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

CCP-629 replaced the three severity pills with a 3-step discrete slider. This prompt
replaces that with a fully continuous slider so the user can set any loss threshold
from 1% to 25% in 1% steps.

Divider markers on the gradient track show where each classification label begins:
- Inaccuracy: loss ≥ 5% (20% along the 1–25 track)
- Mistake: loss ≥ 10% (40% along the track) — also the Lichess default
- Blunder: loss ≥ 15% (60% along the track)

The data model must change: `minClassification: 'inaccuracy' | 'mistake' | 'blunder'`
is replaced by `minLossThreshold: number` (stored as a fraction, e.g. 0.10 for 10%).
`retro.ts` uses the threshold directly instead of mapping from a classification enum.

## Target files

- `src/analyse/retroConfig.ts` — replace `minClassification` with `minLossThreshold`
- `src/analyse/retro.ts` — use `retroConfig.minLossThreshold` directly
- `src/header/index.ts` — update slider to continuous range, update value label, remove classification label references
- `src/styles/main.scss` — add divider marker styles, remove no-longer-needed 3-step styles if any

Note: 4 files is unavoidable here — the data model change and its consumer must move
together, and the UI and its styles are inseparable.

## Changes required

### src/analyse/retroConfig.ts

Replace `minClassification` with `minLossThreshold: number`.

In the `RetroConfig` interface, remove:
```ts
minClassification: 'inaccuracy' | 'mistake' | 'blunder';
```

Add:
```ts
/**
 * Minimum win-chance loss to include a move as a candidate. Range [0.01, 0.25].
 * Stored as a fraction (0.10 = 10%). Default: 0.10 (Mistake threshold, Patzer default).
 * Lichess parity (inaccuracy floor): 0.05. Blunder threshold: 0.15.
 */
minLossThreshold: number;
```

In `RETRO_CONFIG_DEFAULTS`, replace:
```ts
minClassification: 'mistake',
```
with:
```ts
minLossThreshold: 0.10,
```

In `loadFromStorage()`, replace the `minClassification` validation block with:
```ts
minLossThreshold:
  typeof p.minLossThreshold === 'number' &&
  p.minLossThreshold >= 0.01 && p.minLossThreshold <= 0.25
    ? p.minLossThreshold
    : RETRO_CONFIG_DEFAULTS.minLossThreshold,
```

Remove the `VALID_CLASSIFICATIONS` Set — it is no longer needed.

Remove the `CLASSIFICATION_OPTIONS` export if it was exported from this file (it lives
in `src/header/index.ts` and can stay there as a local const for label display only).

### src/analyse/retro.ts

Remove the `CLASSIFICATION_RANK` const entirely.

In `buildRetroCandidates()`, replace the condition-1 block:
```ts
const minRank        = CLASSIFICATION_RANK[retroConfig.minClassification];
const qualifiesByLoss = (
  classification !== null &&
  CLASSIFICATION_RANK[classification] >= minRank
);
```

with a direct threshold comparison:
```ts
const qualifiesByLoss = loss !== undefined && loss >= retroConfig.minLossThreshold;
```

Remove the now-unused `classification` variable and `classifyLoss` import if
`classifyLoss` is no longer called anywhere in this file. (Check before removing —
it may be used elsewhere in the file for labelling candidates.)

### src/header/index.ts

Remove `CLASSIFICATION_OPTIONS` const (no longer needed for the slider).

In `renderRetroModal()`:

Update the `isDefault` check — replace the `minClassification` line with:
```ts
cfg.minLossThreshold === RETRO_CONFIG_DEFAULTS.minLossThreshold &&
```

Update the `detection-modal__value` span to show the actual percentage:
```ts
h('span.detection-modal__value', `loss ≥ ${Math.round(cfg.minLossThreshold * 100)}%`),
```

Replace the existing `detection-modal__severity-slider` vnode with:

```ts
h('div.detection-modal__severity-slider', [
  h('input.severity-range', {
    attrs: {
      type: 'range', min: 1, max: 25, step: 1,
      value: Math.round(cfg.minLossThreshold * 100),
    },
    on: {
      input: (e: Event) => {
        const pct = parseInt((e.target as HTMLInputElement).value, 10);
        setRetroConfig({ minLossThreshold: pct / 100 });
        redraw();
      },
    },
  }),
  // Divider markers at Inaccuracy (20%), Mistake/Lichess default (40%), Blunder (60%)
  h('span.severity-divider--inaccuracy', {
    attrs: { title: 'Inaccuracy: loss ≥ 5%' },
  }),
  h('span.severity-divider--mistake', {
    attrs: { title: 'Lichess default / Mistake: loss ≥ 10%' },
  }),
  h('span.severity-divider--blunder', {
    attrs: { title: 'Blunder: loss ≥ 15%' },
  }),
  h('div.detection-modal__severity-ticks', [
    h('span', '1%'),
    h('span', 'Inaccuracy'),
    h('span', 'Mistake'),
    h('span', 'Blunder'),
    h('span', '25%'),
  ]),
])
```

Remove the old `severity-range__default-mark` span.

### src/styles/main.scss

Remove the `.severity-range__default-mark` rule (replaced by per-classification dividers).

Add three divider marker rules. Each is a thin vertical tick on the gradient track, positioned
absolutely within `.detection-modal__severity-slider`.

The positions (as `left` percentages within the 1–25 range):
- Inaccuracy (5%) = (5-1)/(25-1) * 100 ≈ 16.7%
- Mistake (10%) = (10-1)/(25-1) * 100 ≈ 37.5%
- Blunder (15%) = (15-1)/(25-1) * 100 ≈ 58.3%

```scss
.severity-divider--inaccuracy,
.severity-divider--mistake,
.severity-divider--blunder {
  position: absolute;
  top: 0;
  width: 2px;
  height: 6px; // matches track height
  background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.5);
  transform: translateX(-50%);
  pointer-events: none;
  border-radius: 1px;
}

.severity-divider--inaccuracy { left: 16.7%; }
.severity-divider--mistake    { left: 37.5%; }
.severity-divider--blunder    { left: 58.3%; }
```

Update `.detection-modal__severity-ticks` to use 5 items with `justify-content: space-between`
(already done) — no change needed if it's already flex with space-between.

Remove the old `.severity-range__default-mark` rule block.

### What NOT to change

- Do not change the `missedMateDistance` slider
- Do not change the Blown Wins, Defensive, or Punish families
- Do not change the description paragraph text
- Do not change any other retroConfig fields

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run build
```

Open the Mistake Detection modal and verify:
- Slider moves continuously from 1% to 25%
- Three divider markers visible on the track at Inaccuracy, Mistake, Blunder positions
- Value label shows `loss ≥ N%` updating live as slider moves
- Setting persists on page reload
- No regression on Missed Mate in N slider or other settings rows

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-630
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-630 --checklist "- [ ] Slider is continuous 1–25% with live value label update|- [ ] Three divider markers visible at Inaccuracy / Mistake / Blunder positions|- [ ] Setting persists on page reload|- [ ] Build passes cleanly|- [ ] No regression on Missed Mate in N or other rows"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-630 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Slider is continuous 1–25% with live value label update|- [ ] Three divider markers visible at Inaccuracy / Mistake / Blunder positions|- [ ] Setting persists on page reload|- [ ] Build passes cleanly|- [ ] No regression on Missed Mate in N or other rows"
```

# CCP-631 — Retro: style retro-reason label in learn from mistakes feedback

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

The Learn From Your Mistakes feedback box renders several elements that have no CSS rules:
- `.retro-reason` — "Chosen because: Blown win" label + summary sentence
- `.retro-reason__label` — the short label line
- `.retro-reason__summary` — the one-sentence explanation
- `.retro-feedback__eval-diff` — the eval diff badge (e.g. "+1.2", "M3")
- `.retro-save` / `.retro-save__btn` / `.retro-save__done` / `.retro-save__confirm` — save to library button and states

All of these render invisible or completely unstyled. This prompt adds appropriate styles
for all of them inside the existing `.retro-box { }` block in `src/styles/main.scss`.

## Target file

`src/styles/main.scss` only.

## Insertion point

Inside `.retro-box { }`, before the closing `}` at line ~1689 (just after `.retro-engine-reveal`).

## Changes required

### .retro-reason

A small callout block that appears after the eval diff in the win/view feedback states.
Should be visually distinct but quiet — not competing with the main "Good move!" heading.
Use a subtle left border accent to visually group it.

```scss
.retro-reason {
  margin-top: 10px;
  padding: 7px 10px;
  border-left: 3px solid #3a5a8a;
  background: rgba(56, 100, 160, 0.1);
  border-radius: 0 4px 4px 0;
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
  gap: 2px;
}

.retro-reason__label {
  font-size: 0.8rem;
  font-weight: 600;
  color: #7aaddf;
}

.retro-reason__summary {
  font-size: 0.78rem;
  color: #888;
  line-height: 1.4;
}
```

### .retro-feedback__eval-diff

A compact badge shown inline in the instruction column between the heading and the reason note.
Should stand out as a numeric value without dominating.

```scss
.retro-feedback__eval-diff {
  display: inline-block;
  margin-top: 4px;
  font-size: 0.85rem;
  font-weight: 600;
  color: #dc322f;
  font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums;
}
```

### .retro-save family

The save button and its state variants. The button should match the quiet `.fbt` style
(already used elsewhere in the retro box for nav buttons) but with a subtle accent.

```scss
.retro-save {
  margin-top: 10px;
}

.retro-save__btn {
  font-size: 0.8rem;
  padding: 4px 12px;
  border-radius: 4px;
  border: 1px solid #3a5a8a;
  background: transparent;
  color: #7aaddf;
  cursor: pointer;
  font-family: inherit;
  transition: background 0.15s, color 0.15s;

  &:hover {
    background: rgba(56, 100, 160, 0.2);
    color: #a8ccee;
  }
}

.retro-save__done {
  font-size: 0.8rem;
  color: #666;
}

.retro-save__confirm {
  font-size: 0.8rem;
  color: #759900;
  font-weight: 600;
}
```

### What NOT to change

- Do not change any retro layout, feedback states, or existing retro rules
- Do not change `.retro-bulk-save` if it already has styles (check first)
- Do not touch any files other than `src/styles/main.scss`

## Validation

Run:
```
npm run build
```

Open a reviewed game, click Mistakes, complete a candidate. Verify:
- The "Chosen because: ..." block is visible with blue-left-border styling
- The label line is readable (light blue, semi-bold)
- The summary line is visible (muted grey)
- The eval diff badge (e.g. "+1.2") is visible in red above the reason block
- The Save to Library button is visible and styled
- "Saved!" confirmation appears in green when clicked
- No visual regression on other retro feedback states (find, fail, offTrack, eval)

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-631
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-631 --checklist "- [ ] retro-reason block visible with blue left border after finding/viewing a move|- [ ] retro-reason__label readable in light blue|- [ ] retro-reason__summary visible in muted grey|- [ ] eval diff badge visible above reason block|- [ ] Save to Library button styled and functional|- [ ] Saved! confirmation appears in green|- [ ] No regression on other feedback states"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-631 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] retro-reason block visible with blue left border after finding/viewing a move|- [ ] retro-reason__label readable in light blue|- [ ] retro-reason__summary visible in muted grey|- [ ] eval diff badge visible above reason block|- [ ] Save to Library button styled and functional|- [ ] Saved! confirmation appears in green|- [ ] No regression on other feedback states"
```

# CCP-632 — Retro engine: track live best move and detect game-move vindication

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

Part 1 of 4 in the retro live eval diff feature (manager: CCP-636).

The retro session currently stores a static `bestMove` from batch analysis on each
`RetroCandidate`. This prompt extends `RetroCtrl` to:

1. Track the live engine's current best move and eval for the active candidate position,
   updating as engine depth increases.
2. Expose the live best move and its eval so downstream code can use whichever is deeper
   (live vs batch).
3. Fire a callback the moment the live engine determines the original game move was
   actually the best move available — the "vindication" event.

## Target files

- `src/analyse/retroCtrl.ts`
- `src/analyse/retro.ts` (add `gameMoveSan` field to `RetroCandidate` if not present)

## Changes required

### src/analyse/retro.ts

Add `gameMoveSan: string` to `RetroCandidate` if it is not already there. This is the
SAN of the move played in the game (the mistake). It is needed by the view to display
the label "Move played in game" without requiring a UCI→SAN conversion at render time.

Check first — `playedMoveSan` already exists. If it covers this use case, no change needed.

### src/analyse/retroCtrl.ts

Add the following to the `RetroCtrl` interface and implementation:

```typescript
// Live engine best move for the active candidate position.
// Updated whenever the engine reports a new best move at higher depth.
// Starts as the batch best move from the candidate.
liveBestMove(): string | undefined;   // UCI
liveBestEval(): { cp?: number; mate?: number; depth: number } | undefined;

// The eval of the move the user just played during solving (UCI).
// Set by setSolvingMoveEval(); cleared when advancing to a new candidate.
solvingMoveEval(): { cp?: number; mate?: number } | undefined;

// Called by main.ts whenever the engine reports a new line for the active position.
// Updates liveBestMove/liveBestEval if the new depth is higher.
// Fires the vindication callback if the engine's best move matches the game move.
onEngineUpdate(best: string, eval_: { cp?: number; mate?: number; depth: number }): void;

// Register a callback fired once when the game move is vindicated.
// Cleared when advancing to the next candidate.
onVindication(cb: () => void): void;
```

Internal state to add to the retro session object:
- `_liveBestMove: string | undefined` — starts as `cand.bestMove`
- `_liveBestDepth: number` — starts as the batch analysis depth (0 if unknown)
- `_liveBestEval: { cp?: number; mate?: number; depth: number } | undefined`
- `_vindicationCb: (() => void) | undefined`
- `_vindicated: boolean` — prevents firing the callback more than once per candidate

`onEngineUpdate()` logic:
1. If `eval_.depth <= _liveBestDepth`, ignore (older or equal depth).
2. Update `_liveBestMove`, `_liveBestDepth`, `_liveBestEval`.
3. If `best === cand.gameMove` (UCI) and `!_vindicated`, set `_vindicated = true`,
   call `_vindicationCb?.()`.

On `jumpToNext()` / `reset()`: clear `_vindicated`, `_vindicationCb`, reset live state
to the new candidate's batch values.

### What NOT to change

- Do not change candidate selection, scoring, or session lifecycle beyond the above.
- Do not change `retroMoveHandler.ts` — that is CCP-633.
- Do not change `retroView.ts` — that is CCP-634.

## Validation

Run `npm run build`. Verify TypeScript compiles cleanly with no errors on the new
interface additions. No runtime test possible at this stage — CCP-633 wires the engine
feed.

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-632
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:

# CCP-633 — Retro wiring: capture solving move and compute dual eval diffs

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

Part 2 of 4 in the retro live eval diff feature (manager: CCP-636).
Depends on CCP-632 being complete.

When the user plays a move during a retro puzzle, the engine's current best move and
eval must be captured at that moment and stored alongside the solving move's eval.
Two diffs are then computed (both mover-perspective, in pawn units):

1. Solving move vs engine best (how far off from perfect)
2. Solving move vs original game move (was the attempt at least better than the game?)

This prompt wires the engine feed into `RetroCtrl.onEngineUpdate()` and captures the
solving move eval snapshot at move-play time.

## Target files

- `src/analyse/retroMoveHandler.ts` — capture solving move eval at move time
- `src/main.ts` — feed engine updates to `retro.onEngineUpdate()`

## Sign convention (critical)

All diffs must be **mover-perspective**: positive = better for the player being reviewed.

For a white candidate:
- `moverCp = +cp` (white-perspective cp is already mover-perspective)

For a black candidate:
- `moverCp = -cp` (flip sign so positive = better for black)

The diff between two moves A and B (from mover's perspective):
- `diff = moverCp(A) - moverCp(B)`
- Positive diff = A is better for the mover than B

## Dual eval diff type

Add to `src/analyse/evalDiff.ts` (or a shared types location):

```typescript
export interface DualEvalDiff {
  /** Solving move vs engine best. Negative = worse than best. */
  vsEngineBest: EvalDiff;
  /** Solving move vs original game move. Positive = better than game move. */
  vsGameMove: EvalDiff;
}
```

## Changes required

### src/main.ts

In the engine update handler (wherever `evalCache` entries are written / engine lines
are processed for the active position), after updating the eval cache, call:

```typescript
if (ctrl.retro) {
  ctrl.retro.onEngineUpdate(bestUci, { cp, mate, depth });
}
```

Only call this when the update is for the active retro candidate's position
(`cand.fenBefore`). Do not feed engine updates for other positions into retro.

### src/analyse/retroMoveHandler.ts

When the user plays a move during solving (the existing move-capture path), after
determining the feedback state, add:

```typescript
// Snapshot the engine's current best and eval at the moment of the move.
// Use whichever depth is higher: live (via retro.liveBestEval) or batch (cand.bestMove).
const liveBest = retro.liveBestMove() ?? cand.bestMove;
const liveEval = retro.liveBestEval();
const batchDepth = 0; // batch has no stored depth
const useLive = liveEval && liveEval.depth > batchDepth;

// Store the solving move's position eval and the snapshotted best for CCP-634 to render.
retro.setSolvingMoveSnapshot({
  solvingMoveUci: playedUci,
  solvingMoveCp:  /* eval of the position after the solving move, from evalCache */,
  engineBestUci:  liveBest,
  engineBestCp:   useLive ? liveEval.cp : /* batch best eval cp */,
  gameMoveUci:    cand.bestMove,  // original game move (the mistake)
  gameMoveCp:     /* eval of the position after the original game move, from evalCache */,
  playerColor:    cand.playerColor,
});
```

Add `setSolvingMoveSnapshot(snapshot: SolvingMoveSnapshot): void` to `RetroCtrl`
(defined in CCP-632's interface additions).

Add `SolvingMoveSnapshot` type:
```typescript
export interface SolvingMoveSnapshot {
  solvingMoveUci: string;
  solvingMoveCp:  number | undefined;
  solvingMoveMate: number | undefined;
  engineBestUci:  string;
  engineBestCp:   number | undefined;
  engineBestMate: number | undefined;
  gameMoveUci:    string;
  gameMoveCp:     number | undefined;
  gameMoveMate:   number | undefined;
  playerColor:    'white' | 'black';
}
```

Expose `getSolvingMoveSnapshot(): SolvingMoveSnapshot | undefined` on `RetroCtrl`.
Clear snapshot on `jumpToNext()` / `reset()`.

### What NOT to change

- Do not change `retroView.ts` — that is CCP-634.
- Do not change candidate selection or session lifecycle.

## Validation

Run `npm run build`. TypeScript must compile cleanly. The snapshot data is not yet
rendered (that is CCP-634) but should be accessible via `retro.getSolvingMoveSnapshot()`
after a move is played.

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-633
```

After all work is complete:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-633 --checklist "- [ ] Engine updates fed to retro.onEngineUpdate for active candidate position only|- [ ] SolvingMoveSnapshot captured at move-play time|- [ ] Snapshot cleared on jumpToNext and reset|- [ ] DualEvalDiff type defined|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-633 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] Engine updates fed to retro.onEngineUpdate for active candidate position only|- [ ] SolvingMoveSnapshot captured at move-play time|- [ ] Snapshot cleared on jumpToNext and reset|- [ ] DualEvalDiff type defined|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

# CCP-634 — Retro view: live vindication notice and stacked eval diff boxes

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

Part 3 of 4 in the retro live eval diff feature (manager: CCP-636).
Depends on CCP-632 and CCP-633 being complete.

This prompt updates `retroView.ts` to:

1. Show a live vindication notice in the `find` state when `retro.isVindicated()` is true.
2. Replace the existing single `renderEvalDiff` badge with two stacked color-coded eval
   diff boxes shown in the `win`, `fail`, and `view` feedback states.

## Target file

- `src/analyse/retroView.ts`

## Changes required

### Vindication notice (find state)

In the `find` state feedback block, add a conditional above the skip/view choices:

```typescript
retro.isVindicated() && h('div.retro-vindication', [
  h('div.retro-vindication__icon', '✓'),
  h('p.retro-vindication__msg',
    'Actually upon deeper review, you did play the best move during the game.'),
  renderContinue(retro, navigate, redraw),
]),
```

When vindicated, still show the existing "X was played / Find a better move" content
above — the vindication block appears below it. The user clicks Next from the
vindication block to advance.

Add `isVindicated(): boolean` to the `RetroCtrl` interface (implemented in CCP-632).

### Stacked eval diff boxes (win / fail / view states)

Replace `renderEvalDiff(cand?.evalDiff)` in all three states with a call to
`renderDualEvalBoxes(retro)`.

```typescript
function renderDualEvalBoxes(retro: RetroCtrl): VNode | null {
  const snapshot = retro.getSolvingMoveSnapshot();
  if (!snapshot) return null;

  // --- Box 1: solving move vs engine best ---
  // diff = mover(solvingMove) - mover(engineBest)
  // 0 = exact best → green ✓
  // -0.01 to -0.5 = slightly worse → yellow
  // < -0.5 but better than game move → blue
  // worse than or equal to game move → red
  const vsBestDiff = computeMoverCpDiff(snapshot.solvingMoveCp, snapshot.engineBestCp, snapshot.playerColor);
  const vsGameDiff = computeMoverCpDiff(snapshot.solvingMoveCp, snapshot.gameMoveCp, snapshot.playerColor);

  return h('div.retro-eval-boxes', [
    renderEvalBox(vsBestDiff, 'vs-best', 'vs Engine Best'),
    renderEvalBox(vsGameDiff, 'vs-game', 'vs Move Played'),
  ]);
}
```

**`computeMoverCpDiff(a, b, color)`**: returns `(moverCp(a) - moverCp(b)) / 100` in
pawn units. `moverCp = color === 'white' ? cp : -cp`. Returns `null` if either cp
is undefined.

**`renderEvalBox(diffPawns, key, label)`**:

Tier logic for `vs-best` box:
- `diffPawns === 0` → `grade: 'best'` → display `✓`
- `diffPawns > -0.5 && diffPawns < 0` → `grade: 'near'` → display diff as `-0.3`
- `diffPawns <= -0.5` and solving move better than game → `grade: 'ok'`
- solving move worse than or equal to game move → `grade: 'bad'`

For `vs-game` box:
- `diffPawns > 0` → `grade: 'ok'` (blue — better than game)
- `diffPawns === 0` → `grade: 'near'` (yellow — equal to game)
- `diffPawns < 0` → `grade: 'bad'` (red — worse than game)

```typescript
h('div.retro-eval-box', { class: { [`retro-eval-box--${grade}`]: true } }, [
  h('span.retro-eval-box__label', label),
  h('span.retro-eval-box__value', display),
])
```

### What NOT to change

- Do not change session lifecycle, navigation, or save-to-library logic.
- Do not change `retroCtrl.ts` — that is CCP-632.
- Do not add CSS — that is CCP-635.

## Validation

Run `npm run build`. Open a reviewed game, click Mistakes.
- Verify vindication notice appears when engine confirms game move was best.
- Verify two stacked boxes appear after playing any move in win/fail/view states.
- Verify boxes render without crashing when snapshot is unavailable.

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-634
```

After all work is complete:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-634 --checklist "- [ ] Vindication notice appears in find state when engine confirms game move|- [ ] Two stacked eval boxes render in win/fail/view states|- [ ] vs-best box shows checkmark for exact best move|- [ ] vs-game box correctly grades better/equal/worse than game move|- [ ] No crash when snapshot unavailable|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-634 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] Vindication notice appears in find state when engine confirms game move|- [ ] Two stacked eval boxes render in win/fail/view states|- [ ] vs-best box shows checkmark for exact best move|- [ ] vs-game box correctly grades better/equal/worse than game move|- [ ] No crash when snapshot unavailable|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```
