# CCP-635 — Retro styles: color-coded eval diff boxes

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

Part 4 of 4 in the retro live eval diff feature (manager: CCP-636).
Depends on CCP-634 being complete.

This prompt adds CSS for the eval diff boxes and the vindication notice rendered
by CCP-634. All rules go inside the existing `.retro-box { }` block in
`src/styles/main.scss`, before the closing `}` (after `.retro-engine-reveal`).

## Target file

- `src/styles/main.scss` only

## Changes required

### Vindication notice

```scss
.retro-vindication {
  margin-top: 12px;
  padding: 10px 12px;
  border-left: 3px solid #759900;
  background: rgba(117, 153, 0, 0.1);
  border-radius: 0 4px 4px 0;
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
  gap: 6px;

  &__icon {
    font-size: 1.2rem;
    color: #759900;
  }

  &__msg {
    margin: 0;
    font-size: 0.82rem;
    color: #aaa;
    line-height: 1.5;
  }
}
```

### Stacked eval diff boxes container

```scss
.retro-eval-boxes {
  display: flex;
  flex-direction: column;
  gap: 6px;
  margin-top: 10px;
}
```

### Individual eval box

Each box has a label on the left and a value badge on the right.
Four color tiers: `--best` (green), `--near` (yellow), `--ok` (blue), `--bad` (red).

```scss
.retro-eval-box {
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: space-between;
  padding: 5px 10px;
  border-radius: 4px;
  border: 1px solid transparent;

  &__label {
    font-size: 0.78rem;
    color: #888;
  }

  &__value {
    font-size: 0.85rem;
    font-weight: 700;
    font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums;
    min-width: 2.5em;
    text-align: right;
  }

  // Green — exact best move
  &--best {
    border-color: #3a6b2a;
    background: rgba(117, 153, 0, 0.12);
    .retro-eval-box__value { color: #759900; }
  }

  // Yellow — slightly worse than best (0 to -0.5 pawns)
  &--near {
    border-color: #7a6000;
    background: rgba(240, 165, 0, 0.1);
    .retro-eval-box__value { color: #f0a500; }
  }

  // Blue — better than game move, more than 0.5 pawns from best
  &--ok {
    border-color: #2a4a7a;
    background: rgba(56, 100, 160, 0.1);
    .retro-eval-box__value { color: #7aaddf; }
  }

  // Red — worse than or equal to original game move
  &--bad {
    border-color: #6b2a2a;
    background: rgba(220, 50, 47, 0.1);
    .retro-eval-box__value { color: #dc322f; }
  }
}
```

### What NOT to change

- Do not change any existing retro rules.
- Do not touch any file other than `src/styles/main.scss`.

## Validation

Run `npm run build`. Open a reviewed game, click Mistakes, play moves.
- Green box with ✓ when solving move matches engine best.
- Yellow box when within 0.5 pawns of best.
- Blue box when better than game move but clearly not best.
- Red box when worse than or equal to the original game move.
- Vindication notice styled with green left border when it appears.

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-635
```

After all work is complete:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-635 --checklist "- [ ] Green box with correct color for exact best move|- [ ] Yellow box for 0 to -0.5 pawn range|- [ ] Blue box for better than game but not best|- [ ] Red box for worse than or equal to game move|- [ ] Vindication notice has green left border styling|- [ ] No regression on existing retro styles|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-635 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] Green box with correct color for exact best move|- [ ] Yellow box for 0 to -0.5 pawn range|- [ ] Blue box for better than game but not best|- [ ] Red box for worse than or equal to game move|- [ ] Vindication notice has green left border styling|- [ ] No regression on existing retro styles|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

# CCP-636 — Retro live eval diff manager

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

Manager prompt for the retro live eval diff feature.

This feature makes the Learn From Your Mistakes puzzle session evaluate every move
the user plays during solving against the live engine's best move in real time.
It also notifies the user immediately when the engine determines the original game
move was actually best.

## Child prompts — run in order

1. **CCP-632** — retroCtrl.ts: track live best move, detect game-move vindication
2. **CCP-633** — retroMoveHandler.ts + main.ts: capture solving move, compute dual diffs
3. **CCP-634** — retroView.ts: vindication notice + stacked eval diff boxes
4. **CCP-635** — main.scss: color-coded box styles

Each child prompt must be fully complete and building cleanly before the next begins.

## Feature summary

**Live engine tracking:**
- The live engine continuously updates the best move for the active candidate position.
- Whichever depth is higher — live or batch — wins for both the best move reference
  and the eval value.
- The puzzle's correct answer updates dynamically as the engine deepens.

**Vindication (find state):**
- The moment the engine determines the original game move was actually the best move
  available, a notice appears immediately:
  "Actually upon deeper review, you did play the best move during the game."
- No move needs to be played to trigger this.
- User must manually click Next to advance.

**Dual eval diff boxes (all post-move states):**
- Shown after any move is played: correct, near-best, or wrong.
- Two stacked boxes, always mover-perspective pawn units:
  - Top: solving move vs engine best
  - Bottom: solving move vs original game move

**Color tiers (top box — vs engine best):**
- Green + ✓ — exact best move (diff = 0)
- Yellow — slightly worse (0 to -0.5 pawns from best)
- Blue — better than game move but more than -0.5 from best
- Red — worse than or equal to the original game move

**Color tiers (bottom box — vs game move):**
- Blue — better than game move
- Yellow — equal to game move
- Red — worse than game move

**Sign convention:** always mover-perspective. Positive = better for the player
being reviewed, regardless of color.

## Lifecycle

Before running any child prompts, mark this manager as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-636
```

After all four child prompts are complete and verified:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-636 --checklist "- [ ] CCP-632 complete: live engine tracking and vindication detection|- [ ] CCP-633 complete: solving move snapshot and dual diff wiring|- [ ] CCP-634 complete: vindication notice and stacked boxes rendered|- [ ] CCP-635 complete: all four color tiers styled correctly|- [ ] Full flow tested: play correct move → green box|- [ ] Full flow tested: play near-best → yellow box|- [ ] Full flow tested: play worse than game → red box|- [ ] Vindication notice appears without playing a move when engine confirms game move"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-636 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] CCP-632 complete: live engine tracking and vindication detection|- [ ] CCP-633 complete: solving move snapshot and dual diff wiring|- [ ] CCP-634 complete: vindication notice and stacked boxes rendered|- [ ] CCP-635 complete: all four color tiers styled correctly|- [ ] Full flow tested: play correct move → green box|- [ ] Full flow tested: play near-best → yellow box|- [ ] Full flow tested: play worse than game → red box|- [ ] Vindication notice appears without playing a move when engine confirms game move"
```

# CCP-637 — Audit and fix bulk-review accuracy scores

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

Accuracy scores produced by bulk game review are incorrect regardless of engine depth.
This is a user-reported bug: games reviewed in batch show accuracy numbers that do not
reflect real play quality, and the problem persists even at higher depths.

The goal of this prompt is to:
1. Understand exactly how accuracy is computed from batch review data
2. Identify the root cause of the incorrect numbers — depth may be a contributing factor
   but is not confirmed as the sole cause
3. Decide whether to fix the calculation, gate the display on a quality threshold,
   or hide accuracy entirely for bulk-reviewed games

## Background

Accuracy in Patzer Pro is derived from win-chance loss per move, following the Lichess model:
- For each move, compute the win-chance delta (mover-perspective)
- Accuracy per move = a function of that delta
- Game accuracy = average across all moves

The bug manifests as accuracy numbers that appear random or implausible across games reviewed
in batch, at varying depths. The root cause is unknown — possibilities include a formula
error, a sign or perspective bug, incorrect move inclusion, or a wiring problem between
batch eval data and the accuracy calculation path.

## Investigation required

Before writing any fix, read and understand:

1. **Where accuracy is computed** — find the function(s) that calculate per-move and
   per-game accuracy. Likely in `src/engine/` or `src/analyse/`.

2. **How batch review feeds accuracy** — check `src/engine/batch.ts`. Trace how batch
   eval results reach the accuracy calculation. Is the data path the same as for
   single-game review? Are there places where eval fields (cp, mate, depth, best) could
   be missing or structured differently?

3. **How Lichess handles accuracy** — check `ui/lib/src/ceval/winningChances.ts` and
   the Lichess accuracy logic. What formula does Lichess use? Are there any guards,
   exclusions (book moves, forced moves), or minimum-depth gates?

4. **What "wildly off" looks like concretely** — trace an example game through the
   accuracy path and identify the specific step where the output diverges from
   expected. Report the diagnosis before writing any fix.

## Decision tree

After investigation, apply exactly one of these fixes:

**Option A — Formula or wiring fix**: If the root cause is a bad formula, wrong sign
convention, or a wiring gap between batch evals and the accuracy path, fix it. Document
the correct Lichess-aligned formula and verify output is plausible (e.g. 70–85% for a
clean game, not 12% or 99%).

**Option B — Depth or quality gate**: If accuracy is inherently unreliable below a
certain eval quality threshold, suppress the display when that threshold isn't met.
Show "—" or "N/A" instead. Gate value should match Lichess's threshold if one exists
in the source.

**Option C — Hide entirely for bulk**: If accuracy cannot be computed reliably from
batch review data regardless of depth or formula, add a flag to suppress accuracy
display on bulk-reviewed games and explain this clearly in the UI.

Only implement one option. Do not bundle options. Pick the one that is most faithful to
Lichess behavior and document why.

## Files likely involved

- `src/engine/batch.ts` — bulk review pipeline, eval data shape
- `src/engine/winchances.ts` — win-chance calculation
- `src/analyse/` — accuracy summary rendering
- `src/main.ts` — where accuracy is wired into the display
- `~/Development/lichess-source/lila/ui/lib/src/ceval/winningChances.ts` — Lichess reference

## What NOT to do

- Do not change the bulk review depth target without a separate tracked prompt
- Do not redesign the accuracy display UI beyond gating/hiding the broken number
- Do not touch puzzle or retrospection logic

## Validation

- Run `npm run build` — must pass cleanly
- Open a bulk-reviewed game and verify the accuracy display matches the chosen fix
- Open a single-reviewed game and verify accuracy still displays correctly (or is
  consistently gated/hidden if Option C)
- If Option A: confirm formula output is plausible across multiple games
- If Option B: confirm the gate threshold is documented and sourced from Lichess or
  explicitly noted as a Patzer deviation
- If Option C: confirm no accuracy number appears on any bulk-reviewed game

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-637
```

After all work is complete:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-637 --checklist "- [ ] Root cause of incorrect accuracy scores identified and documented|- [ ] Lichess accuracy formula and any gate behavior confirmed from source|- [ ] Fix implemented: Option A/B/C (state which and why)|- [ ] Bulk-reviewed game shows correct behavior|- [ ] Single-reviewed game accuracy unaffected (or consistently handled)|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-637 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] Root cause of incorrect accuracy scores identified and documented|- [ ] Lichess accuracy formula and any gate behavior confirmed from source|- [ ] Fix implemented: Option A/B/C (state which and why)|- [ ] Bulk-reviewed game shows correct behavior|- [ ] Single-reviewed game accuracy unaffected (or consistently handled)|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

# CCP-638 — Review button splits into queue-position icons when queue is active

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

When no review queue is active, each game row in the games list shows a single Review
button. When a review queue IS active, that button should split into two icon-buttons
that occupy the exact same total width — one to add the game to the top of the queue
("Review Next") and one to add it to the bottom ("Add to Queue").

**Important prerequisite:** it is not confirmed that a review queue with ordered
add-to-top / add-to-bottom semantics currently exists in the codebase. The executor
must audit the current queue implementation first. If add-to-top / add-to-bottom
queue positioning does not exist, that infrastructure must be engineered as part of
this prompt before the UI can be wired up.

## Behaviour spec

**No queue active (current behaviour — do not change):**
- Single Review button per game row
- Clicking it starts a review for that game immediately

**Queue active — single game row:**
- The Review button is replaced by two icon-buttons side by side
- Combined width = exactly the width of the single Review button
- Each icon-button takes 50% of that width
- Left icon: add to top of queue ("Review Next")
- Right icon: add to bottom of queue ("Add to Queue")
- Each icon has a visible hover tooltip:
  - Left: "Review next"
  - Right: "Add to queue"
- Clicking either button adds that game to the correct queue position and gives
  immediate visual feedback on the row (e.g. brief highlight or icon state change)

**Queue active — multiple games selected:**
- The existing multi-select behaviour in the games list must be preserved
- When one or more games are selected and the user clicks either icon on any selected
  row, ALL currently selected games are added to the top or bottom of the queue
  respectively, in the order they appear in the list
- This mirrors standard multi-select queue behaviour in media player apps

## Visual design direction

Style these buttons to follow the visual language already established in the app.
The primary external reference for the interaction pattern and icon style is the
**Pocket Casts** podcast app's "Play Next" / "Play Last" queue buttons, which appear
in their track list rows. Key characteristics to mirror:

- Icon-only buttons — no label text inside the button
- Icons are compact and vertically centered within the row height
- "Add to top" icon: conveys "move to front" — a common approach is an arrow-with-line
  pointing up (⬆ with a bar at top) or a similar queue-top metaphor. Choose whichever
  reads most clearly at small size from standard Unicode or an SVG icon.
- "Add to bottom" icon: same metaphor pointing down
- Buttons have a subtle hover state (background tint or opacity change) consistent with
  other interactive elements in the games list
- No border between the two buttons, or a very thin divider — they should read as a
  unified control occupying the original button footprint
- Inactive / disabled state should be visually distinct if a game is already in the
  queue at either end (optional — implement only if trivially available from queue state)

Do not introduce new design patterns. Match existing button colors, border-radius,
and spacing conventions already present in the app's game list rows.

## Pre-implementation checklist

Before writing any code:

1. **Audit the review queue** — does a review queue with ordered positioning currently
   exist? Check `src/engine/batch.ts` and any queue module. Specifically confirm:
   - Is there a concept of an ordered queue of games awaiting review?
   - Is there an API to add a game to the top vs bottom of that queue?
   - If not, document what exists and what needs to be added.

2. **Read the current games list view** — find where the Review button is rendered in
   `src/games/view.ts`. Understand the button's current width, class, and click handler.

3. **Read multi-select handling** — find where selected games are tracked. Understand
   what state or set holds the current selection so the bulk-add path can use it.

4. **Confirm queue-active detection** — find the predicate or flag that indicates a
   review queue is currently running. This gates the button split.

5. **Check Lichess reference** — look for any analogous split-button, queue-action, or
   icon-button pattern in `ui/analyse/src/` or `ui/lib/src/` that informs the
   implementation. Lichess study/playlist patterns may be relevant.

## Implementation scope

**If the queue infrastructure does not exist:**
Engineer the minimum required queue primitives (ordered list, add-to-top, add-to-bottom)
before touching the UI. Keep this isolated to the appropriate module — do not expand
`main.ts`. Document what was added.

**UI changes (always required):**
- `src/games/view.ts` — conditional rendering of single button vs split icon pair;
  multi-select bulk-add path
- `src/styles/main.scss` — styles for the split icon buttons

Do not use raw DOM manipulation. All rendering uses `h()` Snabbdom vnodes.

Width constraint: wrap both buttons in a `div` constrained to the same width as the
original Review button; each child `button` at `width: 50%`.

Hover tooltip: use the `title` attribute on each `button` element. This is the standard
approach used elsewhere in the app for icon-button affordances.

## What NOT to do

- Do not change multi-select selection logic — only the action triggered by the icon buttons
- Do not redesign the game row layout beyond the button cell
- Do not add labels or text inside the icon buttons

## Validation

- Run `npm run build` — must pass cleanly
- With no queue active: single Review button is unchanged
- With a queue active:
  - Review button is replaced by two equal-width icon buttons
  - Combined width matches the original Review button width
  - Hovering each button shows the correct tooltip text
  - Clicking the left icon on a single-selected row adds that game to the top of the queue
  - Clicking the right icon on a single-selected row adds that game to the bottom
  - With multiple games selected, clicking either icon adds all selected games in list order
    to the top or bottom respectively
- No layout shift or wrapping at normal game list widths
- Icons are legible and visually distinct at row height

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-638
```

After all work is complete:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-638 --checklist "- [ ] Queue infrastructure confirmed or engineered (add-to-top and add-to-bottom)|- [ ] Single Review button unchanged when no queue active|- [ ] Split icon buttons appear when queue is active, matching original button width|- [ ] Left icon = Review Next (top of queue), right icon = Add to Queue (bottom)|- [ ] Hover tooltips present and correct on both icons|- [ ] Multi-select: all selected games added to correct position in list order|- [ ] Icon style consistent with app visual language|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-638 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] Queue infrastructure confirmed or engineered (add-to-top and add-to-bottom)|- [ ] Single Review button unchanged when no queue active|- [ ] Split icon buttons appear when queue is active, matching original button width|- [ ] Left icon = Review Next (top of queue), right icon = Add to Queue (bottom)|- [ ] Hover tooltips present and correct on both icons|- [ ] Multi-select: all selected games added to correct position in list order|- [ ] Icon style consistent with app visual language|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

# CCP-639 — Audit all buttons for hover text and aria-label accessibility

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

Every interactive button in the app should have:
1. A `title` attribute — browser-native hover tooltip visible to sighted users
2. An `aria-label` attribute — the WCAG-required accessible name read by screen readers

This prompt audits every button in the codebase, fixes all gaps, and establishes the
standard that must be followed for all buttons created going forward.

## Accessibility standard

The correct pattern for all buttons (especially icon-only buttons) is:

```typescript
h('button', {
  attrs: {
    title: 'Human-readable description of what this button does',
    'aria-label': 'Human-readable description of what this button does',
  },
}, iconOrLabel)
```

**Why both:**
- `title` renders as a browser tooltip on hover — visible to sighted users
- `aria-label` is the WCAG 4.1.2 (Level A) accessible name — used by screen readers,
  voice control, and other assistive technologies
- `title` alone is not reliably announced by all screen readers
- `aria-label` alone gives no visible affordance to sighted users

**When they can differ:**
- `title` may include slightly more context (e.g. keyboard shortcut hint)
- `aria-label` should be the concise accessible name
- Example: `title: "Flip board (F)"`, `aria-label: "Flip board"`

**For buttons with visible text labels:**
- `aria-label` is not required if the button text already provides the accessible name
- `title` is still encouraged for additional context or shortcut hints
- Icon-only buttons always require both

**For buttons with both an icon and a visible text label:**
- `aria-label` is not required — the visible text is the accessible name
- `title` may still be added for shortcut hints or extra context

## Audit scope

Search every `.ts` file under `src/` for `h('button'` and `h('a'` used as interactive
controls. For each one, record:

| File | Button description | Has `title`? | Has `aria-label`? | Action needed |
|---|---|---|---|---|

Categorise each button as one of:
- **Icon-only** — no visible text label; both `title` and `aria-label` required
- **Text label** — visible text present; `aria-label` not required, `title` encouraged
- **Icon + text** — both visible; `aria-label` not required, `title` encouraged

Priority order for fixing:
1. Icon-only buttons missing both — highest risk, completely opaque to screen readers
2. Icon-only buttons missing `aria-label` only
3. Icon-only buttons missing `title` only
4. Text-label buttons missing `title` (lower priority, not a WCAG failure)

## Files to search

Start with:
- `src/analyse/retroView.ts`
- `src/analyse/analysisControls.ts`
- `src/header/index.ts`
- `src/games/view.ts`
- `src/board/index.ts`
- `src/puzzles/view.ts`
- `src/openings/view.ts`
- `src/ceval/view.ts`
- `src/main.ts`

Then grep the full `src/` tree to catch any remaining files:
```sh
grep -rn "h('button'" src/
grep -rn 'h("button"' src/
```

## Fix standard

For every icon-only button found missing `title` or `aria-label`, add both attributes.
Write descriptions that are:
- Concise (3–6 words)
- Action-oriented ("Flip board", "Close", "Show engine", "Skip this move")
- Consistent — if the same action appears in multiple places, use the same wording

For buttons with visible text where `title` is absent but would add value (e.g. shortcut
hints), add `title` only — do not add a redundant `aria-label`.

## Scope constraint

This prompt touches rendering files only. Do not change:
- Business logic
- State management
- Engine or batch pipeline
- CSS / SCSS

All changes are attribute additions to existing `h('button', ...)` or `h('a', ...)`
vnodes. No new buttons, no layout changes.

## Forward-looking standard (document in CLAUDE.md)

After completing the audit and fixes, add the following note to `CLAUDE.md` under a
new section **## Button Accessibility Rule**:

```
Every button created in this project must include:
- `title` attribute — hover tooltip for sighted users (always required)
- `aria-label` attribute — accessible name for screen readers (required for icon-only buttons)

For icon-only buttons, both are always required. For buttons with visible text labels,
`title` is encouraged and `aria-label` is not required. When in doubt, add both.

Standard pattern:
  h('button', { attrs: { title: 'Do the thing', 'aria-label': 'Do the thing' } }, icon)
```

Also add the same note to `AGENTS.md` under a matching **## Button Accessibility Rule**
section so Codex agents follow the same standard.

## Validation

- Run `npm run build` — must pass cleanly
- Search `src/` for icon-only buttons — none should be missing both `title` and `aria-label`
- Manually hover over 5–10 representative buttons and confirm tooltip text appears
- Confirm CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md both contain the Button Accessibility Rule section

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-639
```

After all work is complete:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-639 --checklist "- [ ] Full audit table produced covering all button/anchor elements in src/|- [ ] All icon-only buttons have both title and aria-label|- [ ] Descriptions are concise, action-oriented, and consistent|- [ ] Button Accessibility Rule added to CLAUDE.md|- [ ] Button Accessibility Rule added to AGENTS.md|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-639 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] Full audit table produced covering all button/anchor elements in src/|- [ ] All icon-only buttons have both title and aria-label|- [ ] Descriptions are concise, action-oriented, and consistent|- [ ] Button Accessibility Rule added to CLAUDE.md|- [ ] Button Accessibility Rule added to AGENTS.md|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

# CCP-614-F1 — Bulk-copy dashboard IDs should be comma-separated only

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

Prompt ID: CCP-614-F1
Task ID: CCP-614
Parent Prompt ID: CCP-614
Short task title: Bulk-copy dashboard IDs should be comma-separated only
Source document: User request
Source step: prompt dashboard bulk-copy formatting bug fix

## Context

The prompt dashboard header includes two bulk-copy buttons:
- `Copy Needs Review IDs`
- `Copy Issues Found IDs`

Current bug:
- the copied output includes numbered-list prefixes such as `1. CCP-323, 2. CCP-355`
- the user wants plain comma-separated prompt IDs only, with no extra numbering

Expected behavior:
- copied output should be raw comma-separated prompt IDs such as:
  - `CCP-323, CCP-355, CCP-363`
- no leading ordinal numbers should be added
- both bulk-copy buttons should use the same plain-ID format

## Target files

- `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- generated output: `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

Do not hand-edit `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`.

## Required workflow

1. Inspect the current bulk-copy button logic in `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`.
2. Confirm where the numbering is added.
3. Apply the smallest safe fix so the copied values are plain comma-separated IDs only.
4. Keep the button behavior, filters, and feedback states unchanged outside this format fix.
5. Regenerate the dashboard after the change.

## Implementation guidance

- keep this fix scoped to the copied string format only
- do not redesign the buttons or the header layout
- preserve:
  - which prompts each button targets
  - the `None` fallback behavior
  - the `✓ Copied!` success feedback
- prefer changing the ID mapping at the source rather than post-processing a numbered string

## Validation

Run:

```sh
npm run prompts:dashboard
```

Open the dashboard and verify:
- `Copy Needs Review IDs` copies plain comma-separated IDs with no numbering
- `Copy Issues Found IDs` copies plain comma-separated IDs with no numbering
- paste results into a text editor and confirm the format looks like:
  - `CCP-323, CCP-355, CCP-363`
- the copied lists still contain the correct prompt IDs for each button
- `✓ Copied!` still appears briefly on success
- `None` still appears briefly when no prompts qualify
- no console errors appear

In your final report, echo:
- `Prompt ID: CCP-614-F1`
- `Task ID: CCP-614`

# CCP-640 — Fix eval diff boxes not populating in Learn From Mistakes

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

The "vs Engine Best" and "vs Move Played" eval diff boxes added in CCP-632–635 are not
populating on most moves during Learn From Mistakes sessions. The engine IS running and
completing evaluations (confirmed via console), but the displayed boxes show no values.

The bug is almost certainly a timing problem: the snapshot of `evalCache` entries is captured
synchronously at move-play time inside `retroMoveHandler.ts`, but the engine has not yet
evaluated the positions that the snapshot reads from.

## Current flow (broken)

1. User plays a move in retro mode — `retroMoveHandler.ts` fires `afterMove` hook
2. `afterMove` immediately reads three `evalCache` entries:
   - `evalCache.get(ctrl.path)` — solving move position (engine hasn't evaluated this yet)
   - `evalCache.get(cand.parentPath)` — pre-move baseline (may have batch eval, may be stale)
   - `evalCache.get(cand.path)` — game mistake position (may have batch eval, may be stale)
3. Snapshot is written with whatever (often `undefined`) values were found
4. The live engine later evaluates these positions and calls `_onLiveEvalUpdated`, but the
   filter in `main.ts` only watches for `cand.parentPath` updates — it does not update the
   snapshot when the solving move position (`ctrl.path`) completes
5. Result: boxes render as blank or missing on nearly every move

## Investigation required

Before writing any fix, trace the full data path:

1. **Read `src/analyse/retroMoveHandler.ts`** — find the `afterMove` hook. Confirm which paths
   are read from `evalCache` and when the snapshot is set.

2. **Read `src/engine/ctrl.ts`** — find `setOnLiveEvalUpdated`. Confirm what path/eval data it
   fires with after each cache write. Confirm whether solving-move positions ever trigger it.

3. **Read `src/main.ts`** — find where `setOnLiveEvalUpdated` callback is registered. Confirm
   the filter: `if (!cand || path !== cand.parentPath) return`. This filter likely needs to
   be broadened to also handle `ctrl.path` and `cand.path` updates.

4. **Read `src/analyse/retroCtrl.ts`** — find `setSolvingMoveSnapshot` and
   `getSolvingMoveSnapshot`. Understand what fields the snapshot holds and how retroView
   reads them.

5. **Read `src/analyse/retroView.ts`** — find `renderDualEvalBoxes` and
   `computeMoverCpDiff`. Understand how they consume the snapshot and what conditions
   cause them to render blank boxes vs populated ones.

## Likely fix

The correct approach is to make the snapshot **reactive to engine updates**:

Instead of capturing a one-shot snapshot at move-play time and never updating it, the
`setOnLiveEvalUpdated` callback in `main.ts` should be expanded to also handle updates
for ALL three positions involved:

- `cand.parentPath` — engine best baseline
- `ctrl.path` — solving move eval (the position the user navigated to)
- `cand.path` — game mistake move eval

When any of these three paths receives a live engine update, the relevant field(s) in the
snapshot should be refreshed and `redraw()` called.

An alternative approach: re-read `evalCache` lazily in `renderDualEvalBoxes` at render time
(passing evalCache into the view), instead of relying on a one-shot snapshot. This avoids
timing issues entirely.

Choose whichever approach is simpler and more correct. Document the choice.

## Files to modify

- `src/analyse/retroMoveHandler.ts` — snapshot capture logic
- `src/engine/ctrl.ts` — `setOnLiveEvalUpdated` callback plumbing (if signal broadening needed)
- `src/main.ts` — callback registration and filter logic
- `src/analyse/retroCtrl.ts` — snapshot update method (if reactive approach chosen)
- `src/analyse/retroView.ts` — lazy evalCache read (if lazy approach chosen)

Maximum 3 files changed. If the fix requires more, split and implement the most critical path first.

## What NOT to do

- Do not change the retro puzzle selection logic
- Do not change the vindication logic
- Do not redesign the eval box UI layout or styles
- Do not touch any files outside the retro / engine plumbing path

## Validation

- Run `npm run build` — must pass cleanly
- Open a bulk-reviewed game and enter Learn From Mistakes mode
- Play a move (correct or incorrect) — eval diff boxes must populate within ~1–2 seconds
  as the engine completes evaluation of the solving move position
- Play 5–10 retro moves across different games — boxes should populate consistently
- Verify the "vs Engine Best" diff uses mover-perspective (positive = better than engine best)
- Verify the "vs Move Played" diff uses mover-perspective (positive = better than game mistake)

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-640
```

After all work is complete:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-640 --checklist "- [ ] Root cause of missing eval diff values identified and documented|- [ ] Snapshot capture or live-update path fixed so all three positions populate|- [ ] setOnLiveEvalUpdated filter broadened (or lazy evalCache read implemented)|- [ ] Eval diff boxes populate consistently on retro moves|- [ ] vs Engine Best and vs Move Played diffs are mover-perspective correct|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-640 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] Root cause of missing eval diff values identified and documented|- [ ] Snapshot capture or live-update path fixed so all three positions populate|- [ ] setOnLiveEvalUpdated filter broadened (or lazy evalCache read implemented)|- [ ] Eval diff boxes populate consistently on retro moves|- [ ] vs Engine Best and vs Move Played diffs are mover-perspective correct|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```
```


## CCP-370-F1 — Reviewed

- Task: fix the Style dashboard so the top-3 opening concentration metric is not shown twice
- Task ID: `CCP-370`
- Parent prompt ID: `CCP-370`
- Source document: `CCP-370 review`
```

# CCP-641 — Recommended Next Box: Fallback to Unrun Queued Prompts

## Context

The dashboard's "Recommended Next" box (`renderUrgentBlock` in `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`)
currently shows only prompts that are `queued-pending` AND meet a high-priority filter:
`priority === 'critical' | 'high'` OR `category === 'bugfix' | 'typecheck'`.

When no prompts meet those criteria the box disappears entirely. The user wants it to remain
useful by falling back to the next batch of un-run queued prompts.

## Task

Modify `renderUrgentBlock` in `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs` so that:

1. **Primary list** — existing criteria unchanged: `queued-pending` prompts that are
   `critical`/`high` priority or `bugfix`/`typecheck` category, sorted as today.

2. **Fallback list** — when the primary list is empty, show up to **5** `queued-pending`
   prompts sorted ascending by numeric ID (lowest CCP number first = earliest created =
   the natural completion order).

3. **Manager deduplication** — for both lists, before rendering:
   - Build a reverse index: for every manager prompt (`kind === 'manager'`) collect its
     `batchPromptIds`.
   - If a prompt in the candidate list is a member of any manager's `batchPromptIds`,
     replace it with the manager prompt (if not already in the list).
   - Remove the original child prompt from the list.
   - De-duplicate: if the manager is already in the list, don't add it twice.
   - Apply this deduplication **before** the 5-item cap on the fallback list.

4. **Label** — when displaying the fallback list, change the heading count suffix to
   read `"N prompt(s) — queued"` (instead of the primary list's `"N prompt(s)"`) so the
   user can tell which mode is active.  A simple CSS class or inline style change is fine.

## Scope

- **Only file to touch:** `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- Do not change `isUrgent`, `renderList`, sort logic, CSS, or any other function.
- The change is entirely within `renderUrgentBlock` and any small helper built inside it.

## Validation

- With critical/high/bugfix/typecheck prompts present: box shows them (unchanged behavior).
- With none of those present: box shows up to 5 queued-pending prompts, lowest IDs first.
- If any of the shown prompts are children of a manager, the manager ID appears instead.
- The fallback heading count says "N prompt(s) — queued".
- `npm run prompts:refresh` succeeds with no errors after the change.

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-641
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-641 --checklist "- [ ] With high/critical/bugfix prompts queued: box shows them as before|- [ ] With no high-priority prompts: box shows up to 5 queued-pending by lowest ID first|- [ ] Child prompts of a manager are replaced by the manager in the list|- [ ] Fallback count label reads 'N prompt(s) — queued'|- [ ] npm run prompts:refresh completes without errors"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-641 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] With high/critical/bugfix prompts queued: box shows them as before|- [ ] With no high-priority prompts: box shows up to 5 queued-pending by lowest ID first|- [ ] Child prompts of a manager are replaced by the manager in the list|- [ ] Fallback count label reads 'N prompt(s) — queued'|- [ ] npm run prompts:refresh completes without errors"
```

# CCP-642 — Sprint dashboard card layout polish

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

The `Sprints` tab in the tracking dashboard is functional, but the card layout currently feels
dense and visually underdesigned. The sprint cards are carrying useful information, but the
current hierarchy makes the content feel closer to a debug panel than a polished dashboard
surface.

The card layout currently combines:
- title and source doc
- status badge
- a three-label progress stack
- summary text
- pills for tasks, prompts, audits, next steps, and dependencies

This needs a stronger visual rhythm, spacing system, and information hierarchy without changing
the underlying sprint semantics.

## Relevant files

- `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- generated output to inspect after changes:
  - `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

## Goal

Improve the sprint card presentation so the `Sprints` tab feels intentional and easier to scan.

Focus on:
- better spacing and grouping within each card
- clearer separation between title/meta, progress, summary, and pills
- more balanced visual treatment for dependency pills and metadata pills
- improved contrast and polish on dark surfaces

## Constraints

- Keep this scoped to sprint card layout and card styling only
- Do not redesign prompt-tab UI
- Do not change sprint filtering, sprint data semantics, or detail-panel structure
- Do not edit generated `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` directly
- Keep the current dark theme direction, but make it feel more designed and less utilitarian

## Design direction

Use design judgment, not just spacing tweaks. The sprint cards should feel more like a purposeful
dashboard component and less like a plain record dump.

Good directions include:
- stronger card section rhythm
- more deliberate use of muted typography tiers
- cleaner grouping of pills into readable clusters
- a more polished top row so the status badge feels integrated instead of floating

## Validation

Run:
```sh
npm run dashboard:generate
```

Open `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` and verify:
- sprint cards are easier to scan at a glance
- title, source document, status, progress, summary, and pills feel visually grouped
- dependency pills no longer visually disappear into the rest of the metadata
- no regressions appear in the `Prompts` tab

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-642
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-642 --checklist "- [ ] Open the Sprints tab and confirm card hierarchy is easier to scan|- [ ] Confirm title/status/progress/summary/pills read as separate visual groups|- [ ] Confirm dependency pills remain readable and distinct|- [ ] Confirm the Prompts tab still renders correctly after regenerating the dashboard"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-642 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Open the Sprints tab and confirm card hierarchy is easier to scan|- [ ] Confirm title/status/progress/summary/pills read as separate visual groups|- [ ] Confirm dependency pills remain readable and distinct|- [ ] Confirm the Prompts tab still renders correctly after regenerating the dashboard"
```

# CCP-643 — Audit sprint dashboard UI and land a design plan

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

The sprint tracking system is implemented and the `Sprints` tab is now present in the shared
dashboard, but the visual design was never intentionally polished. The user explicitly wanted the
design review and UI shaping offloaded to Claude, and that step was skipped.

Before broader UI polish prompts are executed, this sprint-tab surface needs a design audit that
identifies the biggest visual problems and establishes a concrete direction for the follow-up
passes.

## Relevant files

- `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- generated output to inspect after changes:
  - `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`
- sprint tracking docs for semantic context only if needed:
  - `docs/mini-sprints/SPRINT_REGISTRY_README.md`

## Goal

Do a focused sprint-tab UI audit, identify the main visual problems, and make the smallest safe
structural or styling adjustment needed to support the later polish prompts.

Expected output in Claude's report:
- exact current visual problems
- design direction for the sprint tab
- what was changed now vs what should be left for later prompts

## Scope

This is not the full redesign prompt.

This prompt should:
- inspect the current `Sprints` tab as rendered
- identify the most obvious hierarchy, spacing, density, and scanability issues
- land one small foundational improvement if needed to support the rest of the UI polish work

This prompt should not:
- try to solve every sprint-tab design issue at once
- rewrite the whole dashboard renderer
- change sprint data semantics

## Implementation guidance

Prefer one safe foundational improvement such as:
- improving the sprint-tab container rhythm
- strengthening the overall section framing around the sprint grid and toolbar
- clarifying the visual shell that the later prompts will build on

Keep the actual implementation small and safe, but make the diagnosis strong and useful.

## Validation

Run:
```sh
npm run dashboard:generate
```

Open `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` and verify:
- the `Sprints` tab still renders correctly
- the foundational visual cleanup is visible
- the follow-up polish prompts still have clean, well-bounded seams to work on

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-643
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-643 --checklist "- [ ] Inspect the Sprints tab and identify the most obvious visual hierarchy problems|- [ ] Confirm the foundational cleanup improves the sprint-tab shell without changing sprint semantics|- [ ] Confirm the dashboard still renders correctly after regeneration|- [ ] Confirm the remaining card/progress/detail/mobile work is still cleanly separable into later prompts"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-643 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Inspect the Sprints tab and identify the most obvious visual hierarchy problems|- [ ] Confirm the foundational cleanup improves the sprint-tab shell without changing sprint semantics|- [ ] Confirm the dashboard still renders correctly after regeneration|- [ ] Confirm the remaining card/progress/detail/mobile work is still cleanly separable into later prompts"
```

# CCP-644 — Improve sprint progress and status visualization

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

The sprint cards and sprint detail view already show useful status data:
- sprint status badge
- plan coverage
- execution progress
- implementation progress
- segmented progress bar by task state

But the current presentation is visually weak. The progress stack reads as flat text, the
relationship between the three percentages is not especially intuitive, and the status treatment
does not yet feel polished enough for a dashboard meant to communicate planning truth,
execution truth, and audit truth.

## Relevant files

- `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- generated output to inspect after changes:
  - `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

## Goal

Make progress and status information read clearly and feel visually deliberate.

Improve:
- the readability of the three progress dimensions
- the visual treatment of the segmented progress bar
- the hierarchy between overall sprint status and supporting progress metrics
- the consistency of status styling between sprint cards and sprint detail

## Constraints

- Keep this scoped to sprint progress/status visualization
- Do not redesign the full card layout beyond what this visualization needs
- Do not change sprint math, registry data, or state names
- Do not edit generated HTML directly

## Design direction

The sprint tab should make it easy to answer:
- how planned-out is this sprint
- how much execution happened
- how much is actually implemented / verified

Use visual hierarchy, labels, spacing, and bar treatment to make those distinctions clear.

## Validation

Run:
```sh
npm run dashboard:generate
```

Open `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` and verify:
- plan coverage, execution progress, and implementation progress are easier to distinguish
- the segmented progress bar feels intentional and legible
- sprint status badges and progress indicators feel consistent between card and detail surfaces
- no prompt-tab visuals regress

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-644
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-644 --checklist "- [ ] Confirm the three sprint progress dimensions are visually easier to distinguish|- [ ] Confirm the segmented progress bar is legible and polished on dark surfaces|- [ ] Confirm sprint status treatment feels consistent between card and detail views|- [ ] Confirm the Prompts tab still renders correctly after dashboard regeneration"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-644 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Confirm the three sprint progress dimensions are visually easier to distinguish|- [ ] Confirm the segmented progress bar is legible and polished on dark surfaces|- [ ] Confirm sprint status treatment feels consistent between card and detail views|- [ ] Confirm the Prompts tab still renders correctly after dashboard regeneration"
```

# CCP-645 — Refine sprint detail panel information hierarchy

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

The sprint detail panel currently exposes the right data, but the presentation is crowded and
flat. Dependencies, audits, recommended next steps, phases, tasks, and unassigned prompts all
compete for attention in similar visual containers.

This is making the detail view harder to read than it needs to be, especially for large sprints
with many phases and tasks.

## Relevant files

- `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- generated output to inspect after changes:
  - `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

## Goal

Improve the sprint detail overlay so the information hierarchy is clearer and the user can more
quickly distinguish:
- summary/meta information
- dependencies and audits
- recommended next steps
- phases and tasks
- unassigned prompts

## Constraints

- Keep this scoped to sprint detail-panel structure and styling
- Do not change sprint data semantics
- Do not redesign the full dashboard shell
- Do not edit generated `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` directly

## Design direction

Use grouping and hierarchy, not just smaller text tweaks. The detail view should feel like a
purposeful inspection panel instead of one long list of similarly styled blocks.

Good improvements include:
- clearer section separation
- stronger task-row hierarchy
- better treatment for recommended next steps so they read as actionable
- more readable phase framing for large sprints

## Validation

Run:
```sh
npm run dashboard:generate
```

Open `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`, open several sprint details, and verify:
- the detail overlay is easier to scan
- recommended next steps feel distinct from audits and dependencies
- phases and task rows are easier to parse in large sprints
- unassigned prompts still remain visible without overwhelming the rest of the panel

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-645
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-645 --checklist "- [ ] Open multiple sprint detail views and confirm section hierarchy is easier to scan|- [ ] Confirm recommended next steps are visually distinct from audits and dependencies|- [ ] Confirm phase and task rows are easier to parse on large sprints|- [ ] Confirm unassigned prompts remain visible without dominating the detail panel"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-645 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Open multiple sprint detail views and confirm section hierarchy is easier to scan|- [ ] Confirm recommended next steps are visually distinct from audits and dependencies|- [ ] Confirm phase and task rows are easier to parse on large sprints|- [ ] Confirm unassigned prompts remain visible without dominating the detail panel"
```

- `CCP-646` is the manager prompt id only

- stop the batch on a real validation failure, unsafe overlap, or design issue that makes the next prompt unsafe

# CCP-647 — Improve sprint dashboard responsiveness and accessibility

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

The sprint tab needs design polish, but it also needs a cleaner small-screen and interaction
story. The current card grid, toolbar, pills, and detail content can easily become cramped or
hard to scan on narrower viewports, and the visual polish pass should leave this surface more
robust rather than only nicer on desktop.

## Relevant files

- `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- generated output to inspect after changes:
  - `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

## Goal

Improve the sprint-tab experience for narrower widths and make obvious accessibility and
interaction polish improvements where they are cheap and safe.

Focus on:
- sprint toolbar wrapping and readability
- sprint card behavior on narrower widths
- pill wrapping and density
- detail overlay readability on smaller screens
- obvious focus/interaction polish for sprint-tab controls if needed

## Constraints

- Keep this scoped to responsiveness and accessibility polish for sprint-tab surfaces
- Do not rewrite the sprint data model
- Do not change prompt-tab interaction unless a shared safe improvement is required
- Do not edit generated HTML directly

## Validation

Run:
```sh
npm run dashboard:generate
```

Open `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` and verify:
- the `Sprints` tab remains usable at narrower window widths
- toolbar controls wrap cleanly
- pills do not collapse into unreadable clutter
- sprint detail content remains readable on smaller widths
- any added focus or interaction states feel consistent with the rest of the dashboard

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-647
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-647 --checklist "- [ ] Narrow the window and confirm sprint toolbar controls wrap cleanly|- [ ] Confirm sprint cards and pills remain readable at smaller widths|- [ ] Confirm the sprint detail view remains readable on smaller widths|- [ ] Confirm any added focus or interaction states feel consistent and do not regress the Prompts tab"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-647 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Narrow the window and confirm sprint toolbar controls wrap cleanly|- [ ] Confirm sprint cards and pills remain readable at smaller widths|- [ ] Confirm the sprint detail view remains readable on smaller widths|- [ ] Confirm any added focus or interaction states feel consistent and do not regress the Prompts tab"
```

# CCP-648 — Polish sprint audit copy panel design

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

Codex has already implemented the sprint-detail backend contract for sprint audit actions.

The sprint detail view now always includes a functional sprint-audit copy panel backed by
precomputed data:
- `auditPromptTemplate`
- sprint status and phase/task state rollups
- normalization readiness
- latest audit references
- linked prompt coverage

This functionality exists, but the visual presentation is still utilitarian. The user wants
Claude to make the sprint audit copy surface feel designed and intentional.

## Relevant file

- `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

Generated output to inspect:
- `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

## Goal

Improve the visual design of the always-visible sprint audit copy panel in sprint detail.

Focus on:
- hierarchy and framing
- copy-button treatment
- making the panel feel important but not overwhelming
- making the long audit prompt content feel manageable on a dark dashboard surface

## Hard constraints

- Do not change sprint semantics, registry logic, or audit-template generation logic
- Do not rename or remove the backend fields Codex added
- Do not redesign the whole sprint detail view in this prompt
- Keep this scoped to the sprint audit copy panel presentation and interaction treatment

## Backend contract already implemented

Consume the existing backend fields as-is:
- always-visible audit panel in sprint detail
- `auditPromptTemplate`
- current sprint status
- normalization readiness
- next available phase context

Do not invent new sprint state meanings.

## Validation

Run:
```sh
npm run dashboard:generate
```

Open `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`, open several sprint details, and verify:
- the sprint audit copy panel feels visually intentional
- the copy action is obvious and pleasant to use
- long audit prompt content is easier to read or manage
- no regressions appear in prompt detail or the rest of sprint detail

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh

```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-648 --checklist "- [ ] Open several sprint details and confirm the sprint audit panel feels visually intentional|- [ ] Confirm the audit copy action is obvious and easy to use|- [ ] Confirm long audit prompt content is more manageable on the dark surface|- [ ] Confirm no regressions appear elsewhere in sprint detail"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-648 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Open several sprint details and confirm the sprint audit panel feels visually intentional|- [ ] Confirm the audit copy action is obvious and easy to use|- [ ] Confirm long audit prompt content is more manageable on the dark surface|- [ ] Confirm no regressions appear elsewhere in sprint detail"
```

# CCP-649 — Polish next phase prompt creation panel

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

Codex has already implemented the backend contract for the `Generate Next Prompts For Sprint`
panel in sprint detail.

The panel is now conditionally available only when:
- the sprint is normalized enough for reliable next-phase generation
- a next available phase exists
- that phase still contains at least one task with `No Prompt Exists Yet`

The backend also already provides:
- `nextAvailablePhase`
- `tasksNeedingPrompts`
- `nextPhaseCoveredTasks`
- `nextPromptsTemplate`

The current presentation is functional, but it still reads like raw tooling instead of a polished
operator surface.

## Relevant file

- `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

Generated output to inspect:
- `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

## Goal

Improve the design and information hierarchy of the `Generate Next Prompts For Sprint` panel so
it is obvious, high-signal, and easy to trust.

Focus on:
- making the panel feel like the main action surface when it appears
- clarifying that it is scoped to the next available phase
- helping covered vs uncovered tasks read clearly
- making the copy action feel primary

## Hard constraints

- Do not change the backend rules for when this panel appears
- Do not change the generated template semantics
- Do not invent new sprint state logic
- Keep the changes scoped to this action panel and its immediate presentation

## Backend contract already implemented

Consume the existing backend fields as-is:
- `showNextPromptPanel`
- `nextAvailablePhase`
- `tasksNeedingPrompts`
- `nextPhaseCoveredTasks`
- `nextPromptsTemplate`

Do not rewrite backend derivation logic in this prompt.

## Validation

Run:
```sh
npm run dashboard:generate
```

Open `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`, open sprint details where the panel appears, and verify:
- the panel clearly reads as the next action for the sprint
- the next available phase context is visually clear
- tasks needing prompts vs already-covered tasks are easier to distinguish
- the copy action is primary and obvious

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-649
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-649 --checklist "- [ ] Open a sprint detail where the next-prompt panel appears and confirm it reads as the main action surface|- [ ] Confirm the next available phase context is visually clear|- [ ] Confirm tasks needing prompts vs already-covered tasks are easier to distinguish|- [ ] Confirm the copy action feels primary and obvious"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-649 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Open a sprint detail where the next-prompt panel appears and confirm it reads as the main action surface|- [ ] Confirm the next available phase context is visually clear|- [ ] Confirm tasks needing prompts vs already-covered tasks are easier to distinguish|- [ ] Confirm the copy action feels primary and obvious"
```

# CCP-650 — Design sprint normalization warning and mixed task presentation

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

## Context

Codex has already added backend support for two important sprint-detail states:

1. a normalization warning when a sprint is not reliable enough for next-phase prompt generation
2. mixed next-phase task coverage where some tasks already have prompts and some still need them

Those backend states are present, but the UI treatment is basic. The user wants Claude to help
design this so the warning state and mixed-task state are understandable instead of feeling like
raw diagnostic output.

## Relevant file

- `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`

Generated output to inspect:
- `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

## Goal

Improve the UI treatment for:
- the sprint normalization warning
- mixed covered/uncovered task presentation inside sprint detail

## Hard constraints

- Do not alter sprint semantics
- Do not change the rules for when the normalization warning appears
- Do not change the rules for when the next-prompt panel appears
- Keep this prompt scoped to warning/mixed-state presentation only

## Backend contract already implemented

Consume the existing backend fields as-is:
- `normalizedStructure`
- `showNormalizationWarning`
- `showNextPromptPanel`
- `nextPhaseTasks`
- `tasksNeedingPrompts`
- `nextPhaseCoveredTasks`

Do not introduce new workflow rules here.

## Validation

Run:
```sh
npm run dashboard:generate
```

Open `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` and verify:
- non-normalized sprint detail clearly communicates that an audit/normalization step is required
- mixed covered/uncovered task situations are easier to understand
- warning state feels meaningful without overpowering the rest of the view

## Lifecycle


After all work is complete, mark it as done:


If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:

# CCP-654 — Investigate: skip opponent import dialog and show loading bar inline

## Context

Currently, importing an opponent in the Opponents tool shows a dialogue (modal or
panel) before or during the fetch. Separately, once an opponent's games load, the
opening tree build triggers a visible loading bar in a fixed location in the view.

The question is: **should the import dialogue be removed entirely, and should the
initial opponent-fetch loading bar be rendered in the same place the opening tree
loading bar already lives?**

This is a research and investigation prompt. No code changes are made. The output is
a written recommendation — yes, no, or conditional — with evidence from the current
codebase and a clear rationale.

## Investigation scope

### 1. Audit the import dialogue

Find where the opponent import dialogue/panel/modal is rendered and triggered.

Answer:
- What file(s) render it?
- Is it a modal overlay, an inline panel, or something else?
- What state triggers it to open / close?
- What does it do that an inline flow would need to replicate? (e.g. username input,
  platform selection, error display, cancel affordance)
- Is there a case where the user might need to interact with it before the fetch can
  start (e.g. entering a username they haven't yet set)?

### 2. Audit the opening tree loading bar

Find where the opening tree loading bar is rendered.

Answer:
- What file renders it and what class / vnode structure?
- What state variable drives it (e.g. a boolean flag, a progress value)?
- Where in the layout does it appear — is it a fixed position within the Opponents
  view, or somewhere more global?
- How wide is its display lifecycle? (e.g. visible only during tree build, or also
  during game fetch?)

### 3. Assess the proposed change

Given the findings above, evaluate:

- **Feasibility**: can the import dialogue's function be absorbed into the inline
  loading bar area without losing any user-facing capability?
- **State coverage**: does the loading bar location have enough room to show fetch
  progress, errors, and a cancel affordance? Or does it only show a spinner/bar?
- **UX quality**: does removing the dialogue make the flow feel faster and more
  direct, or does it remove useful friction (e.g. confirming the username before
  a long fetch)?
- **Edge cases**: what happens if the username is not yet set and the user navigates
  to the Opponents tool? Does the dialogue serve as the username-entry point in that
  path?
- **Risk**: are there any states where removing the dialogue would leave the user
  with no feedback or no way to cancel a long-running fetch?

### 4. Lichess reference check

Look at `ui/learn/src/` or any analogous flow in the Lichess source where a fetch or
import is initiated without a modal — is there a pattern for inline progress
replacing a pre-fetch dialog?

## Output

Write a structured recommendation with the following sections:

### Findings
Bullet-point summary of what each audit found (dialogue, loading bar, Lichess ref).

### Recommendation
One of:
- **Yes — implement the change**: rationale + any preconditions
- **No — keep the dialogue**: rationale + what would need to change before this
  could be reconsidered
- **Conditional**: what conditions must be met first

### If yes: rough implementation notes
- Which files to touch
- What state changes are needed
- Whether existing loading bar state can be reused or extended
- Any new affordances needed (error display, cancel) that aren't currently there

Write the recommendation as a comment in the prompt body below the `## Output`
header, or in a short `docs/reference/` note if it is substantial.

**Do not make any code changes.** Research only.

## Validation

- [ ] Import dialogue location identified (file + render site)
- [ ] Dialogue responsibilities documented (what it does that inline must replicate)
- [ ] Opening tree loading bar location and state driver identified
- [ ] UX edge cases assessed (username not set, long fetch, error, cancel)
- [ ] Lichess reference checked for analogous pattern
- [ ] Written recommendation produced with clear rationale

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-654
```

After all work is complete:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-654 --checklist "- [ ] Import dialogue location identified (file + render site)|- [ ] Dialogue responsibilities documented (what it does that inline must replicate)|- [ ] Opening tree loading bar location and state driver identified|- [ ] UX edge cases assessed (username not set, long fetch, error, cancel)|- [ ] Lichess reference checked for analogous pattern|- [ ] Written recommendation produced with clear rationale"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-654 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] Import dialogue location identified (file + render site)|- [ ] Dialogue responsibilities documented (what it does that inline must replicate)|- [ ] Opening tree loading bar location and state driver identified|- [ ] UX edge cases assessed (username not set, long fetch, error, cancel)|- [ ] Lichess reference checked for analogous pattern|- [ ] Written recommendation produced with clear rationale"
```

# CCP-655 — Restore "Opponents" label on non-target color toggle button

## Context

On the Opponents opening tree page, the color toggle (`openings__color-toggle`) has
two buttons — White and Black. Each button has an `openings__color-username` span
above the color label:

- The active/selected button shows the imported player's username (e.g. "marox")
- The inactive button should show "Opponents" in the same span

The "Opponents" text was accidentally removed — the non-active button's
`openings__color-username` span was set to empty string `''` instead of
`'Opponents'`. These labels should also swap when the active color changes.

## Location

`src/openings/view.ts` — `renderColorToggle` function (~line 1988).

Two occurrences:
- White button: `filter === 'white' ? playerName : ''` → `filter === 'white' ? playerName : 'Opponents'`
- Black button: `filter === 'black' ? playerName : ''` → `filter === 'black' ? playerName : 'Opponents'`

## Validation

- [ ] White button shows username when White is selected; "Opponents" when Black is selected
- [ ] Black button shows username when Black is selected; "Opponents" when White is selected
- [ ] Labels use the same `openings__color-username` span and styling as the username
- [ ] Build passes cleanly

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-655
```

After all work is complete:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-655 --checklist "- [ ] White button shows username when White is selected; 'Opponents' when Black is selected|- [ ] Black button shows username when Black is selected; 'Opponents' when White is selected|- [ ] Labels use the same openings__color-username span and styling as the username|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-655 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] White button shows username when White is selected; 'Opponents' when Black is selected|- [ ] Black button shows username when Black is selected; 'Opponents' when White is selected|- [ ] Labels use the same openings__color-username span and styling as the username|- [ ] Build passes cleanly"
```

# CCP-656 — Opponent Import: Skip Importing Screen, Show Progress in Tree Build Bar Slot

## Context

Currently when a user clicks "Import Games" in the opponent research details form, the view
switches to a full-page `importing` step (transfer animation + game count + Cancel button)
that replaces the entire body area. After the fetch and tree build complete, a separate `done`
step is shown before the user can reach the board view.

The desired flow:
1. User fills in details (username, source, filters) — **unchanged**
2. User clicks "Import Games" → view **immediately** switches to the opening-tree board view
3. During game fetch: a progress bar appears in the **same slot** where the tree build bar
   lives (`renderTreeBuildBar` position, inside the session panel above the engine section)
4. During tree build: existing `renderTreeBuildBar` takes over seamlessly
5. Import complete → user is already in the board view, no `done` step needed

Research basis: CCP-654 confirmed this is feasible in 2 files with a new `_isFetching` flag.

## Task

### 1. Add fetch-phase state to `src/openings/ctrl.ts`

Add two new pieces of state alongside the existing `_importProgress`:
- `_isFetching: boolean` — true from the moment "Import Games" is clicked until the fetch
  completes (or errors/cancels); false at all other times
- No new progress counter needed — `importProgress()` already tracks game count during fetch

Export getters:
- `isFetching(): boolean`

Update `executeResearchImport` (in `src/openings/import.ts`) to:
- Set `_isFetching = true` right before the fetch starts (after validation)
- Set `_isFetching = false` when the fetch completes, errors, or is cancelled
- Also update `cancelImport()` and `resetImport()` to clear `_isFetching = false`

### 2. Switch to board view on import start in `src/openings/view.ts`

In `executeResearchImport` (called from the Import button click handler), after the existing
validation passes and the fetch is about to begin, call `setActiveTool('opening-tree')` so
the view immediately transitions to the board area.

The simplest hook: since `executeResearchImport` lives in `src/openings/import.ts` and already
calls `setImportStep('importing')`, change `setImportStep('importing')` to instead call
`setActiveTool('opening-tree')` and `setImportStep('idle')` — the import step is no longer
needed to track the fetch phase once the board view owns it.

Alternatively, the click handler in `renderDetailsStep` can call `setActiveTool('opening-tree')`
before delegating to `executeResearchImport`. Either approach is fine; pick whichever is cleaner
after reading the code.

### 3. Render fetch progress bar in `renderOpeningTreeTool` (`src/openings/view.ts`)

At line 1792 (currently `treeBuilding() ? renderTreeBuildBar() : null`), extend the condition:

```
isFetching() ? renderFetchBar() : treeBuilding() ? renderTreeBuildBar() : null
```

Add `renderFetchBar()` as a small private function that reuses the same vnode structure as
`renderTreeBuildBar` but shows fetch-phase content:
- An indeterminate bar (full width, pulsing CSS, or just 100% fill) since total game count
  is not known upfront
- Label: `"Fetching games… N found"` using `importProgress()` for the count (0 = "Connecting…")
- The bar should visually match `renderTreeBuildBar` so the transition is seamless

### 4. Handle the no-collection state in `renderOpeningTreeTool`

During the fetch, `activeCollection()` is null (no collection exists yet). The board and
session panel must still render. Check that `renderOpeningTreeTool` does not crash or blank
when called with `collection = null` — it already accepts `collection: ResearchCollection | null`
so this should be safe, but verify and add any needed null guards.

### 5. Remove `importing` and `done` steps

In `renderImportWorkflow` (`src/openings/view.ts`):
- Remove `step === 'importing' ? renderImportingStep(redraw) : null`
- Remove `step === 'done' ? renderDoneStep(redraw) : null`
- Remove `renderImportingStep` and `renderDoneStep` function bodies

In `executeResearchImport` (`src/openings/import.ts`):
- Remove `setImportStep('importing')` and `setImportStep('done')` calls
- On success: call `resetImport()` (which already sets step back to `idle`) and
  `setActiveTool('opening-tree')` if not already there
- On error: set `_isFetching = false`, keep step at `'details'` so the form is shown with
  the error message (existing `setImportError` behavior)

### 6. Cancel during fetch

`cancelImport()` already aborts the in-flight request and sets step back to `'details'` with
an error message. Add `_isFetching = false` to that path. The Cancel affordance moves: instead
of the full-page `importing` step Cancel button, add a small Cancel link/button in the
`renderFetchBar` vnode (next to the label) that calls `cancelImport(); redraw()`.

## Scope

- `src/openings/ctrl.ts` — add `_isFetching`, `isFetching()`, update `cancelImport()` /
  `resetImport()`
- `src/openings/import.ts` — set/clear `_isFetching`, remove `setImportStep('importing')`
  and `setImportStep('done')`, add `setActiveTool` on success
- `src/openings/view.ts` — add `renderFetchBar()`, update tree-build-bar slot condition,
  remove `renderImportingStep` / `renderDoneStep`, add Cancel to fetch bar

3 files maximum.

## Validation

- [ ] Clicking "Import Games" immediately shows the board view with a "Fetching…" bar at the
  top of the session panel (no full-page importing screen)
- [ ] The bar updates with game count as games stream in
- [ ] Cancel button in the fetch bar aborts the fetch and returns to the details form
- [ ] After fetch completes, tree build bar appears seamlessly in the same slot
- [ ] After tree build completes, user is in the board view with the normal opening tree
- [ ] Import error (bad username, network failure) returns to the details form with the
  error message visible
- [ ] No crash or blank screen when the board view is shown before any collection exists
- [ ] `npm run build` and `npx tsc --noEmit` pass with no errors

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-656
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-656 --checklist "- [ ] Clicking Import Games immediately shows board view with fetch bar (no full-page importing screen)|- [ ] Fetch bar updates game count as games stream in|- [ ] Cancel button in fetch bar aborts and returns to details form|- [ ] Tree build bar appears seamlessly in same slot after fetch|- [ ] Import error returns to details form with error message|- [ ] No crash when board view shown before collection exists|- [ ] npm run build and npx tsc --noEmit pass clean"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-656 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] Clicking Import Games immediately shows board view with fetch bar (no full-page importing screen)|- [ ] Fetch bar updates game count as games stream in|- [ ] Cancel button in fetch bar aborts and returns to details form|- [ ] Tree build bar appears seamlessly in same slot after fetch|- [ ] Import error returns to details form with error message|- [ ] No crash when board view shown before collection exists|- [ ] npm run build and npx tsc --noEmit pass clean"
```
