# CCP-657 — Curated Master Games Dataset: Fischer and Capablanca

## Context

The app needs a static dataset of ~200 iconic chess games by Bobby Fischer and José Raúl
Capablanca. These games will be embedded in the app as a bundled TypeScript module and used
as showcase/sample games wherever a "demo board" is needed — most immediately as the source
for the import animation (CCP-658), but reusable for any future board showcase context.

All games by both players from this era are public domain in the United States.

## Task

### 1. Create the game data file

Create `src/showcase/masterGames.ts`.

Define a `MasterGame` interface:

```typescript
export interface MasterGame {
  id:       string;          // e.g. 'fischer-001'
  white:    string;          // full name
  black:    string;          // full name
  result:   '1-0' | '0-1' | '1/2-1/2';
  year:     number;
  event:    string;          // tournament or match name
  site:     string;          // city / venue if known, else ''
  eco:      string;          // ECO code if known, else ''
  opening:  string;          // opening name if known, else ''
  label:    string;          // one-line human display: "{White} vs {Black}, {Event} {Year}"
  moves:    string[];        // UCI move array, e.g. ['e2e4', 'e7e5', 'g1f3', ...]
}
```

### 2. Curate the games

Include approximately 200 games split roughly evenly between Fischer and Capablanca.

**Fischer selection (~100 games):**
Include his most famous and instructive games. Prioritize:
- Game of the Century (1956, Fischer vs Donald Byrne)
- Match of the Century games vs Spassky (1972 World Championship)
- Fischer vs Reshevsky matches
- US Championship games
- Palma de Mallorca Interzonal 1970 (20/23 score)
- Candidates matches (Taimanov, Larsen, Petrosian)
- Memorable wins in Sicilian, King's Indian, Nimzo-Indian

**Capablanca selection (~100 games):**
- World Championship 1921 (vs Lasker)
- World Championship 1927 (vs Alekhine)
- New York 1924 tournament
- San Sebastian 1911
- London 1922
- Notable endgame masterpieces (Capablanca was renowned for endgames)
- Famous immortal games and sacrifices

### 3. Move encoding

Store moves as UCI strings (e.g. `'e2e4'`, `'g1f3'`, `'e1g1'` for kingside castle).

Use chessops to verify moves are legal before including them:
- Parse starting FEN (standard start position for all these games)
- Walk through each move list using `Chess`, `parseUci`, `makeMove`
- Skip any game where a move is illegal (log to console during generation)

This verification step ensures no corrupt move sequences enter the dataset.

### 4. Module exports

```typescript
export const MASTER_GAMES: MasterGame[] = [ ... ];

/** Return a random game from the full dataset. */
export function randomMasterGame(): MasterGame {
  return MASTER_GAMES[Math.floor(Math.random() * MASTER_GAMES.length)]!;
}

/** Return a random game by a specific player (matched against white or black field). */
export function randomGameByPlayer(name: string): MasterGame | undefined {
  const matches = MASTER_GAMES.filter(
    g => g.white.toLowerCase().includes(name.toLowerCase()) ||
         g.black.toLowerCase().includes(name.toLowerCase()),
  );
  if (matches.length === 0) return undefined;
  return matches[Math.floor(Math.random() * matches.length)]!;
}
```

### 5. File size budget

The move arrays are compact (each UCI string is 4–5 chars). 200 games × ~40 moves average
≈ 8000 strings ≈ ~60–80 KB unminified, well within the existing 1.4 MB bundle budget.

## Scope

- **One new file:** `src/showcase/masterGames.ts`
- Do not modify any existing file.
- Do not create any other files.

## Validation

- [ ] `src/showcase/masterGames.ts` exists and exports `MASTER_GAMES`, `randomMasterGame`,
  `randomGameByPlayer`
- [ ] `MASTER_GAMES.length` is between 180 and 220
- [ ] Games are split roughly evenly between Fischer and Capablanca
- [ ] Each game has a non-empty `label`, `white`, `black`, `year`, `moves`
- [ ] `moves` arrays contain valid UCI strings (4–5 chars each, no empty entries)
- [ ] `npx tsc --noEmit` passes with no errors
- [ ] `npm run build` passes with no errors

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-657
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-657 --checklist "- [ ] masterGames.ts exists with MASTER_GAMES, randomMasterGame, randomGameByPlayer|- [ ] MASTER_GAMES.length is 180–220|- [ ] Fischer and Capablanca represented roughly equally|- [ ] Each game has label, white, black, year, moves populated|- [ ] moves arrays contain valid UCI strings|- [ ] npx tsc --noEmit passes|- [ ] npm run build passes"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-657 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] masterGames.ts exists with MASTER_GAMES, randomMasterGame, randomGameByPlayer|- [ ] MASTER_GAMES.length is 180–220|- [ ] Fischer and Capablanca represented roughly equally|- [ ] Each game has label, white, black, year, moves populated|- [ ] moves arrays contain valid UCI strings|- [ ] npx tsc --noEmit passes|- [ ] npm run build passes"
```

# CCP-658 — Import Animation: Fast Replay of Master Games During Opponent Fetch

## Context

When a user clicks "Import Games" for opponent research, the view immediately switches to
the board (CCP-656). During the fetch, the board currently sits static at the start position
with only a progress bar showing. This prompt adds a fast game-replay animation: the board
plays through moves from the CCP-657 master games dataset while the fetch runs, giving the
user something engaging to watch.

**Dependency:** CCP-657 must be complete first (`src/showcase/masterGames.ts` must exist).

## Task

All changes go in `src/openings/view.ts` only.

### 1. Import the master games module

```typescript
import { randomMasterGame } from '../showcase/masterGames';
import type { MasterGame } from '../showcase/masterGames';
import { Chess } from 'chessops/chess';
import { parseFen } from 'chessops/fen';
import { parseUci } from 'chessops/util';
import { makeFen } from 'chessops/fen';
```

(chessops is already a dependency — check that `Chess`, `parseFen`, `parseUci`, `makeFen`
are not already imported before adding them to avoid duplicate imports.)

### 2. Animation state (module-level)

Add near the other module-level view state:

```typescript
let _animGame:     MasterGame | null = null;  // current game being replayed
let _animMoveIdx:  number = 0;                // index into _animGame.moves
let _animTimer:    ReturnType<typeof setTimeout> | null = null;
let _animPos:      Chess | null = null;       // chessops position tracking
```

### 3. Animation speed constant

```typescript
const ANIM_MOVE_MS = 600; // ms per move — tweak later
```

### 4. Start/stop functions

```typescript
function startImportAnimation(redraw: () => void): void {
  stopImportAnimation();
  _animGame    = randomMasterGame();
  _animMoveIdx = 0;
  // Parse starting position
  const setup = parseFen('rnbqkbnr/pppppppp/8/8/8/8/PPPPPPPP/RNBQKBNR w KQkq - 0 1');
  _animPos = setup.isOk ? Chess.fromSetup(setup.value).unwrap() : null;
  if (!_animPos || !_openingsCg) return;
  // Reset board to start
  _openingsCg.set({
    fen: 'rnbqkbnr/pppppppp/8/8/8/8/PPPPPPPP/RNBQKBNR w KQkq - 0 1',
    lastMove: [] as Key[],
    movable: { color: 'both', free: false, dests: new Map() },
    animation: { enabled: true },
  });
  scheduleNextAnimMove(redraw);
}

function stopImportAnimation(): void {
  if (_animTimer !== null) {
    clearTimeout(_animTimer);
    _animTimer = null;
  }
  _animGame   = null;
  _animMoveIdx = 0;
  _animPos    = null;
}

function scheduleNextAnimMove(redraw: () => void): void {
  _animTimer = setTimeout(() => {
    _animTimer = null;
    if (!isFetching() || !_animGame || !_animPos || !_openingsCg) return;

    const uciStr = _animGame.moves[_animMoveIdx];
    if (!uciStr) {
      // Game ended — pick a new random game and restart
      startImportAnimation(redraw);
      return;
    }

    const move = parseUci(uciStr);
    if (!move || !_animPos.isLegal(move)) {
      // Bad move — skip to next game
      startImportAnimation(redraw);
      return;
    }

    // Apply move and update board
    _animPos.play(move);
    const newFen = makeFen(_animPos.toSetup());
    const from = uciStr.slice(0, 2) as Key;
    const to   = uciStr.slice(2, 4) as Key;
    _openingsCg.set({
      fen:      newFen,
      lastMove: [from, to],
    });

    _animMoveIdx++;
    scheduleNextAnimMove(redraw);
  }, ANIM_MOVE_MS);
}
```

### 5. Wire animation to fetch lifecycle

In `renderFetchBar(redraw)` (added in CCP-656), add a side-effect hook to start the
animation when the bar first renders and the board is available. The cleanest place is
a Snabbdom `hook: { insert }` on the bar's root element — or, simpler: call
`startImportAnimation(redraw)` once when `_animTimer === null && _animGame === null &&
_openingsCg !== undefined` at the top of `renderFetchBar`.

```typescript
function renderFetchBar(redraw: () => void): VNode {
  // Kick off animation the first time this renders (or if it stopped unexpectedly).
  if (_animTimer === null && _animGame === null && _openingsCg) {
    startImportAnimation(redraw);
  }
  // ... rest of existing renderFetchBar vnode ...
}
```

When `isFetching()` becomes false (fetch complete), `stopImportAnimation()` should be
called before the tree build takes over. The right place is inside `syncOpeningsBoard`:
at the top of that function, if `!isFetching()` and `_animGame !== null`, call
`stopImportAnimation()`. This ensures the board is cleanly handed off when
`openCollection` triggers `syncOpeningsBoard` after the fetch.

### 6. Game attribution label

The `renderFetchBar` vnode currently shows `"Fetching games… N found"`. Extend it to
also show the currently-playing game's label below the bar (small, dimmed text):

```
_animGame ? h('span.openings__fetch-label', _animGame.label) : null
```

Example label: `"Fischer vs Spassky, World Championship 1972"`

Add a style for `.openings__fetch-label` in `src/styles/main.scss`:
```scss
.openings__fetch-label {
  font-size: 0.75rem;
  color: #888;
  font-style: italic;
}
```

This is the only SCSS change needed.

## Scope

- `src/openings/view.ts` — animation state, start/stop/schedule functions, renderFetchBar
  extension, syncOpeningsBoard cleanup hook
- `src/styles/main.scss` — one 5-line `.openings__fetch-label` rule

2 files maximum.

## Notes on chessops API

- `Chess.fromSetup(setup).unwrap()` — may throw; wrap in try/catch
- `pos.isLegal(move)` — check before `pos.play(move)`
- `makeFen(pos.toSetup())` — produces standard FEN string
- `parseUci(str)` — returns `Move | undefined`
- Castling UCIs: `'e1g1'` (white kingside), `'e1c1'` (white queenside), etc.

## Validation

- [ ] During opponent import fetch, the board plays through chess moves automatically
- [ ] Each game plays to completion, then a new random game starts
- [ ] The game label (`"Fischer vs Spassky, ..."`) appears below the fetch bar
- [ ] When the fetch completes, the animation stops and the tree build bar takes over cleanly
- [ ] Cancel during animation: animation stops, board resets, form re-appears
- [ ] No console errors during animation (illegal moves handled gracefully)
- [ ] `npx tsc --noEmit` passes
- [ ] `npm run build` passes

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-658
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-658 --checklist "- [ ] Board animates master game moves during fetch|- [ ] New random game starts when current game ends|- [ ] Game label shows below fetch bar|- [ ] Animation stops cleanly when fetch completes|- [ ] Cancel stops animation and returns to form|- [ ] No console errors during animation|- [ ] npx tsc --noEmit passes|- [ ] npm run build passes"
```

If errors or issues were encountered:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-658 --errors "brief description" --checklist "- [ ] Board animates master game moves during fetch|- [ ] New random game starts when current game ends|- [ ] Game label shows below fetch bar|- [ ] Animation stops cleanly when fetch completes|- [ ] Cancel stops animation and returns to form|- [ ] No console errors during animation|- [ ] npx tsc --noEmit passes|- [ ] npm run build passes"
```

# CCP-659 — Fix Opponents Import Cancel and Progress Display

## Context

The Opponents tool shows an import loading overlay with a cancel button when importing a player's
games. Two bugs need to be fixed:

1. **Cancel clears everything** — pressing Cancel currently dismisses the entire overlay AND clears
   all previously imported data. The intended behavior is that Cancel only stops the current import
   in progress; any games already imported should remain.

2. **Missing month in progress display** — the import progress counter currently shows only a game
   number (e.g. "12125 games"). It should also show the month being processed, e.g.
   "12125 games — March 2024", updating as each month's batch is processed.

## Task

Fix both bugs in the opponents import UI. Do not change any other behavior.

### Bug 1: Cancel behavior

- Cancel should stop the ongoing import fetch/processing loop
- Any games already imported before Cancel was pressed must be preserved
- The UI should return to a usable state showing whatever was already loaded
- Do not reset or clear opponent data on cancel

### Bug 2: Month progress label

- While importing, show the month currently being processed alongside the running game count
- Format: `{count} games — {Month} {Year}` (e.g. "12125 games — March 2024")
- Update as each month's batch of games is fetched/processed
- If month info is not available for a given step, fall back to just the game count

## Scope

- Maximum 3 files
- Do not refactor unrelated import logic
- Do not change how games are stored or deduplicated

## Validation

- [ ] Import a player — cancel mid-import — previously loaded games still appear in the tree
- [ ] Import a player — progress label shows "N games — Month YYYY" updating per batch
- [ ] Cancel does not show an error state, just stops cleanly
- [ ] Full import (no cancel) still works correctly end-to-end

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-659
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-659 --checklist "- [ ] Import a player — cancel mid-import — previously loaded games still appear in the tree|- [ ] Import a player — progress label shows 'N games — Month YYYY' updating per batch|- [ ] Cancel does not show an error state, just stops cleanly|- [ ] Full import (no cancel) still works correctly end-to-end"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-659 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Import a player — cancel mid-import — previously loaded games still appear in the tree|- [ ] Import a player — progress label shows 'N games — Month YYYY' updating per batch|- [ ] Cancel does not show an error state, just stops cleanly|- [ ] Full import (no cancel) still works correctly end-to-end"
```

# CCP-660 — Opening Tree Date Range Filter and Speed Filter Fix

## Context

The Opening Tree session view has a speed filter (Time control chips) rendered below the moves
list. Two related improvements are needed:

1. **Speed filter behavior fix** — the current logic is inverted: clicking a chip *removes* that
   speed from the selection. The correct behavior is: default = all speeds active, clicking a chip
   *narrows* to just that speed, additional clicks *add* to the selection, and if all chips end up
   selected again it resets back to the "all" default.

2. **Date range filter** — a new filter row below the speed chips that lets the user filter the
   opening tree by recency. Changing the date range triggers a full tree rebuild (same as the
   speed filter) and the active filter is shown as a badge in every tool panel.

## Task

### Part 1: Fix speed filter click behavior

Current behavior: clicking a chip removes it from the active set (deselect logic).
Required behavior:
- Default state: all speeds active (empty set = all, same shorthand as today)
- Clicking a chip when all are active: narrows to that chip only
- Clicking a chip when some are active: adds it to the active set
- If all chips end up selected: collapse back to the empty "all" shorthand
- Clicking the only active chip: do not leave an empty set — stay on that chip (or snap back to all)

### Part 2: Add date range filter row

**Location**: new row directly below the speed filter chips, inside the same opening tree panel.

**Default state**: no filter active.  
**Default button label**: `Date Range: All (N)` where N = total games in the collection for the
currently selected colour (ignoring speed filter).

**Options** (shown in a small popup on button click, same popup style as other filter dropdowns):
- Last Week
- Last Month
- Last 3 Months
- Last 6 Months

Each option shows a raw game count for that date window, computed from the full collection for the
selected colour — **ignoring** any active speed filter. These are "available games" counts, not
post-filter counts.

**Active state button label**: `{Range} ({N})` e.g. `Last Month (45)`.  
An × button appears inline to clear back to "All".

**Popup behaviour**: opens on button click, dismisses on click-outside or option select.

**Tree rebuild**: selecting or clearing the date range filter must trigger a full tree rebuild
identical to how `setSpeedFilter` works — node counts, percentages, and move statistics must all
reflect only games within the selected date window.

**Scope**: the date range filter affects all tools in the session — Opening Tree, Prep Report,
Style, and Deviation Scan all read from the rebuilt tree.

**Filter badge**: when a non-default date range is active, a small dismissible pill (e.g.
`Last 3 Months ×`) must appear at the top of every tool panel (Opening Tree, Prep Report, Style,
Deviation Scan). Clicking × clears the filter and triggers a tree rebuild back to all-time.

## Scope

- Maximum 3 files
- Touch only `src/openings/ctrl.ts`, `src/openings/view.ts`, and optionally `src/styles/main.scss`
  for any required new CSS classes
- Do not change how games are stored or the tree builder itself
- Do not refactor unrelated filter logic

## Validation

- [ ] Speed filter: clicking a chip when all are active narrows to that chip only
- [ ] Speed filter: clicking additional chips adds them to the active set
- [ ] Speed filter: when all chips are re-selected, resets to "all" default
- [ ] Speed filter: clicking the only active chip does not leave an empty set
- [ ] Date range button shows `Date Range: All (N)` by default with correct total count
- [ ] Date range popup opens on click, dismisses on click-outside or option select
- [ ] Each popup option shows raw game count for that date window (colour-filtered, speed-ignored)
- [ ] Selecting a date range triggers a full tree rebuild — move counts update
- [ ] Active date range button shows `{Range} (N)` with correct post-date-filter count
- [ ] × on active button clears the filter and rebuilds back to all-time
- [ ] Filter badge appears at top of each tool panel when a date range is active
- [ ] Filter badge × clears the filter and triggers rebuild
- [ ] Prep Report and Style data reflect the active date range
- [ ] Full import + all filter combinations work correctly end-to-end

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-660
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-660 --checklist "- [ ] Speed filter: clicking a chip when all are active narrows to that chip only|- [ ] Speed filter: clicking additional chips adds them to the active set|- [ ] Speed filter: when all chips are re-selected, resets to 'all' default|- [ ] Speed filter: clicking the only active chip does not leave an empty set|- [ ] Date range button shows 'Date Range: All (N)' by default with correct total count|- [ ] Date range popup opens on click, dismisses on click-outside or option select|- [ ] Each popup option shows raw game count for that date window (colour-filtered, speed-ignored)|- [ ] Selecting a date range triggers a full tree rebuild — move counts update|- [ ] Active date range button shows '{Range} (N)' with correct post-date-filter count|- [ ] × on active button clears the filter and rebuilds back to all-time|- [ ] Filter badge appears at top of each tool panel when a date range is active|- [ ] Filter badge × clears the filter and triggers rebuild|- [ ] Prep Report and Style data reflect the active date range|- [ ] Full import + all filter combinations work correctly end-to-end"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:

# CCP-661 — Audit Sprint Workflow For Claude Usability

## Summary

Deeply audit the current sprint workflow, sprint tracking process, sprint dashboard usage model,
and prompt-to-sprint linkage process from the perspective of Claude Code as the executing agent.

The goal is not to validate the process politely. The goal is to identify anything that is still
confusing, brittle, overly manual, easy for Claude to misread, likely to drift, or likely to fail
in real execution.

Assume Codex wants blunt, implementation-relevant feedback so the process can be hardened.

## What To Review

Review the current sprint workflow using the actual repo state, including at minimum:

- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/prompts/PROMPT_REGISTRY_README.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/prompts/PROMPT_CREATION_PROCESS.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/prompts/PROMPT_USER_GUIDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/mini-sprints/SPRINT_REGISTRY_README.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/mini-sprints/SPRINT_CREATION_PROCESS.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/mini-sprints/SPRINT_PROGRESS_PROCESS.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/mini-sprints/SPRINT_AUDIT_PROCESS.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/mini-sprints/SPRINT_USER_GUIDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/mini-sprints/sprint-registry.json`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/prompts/prompt-registry.json`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

Also inspect the relevant tooling/scripts that actually enforce or mutate sprint truth:

- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/sprint-registry-lib.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/sprint-create.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/sprint-backfill.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/sprint-audit-complete.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/prompt-create.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/prompt-complete.mjs`

## What To Evaluate

Evaluate the sprint process specifically for Claude usability and reliability:

1. Can Claude reliably understand how to create a new sprint that is valid from day one?
2. Can Claude reliably understand how to create sprint-linked prompts without drifting?
3. Can Claude reliably understand how sprint progress updates from prompt lifecycle events?
4. Can Claude reliably understand when to run a sprint audit and what it must update?
5. Can Claude tell which docs are canonical vs supporting vs user-facing?
6. Are there places where the docs and tooling still disagree?
7. Are there places where the workflow is technically correct but too easy for Claude to misuse?
8. Are there places where the dashboard semantics could mislead Claude during execution or audit work?
9. Are there places where the process is too manual and should be automated to reduce agent error?
10. If a new Claude session were asked to:
   - create a sprint
   - create prompts for a sprint
   - audit a sprint
   - update the sprint workflow
   would it likely succeed cleanly, or where would it stumble?

## Important Constraints

- Do not make code or doc changes in this task.
- This is a review/audit prompt only.
- Be critical and specific.
- Prefer concrete process risks over generic suggestions.
- If something seems weak, say so directly.
- If something is good and should be preserved, say that too.
- Focus especially on Claude execution failure modes, ambiguity, and drift risk.

## Required Output Format

Return the result in this exact structure:

### 1. Overall Verdict
- one short paragraph
- answer whether the sprint process is currently strong enough for Claude to use safely
- say what level of confidence you have

### 2. Findings
- findings first, ordered by severity
- each finding must include:
  - severity: `high`, `medium`, or `low`
  - exact file path references
  - the concrete failure mode
  - why this is a real risk for Claude specifically

### 3. Workflow Gaps Or Ambiguities
- list any places where the process is unclear, duplicated, underspecified, or too manual

### 4. What Is Already Good
- list the parts of the current sprint process that are well-designed and should be preserved

### 5. Recommended Hardening Changes
- give the smallest high-leverage improvements Codex should make next
- prioritize by impact on Claude reliability, not by niceness

### 6. Best Prompting Advice For The User
- tell the user the best exact ways to ask Claude to:
  - create a sprint
  - create prompts for a sprint
  - audit a sprint
  - update the sprint workflow

## Special Request

Think deeply about this as a working agent, not as a passive reviewer.

I want feedback that helps Codex make the sprint process rock solid for Claude in real use.
Assume the output will be copied back into Codex for action.

## Validation

- Confirm whether the sprint creation path is decision-complete for Claude.
- Confirm whether the sprint audit path is decision-complete for Claude.
- Confirm whether prompt-to-sprint linkage is clear enough to avoid accidental drift.
- Confirm whether the dashboard semantics align with the documented sprint states.
- Confirm whether any current docs or scripts are still likely to mislead Claude.

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-661
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-661 --checklist "- [ ] Reviewed the canonical sprint and prompt workflow docs|- [ ] Reviewed the sprint and prompt tooling that enforces the workflow|- [ ] Produced findings-first feedback with exact file references|- [ ] Included concrete hardening recommendations for Codex"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh

```

# CCP-662 — Sprint card grid visual polish

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

Prompt ID: CCP-662
Task ID: CCP-662
Source Document: ad hoc user request
Execution Target: Claude Code

## Context

The sprint card grid in `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` (generated by
`scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`) currently shows:

- Title + status badge header
- Three metric bars: Plan / Execution / Implementation
- Segmented bar showing task state distribution
- Completion summary text
- Pills: task count, linked count, unreviewed count, audits, next steps, next phase, normalization warning, dependencies

Several issues have been identified:

1. The segmented bar and the three metric bars are redundant — both derived from the same task state data.
2. "Plan" is a misleading label — it actually means % of tasks with a linked prompt (prompt coverage).
3. The pills row is cluttered — `X tasks` and `X linked` add little on top of the bars.
4. `needs normalization` is buried at low contrast in the pills — it is a warning state and deserves visual prominence.
5. There is no age/recency signal on the card.
6. No left-border status colour for fast Kanban-style scanning.
7. Unreviewed prompt count looks the same as neutral pills even when it signals action needed.

## Goal

Polish the sprint card grid in `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs` to be
more scannable and informative without adding clutter.

## Changes required

### 1. Remove the segmented bar

Drop the `<div class="progress-bar">` + `progressSegments` call from each card.
Keep only the three metric bars. The segmented bar is redundant with the metrics.

### 2. Rename "Plan" label to "Prompts"

Change the label on the first metric bar from `Plan` to `Prompts` to accurately
reflect that it measures prompt-coverage, not planning completeness.

### 3. Left border status colour

Add a coloured left border to each `.sprint-card` that maps to the sprint status.
Use the same colour palette as the existing `.sprint-status--*` badges:
- Needs Prompts / Incomplete Start State → amber (`#7a6940`)
- Ready to Start → purple (`#7b62b6`)
- In Progress → blue (`#416b82`)
- Completed Needs Full Review / Completed: With Issues → orange-brown (`#7e6536`)
- Completed: Reviews Passed / Completed: Review Passed / Completed → green (`#3c6a4b`)

Implement as inline `border-left: 3px solid <colour>` on the card element,
or via a CSS class `sprint-card--status-<slug>` with matching rules.

### 4. Warning card tint when normalization required

When `sprint.showNormalizationWarning` is true, add a class (e.g.
`sprint-card--warn`) to the card that applies a subtle amber background tint
and amber border colour, overriding the status border. This makes the card
visually distinct from a healthy card at a glance.

### 5. Unreviewed count as warning badge

When `sprint.promptsUnreviewedCount > 0`, render it as an amber/orange
warning pill (new class e.g. `sprint-pill--warn`) instead of the default
neutral pill. Apply a distinct colour so it reads as "action needed."

### 6. Trim the neutral pills

Remove `X tasks` and `X linked` from the pills row — this information is
already conveyed by the progress bars. Keep:
- Unreviewed count (as warning badge when > 0)
- Audit count (if any)
- Next steps count (if any)
- Next phase name (if any)
- Dependency pills

### 7. Relative date footer

Add a small "Updated X days ago" (or "today" / "yesterday") line at the
bottom of each card using `sprint.updatedAt`. Keep it at the lowest visual
weight (e.g. `font-size: 0.7rem; color: var(--text-dim); opacity: 0.7`).

Use a client-side function — do not recompute in the generator.

## Hard constraints

- Do not change sprint registry logic or computed fields
- Do not change the sprint detail overlay
- Do not change the prompt tab or prompt cards
- Scope all changes to the sprint card CSS and the `renderSprints` function

## Validation

Run:
```sh
npm run dashboard:generate
```

Open `docs/prompts/dashboard.html`, navigate to the Sprints tab, and verify:
- Cards have a coloured left border that matches their status
- Normalization-warning cards have an amber tint
- The segmented bar is gone — only three metric bars remain
- First bar is labelled "Prompts" not "Plan"
- Unreviewed count appears in amber/orange when > 0
- `X tasks` and `X linked` pills are gone
- Each card shows a relative "Updated X ago" footer
- Sprint detail and prompt tab still work

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-662
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-662 --checklist "- [ ] Cards have a coloured left border matching their status|- [ ] Normalization-warning cards have an amber tint|- [ ] Segmented bar removed, only three metric bars remain|- [ ] First bar labelled Prompts not Plan|- [ ] Unreviewed count shown as amber warning badge when > 0|- [ ] X tasks and X linked pills removed|- [ ] Each card shows a relative Updated X ago footer|- [ ] Sprint detail and prompt tab still work"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-662 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Cards have a coloured left border matching their status|- [ ] Normalization-warning cards have an amber tint|- [ ] Segmented bar removed, only three metric bars remain|- [ ] First bar labelled Prompts not Plan|- [ ] Unreviewed count shown as amber warning badge when > 0|- [ ] X tasks and X linked pills removed|- [ ] Each card shows a relative Updated X ago footer|- [ ] Sprint detail and prompt tab still work"
```

# CCP-389-F1 — Implement Post-Game Summary Panel

## Context

CCP-389 was reviewed and passed but the implementation was never committed.
`renderPostGameSummaryPanel()` in `src/analyse/evalView.ts` (line 257) is a stub
that returns an empty `h('div')`. The call site in `src/main.ts` (line 1039) already
passes all required arguments. This prompt fills in the actual implementation.

Sprint: SPR-004 (Improvement Intelligence Platform), Task 12 (Post-game summary panel).

## Lichess Reference

Study `ui/analyse/src/view/` for how Lichess renders post-analysis summary information.
The panel should follow the same visual weight and placement conventions as other
underboard content in Patzer Pro (eval graph, game list).

## Task

Implement `renderPostGameSummaryPanel()` in `src/analyse/evalView.ts` so that after
batch analysis completes, a collapsible summary panel appears below the eval graph.

### Content to render

Using the existing function signature parameters (`analysisComplete`, `evalCache`,
`mainline`, `whiteName`, `blackName`, `userColor`, `navigate`, `redraw`):

1. **Accuracy score** — compute from evalCache using the existing `computeAnalysisSummary()`
   function already in the same file
2. **Classification breakdown** — "X good, Y inaccuracies, Z mistakes, W blunders"
   (from `computeAnalysisSummary()` counts)
3. **Worst move** — use the existing `findWorstMove()` helper in the same file to find
   the worst single-move loss; render as "Move N lost X% win chance" with a clickable
   link that calls `navigate(path)` to jump to that position
4. **Opening assessment** — show the opening name from the first few mainline nodes
   (ECO header or first book moves)
5. **Missed moments count** — count nodes where eval swings significantly; link text
   "Learn from your mistakes" (no navigation wiring needed yet — just the text)

### Behavior

- Panel only renders when `analysisComplete` is true; otherwise return empty `h('div')`
- Collapsible: default expanded on first view
- Collapse state stored in `localStorage` key `patzer.postGameSummary.collapsed`
- Worst-move link calls `navigate(path)` to jump to that position in the analysis board

### Files to modify

- `src/analyse/evalView.ts` — replace the stub body of `renderPostGameSummaryPanel()`

This is a 1-file change. Do not modify the function signature or the call site in main.ts.

### Styling

Use existing Patzer CSS class conventions. Add minimal CSS in `public/css/main.css` if
needed for the collapsible panel — keep it consistent with existing underboard styling.
That makes it a 2-file change maximum.

## Validation

- [ ] `renderPostGameSummaryPanel()` returns real content when `analysisComplete` is true
- [ ] Panel shows accuracy score, classification breakdown, and worst move
- [ ] Worst-move link navigates to the correct position via `navigate(path)`
- [ ] Panel is collapsible with persisted state in localStorage
- [ ] Panel returns empty div when `analysisComplete` is false
- [ ] No changes to the function signature or main.ts call site
- [ ] `npm run build` succeeds with no type errors

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-389-F1
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-389-F1 --checklist "- [ ] renderPostGameSummaryPanel returns real content when analysisComplete is true|- [ ] Panel shows accuracy score, classification breakdown, and worst move|- [ ] Worst-move link navigates to correct position via navigate(path)|- [ ] Panel is collapsible with persisted state in localStorage|- [ ] Panel returns empty div when analysisComplete is false|- [ ] No changes to function signature or main.ts call site|- [ ] npm run build succeeds with no type errors"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-389-F1 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] renderPostGameSummaryPanel returns real content when analysisComplete is true|- [ ] Panel shows accuracy score, classification breakdown, and worst move|- [ ] Worst-move link navigates to correct position via navigate(path)|- [ ] Panel is collapsible with persisted state in localStorage|- [ ] Panel returns empty div when analysisComplete is false|- [ ] No changes to function signature or main.ts call site|- [ ] npm run build succeeds with no type errors"
```

# CCP-389-F2 — Hide Post Game Summary Panel

## Task

The post game summary panel implemented in CCP-389-F1 (`renderPostGameSummaryPanel` in
`src/analyse/evalView.ts`) is not ready to be shown. Hide it temporarily by returning an
empty `h('div')` from `renderPostGameSummaryPanel` regardless of `analysisComplete` state.

Do not delete the implementation — just short-circuit the render so the panel is invisible
until it is ready to be re-enabled.

Also remove the corresponding `div.post-game-panel` mount point from `src/main.ts` if it
renders unconditionally, OR ensure it renders nothing when `renderPostGameSummaryPanel`
returns an empty vnode.

## Scope

- `src/analyse/evalView.ts` — short-circuit `renderPostGameSummaryPanel` to return `h('div')`
- `src/main.ts` — verify nothing blows up when the panel returns an empty vnode (no other changes)

Maximum 2 files.

- [ ] No console errors related to the panel

# CCP-385-F1 — Complete Weakness Dashboard Panel

## Sprint Linkage

- Sprint: SPR-004 — Mini Sprint — Improvement Intelligence Platform
- Phase: SPR-004-P1 — Tasks
- Task: SPR-004-T07 — Weakness dashboard panel
- Source doc: `docs/mini-sprints/IMPROVEMENT_INTELLIGENCE_PLATFORM_SPRINT_2026-03-29.md`
- Fixes: CCP-385 (Add Stats Weakness Panel)

## Context

CCP-385 implemented `src/stats/view.ts` but the weakness panel section contains placeholder sections rather than fully rendered weakness cards. The `stats/weakness.ts` aggregation engine (CCP-384) is complete and produces `DiagnosedWeakness[]` records. The task is to replace the placeholders with real rendered output.

## What CCP-385 Left Incomplete

`src/stats/view.ts` has section stubs for the weakness panel but does not render:
- Individual weakness cards with severity indicators
- Description and sample-size text per weakness
- Training action buttons/links

## What This Prompt Must Build

In `src/stats/view.ts`, replace the weakness panel placeholder with a fully rendered panel:

**Layout per the sprint spec (Task 7)**:
- Header: "Your Weaknesses" (or "Areas for Improvement")
- If fewer than 10 analyzed games: render "Analyze more games to see your weakness profile" with current game count
- Otherwise: for each `DiagnosedWeakness` (up to 5):
  - Severity indicator — apply a CSS class or inline marker reflecting `severity: 'critical' | 'significant' | 'moderate'`
  - Category label
  - Description text (`weakness.description`)
  - Sample-size context ("Based on N analyzed games")
  - Training action link/button if `weakness.trainingAction` is present ("Train this →")
- Maximum 5 cards; do not render more

**Training action navigation**:
- `type: 'puzzles'` → navigate to `/puzzles` (with `target` as filter if applicable)
- `type: 'retro'` → navigate to the relevant game in `/analysis`
- `type: 'openings'` → navigate to `/openings`
- `type: 'review'` → navigate to `/analysis` with relevant game loaded

**Rules**:
- Snabbdom `h()` only — no direct DOM manipulation
- Follow existing `stats/view.ts` rendering patterns
- No new design system, no chart library
- Touch only `src/stats/view.ts`

## Validation

- Stats page shows real weakness cards (not placeholder text) when 10+ games are analyzed
- Severity class applied to each card (verify via DOM inspection or CSS class presence)
- Insufficient-data state renders the "analyze more games" message with count
- Training action buttons present on cards that have `trainingAction` set
- Navigation from training action reaches the correct route

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh

```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh

```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-385-F1 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Stats page shows real weakness cards (not placeholder) with 10+ analyzed games|- [ ] Severity indicator class applied to each card|- [ ] 'Analyze more games' message renders with count when < 10 games|- [ ] Training action button present on cards with trainingAction set|- [ ] Training action navigates to correct route"
```

# CCP-386-F1 — Complete Accuracy and Blunder Trend Charts

## Sprint Linkage

- Sprint: SPR-004 — Mini Sprint — Improvement Intelligence Platform
- Phase: SPR-004-P1 — Tasks
- Task: SPR-004-T08 — Accuracy and blunder trends
- Source doc: `docs/mini-sprints/IMPROVEMENT_INTELLIGENCE_PLATFORM_SPRINT_2026-03-29.md`
- Fixes: CCP-386 (Add Accuracy And Blunder Trend Charts)

## Context

CCP-386 added an SVG polyline chart to `src/stats/view.ts` but the result is minimal — it exists structurally but does not implement the full spec from Task 8 of the sprint. The `GameSummary` records (Tasks 2–5, all complete) contain `accuracy`, `blunderCount`, `timeClass`, and `date` fields needed to drive these charts.

## What CCP-386 Left Incomplete

The existing SVG polyline chart in `src/stats/view.ts`:
- Does not implement the 20-game rolling moving average
- Does not separate lines per time control
- Does not enforce the minimum 20-game threshold with a "Need N more games" message
- May not show both accuracy and blunder rate charts

## What This Prompt Must Build

In `src/stats/view.ts`, replace or complete the trend chart section per sprint Task 8:

**Charts required**:
1. Accuracy moving average — 20-game rolling window
2. Blunder rate moving average — 20-game rolling window (blunders per game)

**Per the sprint spec**:
- Minimum 20 games before showing trends; below that show "Need N more games for trends"
- Separate lines per time control (`timeClass`) — do not mix blitz and rapid on one line
- X-axis: game date (chronological); Y-axis: metric value
- Always show sample size in the chart label or caption
- Use inline SVG rendering — no chart library — following the eval graph pattern in `src/analyse/evalView.ts`

**Moving average algorithm**:
- Sort `GameSummary[]` by `date` ascending
- For each time control group, compute a 20-game rolling average over the sorted games
- Each data point on the chart represents the average of the preceding 20 games (or all available if fewer)

**Rules**:
- Snabbdom `h()` for the surrounding container; inline SVG for chart drawing
- Touch only `src/stats/view.ts`
- No chart library dependency

## Validation

- Accuracy trend chart renders with real `GameSummary` data
- Blunder rate trend chart renders separately
- Each time control (blitz, rapid, etc.) is drawn as a separate line with a label
- "Need N more games" message shown when fewer than 20 total games
- Sample size displayed alongside each chart

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-386-F1
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-386-F1 --checklist "- [ ] Accuracy moving average chart renders with real data|- [ ] Blunder rate moving average chart renders with real data|- [ ] Time controls are separated into distinct lines|- [ ] 'Need N more games' message shown below 20-game threshold|- [ ] Sample size displayed per chart|- [ ] No chart library imported"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-386-F1 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Accuracy moving average chart renders with real data|- [ ] Blunder rate moving average chart renders with real data|- [ ] Time controls are separated into distinct lines|- [ ] 'Need N more games' message shown below 20-game threshold|- [ ] Sample size displayed per chart|- [ ] No chart library imported"
```

# CCP-387-F1 — Add Opening Win Rate Table from Import Data

## Sprint Linkage

- Sprint: SPR-004 — Mini Sprint — Improvement Intelligence Platform
- Phase: SPR-004-P1 — Tasks
- Task: SPR-004-T09 — Opening win rate from import data
- Source doc: `docs/mini-sprints/IMPROVEMENT_INTELLIGENCE_PLATFORM_SPRINT_2026-03-29.md`
- Fixes: CCP-387 (Add Opening Performance Table)

## Context

CCP-387 was linked to the opening performance table task but audit evidence shows no opening-specific stats view exists in `src/stats/view.ts`. This follow-up covers Task 9a only — the surface-level opening win rate table built entirely from raw imported game data, with no dependency on analysis (no `GameSummary` required).

Every imported game already carries `result`, `white`, `black`, `eco`, and `opening` fields. No Stockfish review is needed for this table.

## What This Prompt Must Build

In `src/stats/view.ts`, add an opening win rate table section:

**Columns**: Opening name (ECO code as fallback if opening name missing), color played (White / Black), games, W / D / L counts, win rate %

**Rules (from sprint Task 9a)**:
- Aggregate by `opening` field; fall back to `eco` if opening name is missing
- Filter: only show openings with 5+ games played as that color — openings below threshold are hidden
- Separate rows for White and Black (same opening played as both colors = two rows)
- Default sort: worst win rate first
- Highlight rows where win rate is more than 15 percentage points below the user's overall win rate for that color
- Hide the entire table when fewer than 3 qualifying openings (5+ games each)
- Show a subtle "Analyzed data available" indicator on rows that also have `GameSummary` records — this is a future hook for CCP-387-F2 to upgrade those rows with accuracy/blunder columns

**Data source**: the imported games array already in memory — same source the Opening Tree uses. No dependency on `GameSummary` or the analysis pipeline. Table must render immediately after import, before any games are reviewed.

**Rules**:
- Snabbdom `h()` only
- Touch only `src/stats/view.ts`
- No new design system

## Validation

- Opening win rate table renders after game import, before any games are reviewed
- Rows with < 5 games for that opening+color are excluded
- Table hidden when fewer than 3 qualifying opening+color rows
- Rows sorted worst win rate first
- Rows more than 15 points below color average are visually highlighted

# CCP-387-F2 — Extend Opening Table with Analysis Columns

## Sprint Linkage

- Sprint: SPR-004 — Mini Sprint — Improvement Intelligence Platform
- Phase: SPR-004-P1 — Tasks
- Task: SPR-004-T10 — Opening performance table (full)
- Source doc: `docs/mini-sprints/IMPROVEMENT_INTELLIGENCE_PLATFORM_SPRINT_2026-03-29.md`
- Fixes: CCP-387-F1 (Add Opening Win Rate Table from Import Data)

## Context

CCP-387-F1 (Task 9a) builds the surface-level opening win rate table using only imported game data. This follow-up (Task 9 full) extends that table with accuracy and blunder columns sourced from `GameSummary` records. Rows without analysis show win-rate-only columns; rows with `GameSummary` data show the full column set.

**Dependency**: CCP-387-F1 must be complete before this prompt runs. The "Analyzed data available" indicator from CCP-387-F1 is the upgrade hook this prompt uses.

## What This Prompt Must Build

In `src/stats/view.ts`, upgrade the opening table built by CCP-387-F1:

**Extended columns (added when `GameSummary` records exist for an opening)**:
- Average accuracy
- Average blunders per game

**Rules (from sprint Task 9)**:
- Rows with no `GameSummary` records show only the import-data columns (games, W/D/L, win rate) — these rows already exist from CCP-387-F1
- Rows with 5+ analyzed games show the full column set including accuracy and blunders
- Highlight openings where average accuracy is more than 5 points below the user's overall average accuracy
- Default sort: worst-performing first — prioritize lowest win rate, or lowest accuracy where analysis data exists
- Collapse to top 10 rows with "Show all" toggle if many openings qualify

**Data joining**:
- Join the imported games aggregation (from CCP-387-F1) with `GameSummary` records by `opening`/`eco` field
- A row needs 5+ analyzed games (games with a `GameSummary`) to show accuracy and blunder columns

**Rules**:
- Snabbdom `h()` only
- Touch only `src/stats/view.ts`
- No new design system

## Validation

- Rows with no analyzed games show only win-rate columns
- Rows with 5+ analyzed games show accuracy and average blunders
- Accuracy highlight applied to openings more than 5 points below user average
- "Show all" toggle works when more than 10 rows qualify
- Sort order puts worst-performing openings first

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-387-F2
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-387-F2 --checklist "- [ ] Rows without analysis show win-rate columns only|- [ ] Rows with 5+ analyzed games show accuracy and blunders columns|- [ ] Below-average accuracy rows highlighted|- [ ] 'Show all' toggle present when more than 10 rows qualify|- [ ] Sort order is worst-performing first"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-387-F2 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Rows without analysis show win-rate columns only|- [ ] Rows with 5+ analyzed games show accuracy and blunders columns|- [ ] Below-average accuracy rows highlighted|- [ ] 'Show all' toggle present when more than 10 rows qualify|- [ ] Sort order is worst-performing first"
```

# CCP-388-F1 — Add Tactical Profile Section to Stats Page

## Sprint Linkage

- Sprint: SPR-004 — Mini Sprint — Improvement Intelligence Platform
- Phase: SPR-004-P1 — Tasks
- Task: SPR-004-T11 — Tactical profile
- Source doc: `docs/mini-sprints/IMPROVEMENT_INTELLIGENCE_PLATFORM_SPRINT_2026-03-29.md`
- Fixes: CCP-388 (Add Tactical Profile Section)

## Context

CCP-388 was linked to the tactical profile task but audit evidence shows no tactical breakdown view exists in `src/stats/view.ts`. The `GameSummary` type (`src/stats/types.ts`) includes `missedMomentCount` per game. Task 10 from the sprint spec requires surfacing a tactical profile panel on the stats page.

**Note on missed-moment type breakdown**: Check whether `GameSummary` stores missed-moment type breakdown (swing, collapse, missed mate separately). If it does not, and if adding those fields to the type and extraction logic would touch more than 3 files, implement the panel using only the aggregate `missedMomentCount` field and note the type breakdown as a follow-up item.

## What This Prompt Must Build

In `src/stats/view.ts`, add a tactical profile section per sprint Task 10:

**Content**:
- Missed moments per game (average across all analyzed games: `sum(missedMomentCount) / gameCount`)
- Total missed moments across all analyzed games
- If `GameSummary` has type breakdown fields (swing / collapse / missed mate counts): show per-type breakdown as a sub-list
- If trend data is sufficient (20+ games): show missed-moment rate trend using the same moving-average SVG approach as the trend charts (Task 8 / CCP-386-F1). If CCP-386-F1 has extracted a reusable chart helper, use it.

**Minimum sample**: require 10+ analyzed games before showing this section; hide entirely if below threshold — no empty placeholder.

**Rules**:
- Snabbdom `h()` only
- Touch only `src/stats/view.ts` (plus `src/stats/types.ts` if type breakdown fields already exist but are not used, or if a trivially small addition enables the breakdown)
- Do not add extraction logic if it requires modifying `src/stats/extract.ts` or `src/engine/batch.ts` — note that as a separate follow-up instead
- No chart library

## Validation

- Tactical profile section renders on stats page when 10+ games are analyzed
- Shows average missed moments per game
- Shows total missed moments
- Type breakdown shown if fields exist in `GameSummary`
- Missed-moment rate trend shown when 20+ games are available
- Section completely hidden (not even an empty container) when fewer than 10 games

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh

```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:


If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh

```

# CCP-391-F1 — Add Training Recommendation UI to Weakness Cards

## Sprint Linkage

- Sprint: SPR-004 — Mini Sprint — Improvement Intelligence Platform
- Phase: SPR-004-P1 — Tasks
- Task: SPR-004-T14 — Training recommendations
- Source doc: `docs/mini-sprints/IMPROVEMENT_INTELLIGENCE_PLATFORM_SPRINT_2026-03-29.md`
- Fixes: CCP-391 (Add Weakness Training Actions)

## Context

CCP-391 was linked to the training recommendations task. Audit evidence shows that `src/stats/weakness.ts` produces `DiagnosedWeakness` records with a `trainingAction` field, but no UI surfaces these actions — the weakness cards (being completed by CCP-385-F1) render the card structure but the "Train this" action buttons are not wired to navigate anywhere useful.

**Dependency**: CCP-385-F1 (weakness cards) should be complete before this prompt runs, as this prompt wires the action buttons that CCP-385-F1 renders.

## What This Prompt Must Build

In `src/stats/view.ts`, wire each weakness card's training action button to navigate to the correct Patzer tool with appropriate context, per sprint Task 13:

**Action mapping**:

| `trainingAction.type` | `trainingAction.target` | Navigation |
|---|---|---|
| `'puzzles'` | puzzle theme string (optional) | Navigate to `/puzzles` — pass `target` as a theme filter if present |
| `'retro'` | game ID (optional) | Navigate to `/analysis` with the specified game loaded, open retro mode |
| `'openings'` | opening name (optional) | Navigate to `/openings` — highlight the specified opening if possible |
| `'review'` | game ID (optional) | Navigate to `/analysis` with the specified game loaded |

**Rules**:
- Use the app's existing navigation mechanism (match the pattern used in other `view.ts` files for inter-tool navigation)
- If no `trainingAction` is set on a weakness, the card shows no action button (this is the existing CCP-385-F1 behavior; do not change it)
- Each action button label comes from `trainingAction.label` — use it verbatim
- Snabbdom `h()` only
- Touch only `src/stats/view.ts`

## Validation

- "Train this" button on a tactical blindness weakness navigates to `/puzzles`
- "Train this" button on a blunder rate weakness navigates to `/analysis` with retro context
- "Train this" button on an opening weakness navigates to `/openings`
- "Train this" button on a conversion failure weakness navigates to `/analysis` with the game loaded
- Button label matches `trainingAction.label` from the weakness record
- Cards without `trainingAction` show no button

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-391-F1
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh

```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-391-F1 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Puzzles training action navigates to /puzzles|- [ ] Retro training action navigates to /analysis with game loaded in retro mode|- [ ] Openings training action navigates to /openings|- [ ] Review training action navigates to /analysis with game loaded|- [ ] Button label matches trainingAction.label|- [ ] Cards without trainingAction show no button"
```

# CCP-392-F1 — Add Time Management Profile Section to Stats Page

## Sprint Linkage

- Sprint: SPR-004 — Mini Sprint — Improvement Intelligence Platform
- Phase: SPR-004-P1 — Tasks
- Task: SPR-004-T15 — Time management profile
- Source doc: `docs/mini-sprints/IMPROVEMENT_INTELLIGENCE_PLATFORM_SPRINT_2026-03-29.md`
- Fixes: CCP-392 (Add Time Management Profile Section)

## Context

CCP-392 was linked to the time management profile task. Audit evidence shows that clock data is captured in `GameSummary` (`hasClockData`, `avgTimePerMove`, `timeTroubleMoves` fields from `src/stats/types.ts`) but no time management stats view exists in `src/stats/view.ts`.

## What This Prompt Must Build

In `src/stats/view.ts`, add a time management profile section per sprint Task 14:

**Content** (only shown when 10+ games have `hasClockData: true`):
- Average time per move (mean of `avgTimePerMove` across games with clock data)
- Time-trouble frequency: percentage of analyzed games where `timeTroubleMoves > 0`
- Blunder-in-time-trouble correlation: compare blunder rate in time-trouble games vs. non-time-trouble games
  - "X% of your blunders happen in time trouble" — compute: sum of blunders from time-trouble games / total blunders
- Simple visual element — a short sentence or small indicator showing the correlation clearly

**Show/hide rule**:
- Section is completely hidden (no container, no placeholder) when fewer than 10 `GameSummary` records have `hasClockData: true`
- When shown, always display how many games the data is based on

**Rules**:
- Snabbdom `h()` only
- Touch only `src/stats/view.ts`
- No chart library
- No new design system

## Validation

- Time management section renders when 10+ games have clock data
- Average time per move displayed
- Time-trouble frequency percentage displayed
- "X% of blunders in time trouble" stat displayed
- Section completely absent (no DOM element) when fewer than 10 clock-data games
- Sample size (number of games with clock data) shown

## Lifecycle


# CCP-393-F1 — Add Conversion and Resourcefulness Metrics to Stats Page

## Sprint Linkage

- Sprint: SPR-004 — Mini Sprint — Improvement Intelligence Platform
- Phase: SPR-004-P1 — Tasks
- Task: SPR-004-T16 — Conversion and resourcefulness
- Source doc: `docs/mini-sprints/IMPROVEMENT_INTELLIGENCE_PLATFORM_SPRINT_2026-03-29.md`
- Fixes: CCP-393 (Add Conversion And Resourcefulness Metrics)

## Context

CCP-393 was linked to the conversion and resourcefulness task. Audit evidence shows that `GameSummary` records (from `src/stats/types.ts`) include `hadWinningPosition`, `converted`, `hadLosingPosition`, and `survived` boolean fields, and that these are computed during extraction. However no stats view renders these metrics.

## What This Prompt Must Build

In `src/stats/view.ts`, add a conversion and resourcefulness section per sprint Task 15:

**Conversion rate** (shown when at least 5 games had `hadWinningPosition: true`):
- "You reached a winning position in X games and converted Y of them (Z%)"
- `X` = count of games where `hadWinningPosition: true`
- `Y` = count of games where `converted: true`
- `Z` = Y / X as a percentage

**Resourcefulness rate** (shown when at least 5 games had `hadLosingPosition: true`):
- "You were in a losing position in X games and saved Y of them (Z%)"
- `X` = count of games where `hadLosingPosition: true`
- `Y` = count of games where `survived: true`
- `Z` = Y / X as a percentage

**Failed-conversion game list** (stretch goal — implement if straightforward):
- List the specific games where `hadWinningPosition: true` AND `converted: false`
- Each entry is a clickable link that loads the game in `/analysis`
- Limit to the 5 most recent failed conversions
- Label: opponent name + date

**Show/hide rules**:
- Conversion rate hidden when fewer than 5 games had a winning position
- Resourcefulness rate hidden when fewer than 5 games had a losing position
- If both are hidden, the entire section is absent — no placeholder

**Rules**:
- Snabbdom `h()` only
- Touch only `src/stats/view.ts`
- No chart library
- No new design system

## Validation

- Conversion rate sentence renders when 5+ games had a winning position
- Resourcefulness rate sentence renders when 5+ games had a losing position
- Correct counts and percentages computed from `GameSummary` fields
- Section completely absent when neither threshold is met
- Failed-conversion game links navigate to `/analysis` with the correct game loaded (if stretch goal implemented)

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-393-F1
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-393-F1 --checklist "- [ ] Conversion rate sentence renders with correct X/Y/Z values when 5+ winning-position games|- [ ] Resourcefulness rate sentence renders with correct X/Y/Z values when 5+ losing-position games|- [ ] Section absent from DOM when both thresholds unmet|- [ ] Values computed correctly from hadWinningPosition/converted/hadLosingPosition/survived fields|- [ ] Failed-conversion game links navigate to /analysis (if implemented)"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-393-F1 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Conversion rate sentence renders with correct X/Y/Z values when 5+ winning-position games|- [ ] Resourcefulness rate sentence renders with correct X/Y/Z values when 5+ losing-position games|- [ ] Section absent from DOM when both thresholds unmet|- [ ] Values computed correctly from hadWinningPosition/converted/hadLosingPosition/survived fields|- [ ] Failed-conversion game links navigate to /analysis (if implemented)"
```

# CCP-663 — SPR-004 UI Layer Completion Manager

## Purpose

This manager prompt runs the 8 follow-up prompts that complete the unfinished UI layer
of SPR-004 (Mini Sprint — Improvement Intelligence Platform). Each child prompt targets
a specific `stats/view.ts` section that was found Partial or Not Started by the
2026-04-02 audit.

## Child Prompts (execute in this exact order)

| Step | Prompt ID | Sprint Task | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CCP-385-F1 | SPR-004-T07 | Replace weakness panel placeholders with full DiagnosedWeakness cards |
| 2 | CCP-386-F1 | SPR-004-T08 | Complete accuracy and blunder trend charts with rolling average and per-time-control lines |
| 3 | CCP-387-F1 | SPR-004-T09 | Build opening win rate table from import data (no analysis required) |
| 4 | CCP-387-F2 | SPR-004-T10 | Extend opening table with accuracy/blunder columns from GameSummary |
| 5 | CCP-388-F1 | SPR-004-T11 | Add tactical profile section (missed moments, type breakdown, trend) |
| 6 | CCP-391-F1 | SPR-004-T14 | Wire "Train this" buttons in weakness cards to correct Patzer tool routes |
| 7 | CCP-392-F1 | SPR-004-T15 | Add time management profile section using GameSummary clock fields |
| 8 | CCP-393-F1 | SPR-004-T16 | Add conversion and resourcefulness metrics panel |

## Ordering Rationale

- Step 1 (CCP-385-F1) before Step 6 (CCP-391-F1): weakness cards must exist before training action wiring
- Step 3 (CCP-387-F1) before Step 4 (CCP-387-F2): base opening table must exist before the analysis-column extension
- Steps 2, 5, 7, 8 are independent and run in sprint task order

## Execution Rules

For each child prompt, in order:

1. Read the child prompt body at `docs/prompts/items/<ID>.md`
2. Run `npm run prompt:start -- <ID>`
3. Execute the work described in the prompt
4. Run `npm run prompt:complete -- <ID> --checklist "..."` (use the checklist from the prompt body's Lifecycle section)
5. Proceed to the next step only after the current child is fully complete

Do not skip steps. Do not reorder. Do not start the next child if the current child fails — stop and surface the error.

## Lifecycle

Before executing any child prompts, mark this manager as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-663
```

After all child prompts are complete, mark the manager as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-663 --checklist "- [ ] CCP-385-F1 complete (weakness cards)|- [ ] CCP-386-F1 complete (trend charts)|- [ ] CCP-387-F1 complete (opening win rate table)|- [ ] CCP-387-F2 complete (opening table analysis columns)|- [ ] CCP-388-F1 complete (tactical profile)|- [ ] CCP-391-F1 complete (training action wiring)|- [ ] CCP-392-F1 complete (time management profile)|- [ ] CCP-393-F1 complete (conversion and resourcefulness)"
```

# CCP-664 — PGN file upload for openings import

## Sprint Linkage

- Sprint: SPR-016 (Opponent Research Completion)
- Phase: SPR-016-P2 (Import and Data Integrity)
- Task: SPR-016-T02 (PGN file upload)

## Context

The Opponents page import workflow currently only supports PGN paste via a textarea in
`src/openings/view.ts`. Users need the ability to import PGN files directly via file
browse or drag-and-drop, feeding the parsed file contents into the same existing PGN
import pipeline.

## Task

Add a file input element to the openings import workflow that accepts `.pgn` files via
browse or drag-and-drop. When a file is selected, read its text contents and feed them
into the existing PGN import pipeline — the same path that the paste textarea uses.

## Scope

**Files**: `src/openings/view.ts` (1 file)

- Add an `<input type="file" accept=".pgn">` element near the existing paste textarea
- Support both click-to-browse and drag-and-drop onto the file input area
- Read the selected file using the FileReader API (or equivalent)
- Feed the parsed text into the existing PGN import pipeline
- Do NOT change the import pipeline itself — only add the new input path
- Do NOT touch files outside `src/openings/view.ts`

## Lichess Reference

Check the Lichess PGN import UI for behavioral reference:
- `~/Development/lichess-source/lila/ui/analyse/src/study/studyForm.ts` — study PGN import form
- Adapt the file input pattern to fit the existing openings import workflow

## Validation

- [ ] File input element is visible in the openings import UI
- [ ] Clicking the file input opens a file browser filtered to `.pgn` files
- [ ] Selecting a `.pgn` file reads its contents and triggers the import pipeline
- [ ] Drag-and-drop of a `.pgn` file onto the input area also triggers import
- [ ] Both file upload and paste textarea produce identical import results
- [ ] No changes to files other than `src/openings/view.ts`

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-664
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-664 --checklist "- [ ] File input element visible in openings import UI|- [ ] Click-to-browse opens file picker filtered to .pgn|- [ ] Selecting a .pgn file triggers import pipeline|- [ ] Drag-and-drop of .pgn file triggers import|- [ ] File upload and paste produce identical results|- [ ] Only src/openings/view.ts modified"
```

# CCP-665 — Global sample size warnings for Prep Report

## Sprint Linkage

- Sprint: SPR-016 (Opponent Research Completion)
- Phase: SPR-016-P2 (Import and Data Integrity)
- Task: SPR-016-T03 (Global sample size warnings)

## Context

The Prep Report displays percentages and statistics across multiple surfaces (win rates,
opening frequencies, deviation rates, etc.). Currently none of these show their sample size,
and small collections produce misleading statistics with no warning. This is a data honesty
pass — every stat must show what it's based on, and unreliable numbers must be visually
distinguished.

## Task

Add sample size indicators to every percentage and statistic in the Prep Report. Implement
a collection-level warning when the total game count is below a configurable threshold.
Visually dim unreliable stats where the denominator is too small for the percentage to be
meaningful.

## Scope

**Files**: `src/openings/view.ts`, `src/openings/analytics.ts` (2 files)

### In `src/openings/analytics.ts`:
- Define a threshold constant for minimum collection size (e.g., `MIN_COLLECTION_SIZE`)
- Define a threshold for minimum stat denominator reliability (e.g., `MIN_RELIABLE_SAMPLE`)
- Add helper functions that analytics consumers can use to check sample reliability
- Ensure every computed statistic carries its sample size (n value) alongside the result

### In `src/openings/view.ts`:
- Render sample size (e.g., "62% (n=13)") next to every percentage in the Prep Report
- When total collection size is below the threshold, show an explicit warning banner
- Apply a visual dim/muted style to stats where the denominator is below the reliability threshold
- Use existing CSS classes or minimal inline styling — do NOT create new CSS files

### Do NOT:
- Change the import pipeline or game parsing
- Modify files outside the two listed files
- Add new analytics computations — only annotate existing ones with sample sizes

## Lichess Reference

Check how Lichess handles sample-size caveats in insights/stats surfaces:
- `~/Development/lichess-source/lila/ui/insight/src/` — insight chart rendering
- Adapt the pattern of showing counts alongside percentages

## Validation

- [ ] Every percentage in the Prep Report shows its sample size (n value)
- [ ] Collections below the minimum size threshold display a warning banner
- [ ] Stats with small denominators are visually dimmed/muted
- [ ] Threshold constants are defined in `analytics.ts`, not hardcoded in view
- [ ] No changes to files other than `src/openings/view.ts` and `src/openings/analytics.ts`
- [ ] Existing Prep Report layout and functionality unchanged beyond the additions

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-665
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-665 --checklist "- [ ] Every percentage shows sample size (n value)|- [ ] Small collection warning banner displays|- [ ] Unreliable stats visually dimmed|- [ ] Thresholds defined in analytics.ts|- [ ] Only view.ts and analytics.ts modified|- [ ] Existing Prep Report layout unchanged"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-665 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Every percentage shows sample size (n value)|- [ ] Small collection warning banner displays|- [ ] Unreliable stats visually dimmed|- [ ] Thresholds defined in analytics.ts|- [ ] Only view.ts and analytics.ts modified|- [ ] Existing Prep Report layout unchanged"
```

# CCP-666 — Investigate Opening Tree sidebar disappearance at certain widths

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`

Prompt ID: CCP-666
Task ID: CCP-666
Short task title: Investigate Opening Tree sidebar disappearance at certain widths
Source document: User request
Source step: Opening Tree sidebar disappearance responsive bug

## Context

Bug report:
- on the Opening Tree page, when the window width reaches certain sizes, the sidebar /
  openings list appears to disappear
- the user wants to know whether this behavior is explicitly present in the code and how
  to stop it from happening

The important question is not just "make it look better."
You must determine whether the disappearance is:
- an intentional responsive-collapse rule already in code
- an unintended grid/layout squeeze
- or a broader shell/layout ownership issue

## Relevant current code areas

Start with the actual current ownership points:
- `src/openings/view.ts`
- `src/styles/main.scss`
- `src/openings/ctrl.ts`

Known current suspects from repo inspection:
- the openings session shell is rendered in `renderOpeningsSession()` and uses
  `.openings__session-body`
- the left tool rail is rendered by `renderToolRail()`
- the openings session layout in `src/styles/main.scss` uses a three-column grid for:
  - tool rail
  - board
  - session panel
- there is also a responsive breakpoint:
  - `@media (max-width: 700px) { .openings__session-body { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } }`

## Required workflow

1. Inspect the actual current Patzer code first.
2. Identify exactly which user-facing surface is disappearing:
   - left tool rail
   - right-side Opening Tree move list / session panel
   - or both
3. Determine whether the behavior is explicitly encoded in CSS/layout rules or is an indirect
   consequence of the current grid math.
4. Inspect relevant Lichess source if it helps with board-plus-side-panel layout decisions.
   If Lichess has no direct equivalent for this openings shell, say that clearly instead of
   forcing a false parity claim.
5. Compare:
   - how Patzer currently behaves
   - whether the disappearance is intentional in code
   - the smallest safe way to keep the opening-tree side content visible and usable
6. If the cause is clear and the fix is small/safe, implement it.
7. If the cause is ambiguous or needs broader design decisions, stop after diagnosis and give a
   precise recommendation instead of making a risky layout rewrite.

## Constraints

- keep this scoped to the Opening Tree session layout / responsive behavior
- do not redesign the opponent-research tool suite
- do not bundle unrelated openings polish
- do not move substantial new logic into `src/main.ts`
- prefer the smallest safe fix

## What to answer explicitly before coding

Provide:
- prompt id
- task id
- source document
- source step
- relevant Patzer Pro files
- relevant Lichess files, if any
- exact diagnosis
- whether the disappearing behavior is explicitly found in the code
- exact small step to implement
- why that step is safely scoped

## Validation

If you implement a fix, run:

```sh
npm run build
```

Then do the most relevant task-specific validation you can and report:
- build result
- what width range / responsive case was actually causing the disappearance
- whether the disappearance was explicitly caused by current code
- whether the Opening Tree side content now stays visible and usable
- whether other openings tools still render correctly after the change
- whether there are console/runtime errors
- remaining risks or limitations

## Manual checks to use in completion

- open an opponent collection and switch to `Opening Tree`
- resize the window through the width range where the sidebar / openings list previously disappeared
- confirm the side content remains visible and usable
- confirm the board still renders correctly
- confirm switching between openings tools still works after the fix

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-666
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-666 --checklist "- [ ] Open an opponent collection and switch to Opening Tree — expected result: normal session shell loads|- [ ] Resize the window through the problematic width range — expected result: the sidebar / openings list no longer disappears|- [ ] Confirm the Opening Tree side content remains visible and usable|- [ ] Confirm the board still renders correctly after the responsive change|- [ ] Confirm switching to other openings tools still works|- [ ] Confirm no console errors appear during resize and tool switching"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-666 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Open an opponent collection and switch to Opening Tree — expected result: normal session shell loads|- [ ] Resize the window through the problematic width range — expected result: the sidebar / openings list no longer disappears|- [ ] Confirm the Opening Tree side content remains visible and usable|- [ ] Confirm the board still renders correctly after the responsive change|- [ ] Confirm switching to other openings tools still works|- [ ] Confirm no console errors appear during resize and tool switching"
```

# CCP-667 — Lock Prompt Metadata Lines In Dashboard Editor

## Summary

Fix the prompt detail dashboard editor so registry-owned metadata lines are truly locked in the UI,
not just rejected at save time.

The current bug is:
- prompt detail edit mode visually shows the metadata block separately
- but the editable textarea still contains those lines
- so users can edit `Prompt ID`, `Task ID`, `Parent Prompt ID`, `Source Document`,
  `Source Step`, and `Execution Target`
- save is then blocked by backend validation, but the UI contract is still wrong

The expected behavior is:
- locked metadata remains visible inline at the top of the editor
- those lines are not editable
- the editable surface starts only after the locked metadata block
- diff preview and save behavior continue to work correctly for the editable body

## Scope

Inspect and update the prompt-detail editor path in at least:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/server.mjs`
- any prompt workflow docs that now misdescribe the actual editor behavior

## Requirements

1. Keep registry-owned metadata visibly present in edit mode.
2. Make those lines non-editable in the UI, not merely save-blocked later.
3. Preserve the current save flow:
   - same active `CCP-###`
   - superseded archive handling
   - `lastEditedAt`
   - full artifact regeneration
4. Preserve the current diff preview semantics for the editable portion of the body.
5. Preserve the localhost-only guidance in static `file://` mode.
6. Do not regress prompt copying, cancel/reset behavior, or archived-history display.

## Validation

- open an editable prompt in dashboard detail view and confirm metadata lines are visible but cannot be modified
- edit the first editable line after metadata and confirm live diff preview updates correctly
- save a valid body edit and confirm save still succeeds end to end
- attempt to change only editable body content and confirm backend metadata-mismatch error is not triggered
- confirm static `file://` mode still shows localhost instructions instead of pretending edit/save works

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-667
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-667 --checklist "- [ ] Editable prompt metadata lines are visible but not editable in dashboard detail|- [ ] Live diff preview still updates for real body edits only|- [ ] Prompt save/regeneration flow still succeeds for valid edits|- [ ] Static HTML mode still shows localhost editing instructions"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-667 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Editable prompt metadata lines are visible but not editable in dashboard detail|- [ ] Live diff preview still updates for real body edits only|- [ ] Prompt save/regeneration flow still succeeds for valid edits|- [ ] Static HTML mode still shows localhost editing instructions"
```
