# CCP-667-F1 — Replace Separate Diff Preview With Line-By-Line Stylized Editor

## Summary

Follow up on `CCP-667` by replacing the current textarea-plus-separate-preview editing model
with a single line-by-line stylized diff editor.

Scope split to avoid overlap:
- `CCP-667` remains responsible for making prompt metadata lines truly locked/non-editable
- `CCP-667-F1` is responsible for the new live editor model itself across both prompt detail
  editing and sprint detail panel editing

## Problem

The current implementation does not match the intended editing UX:
- edit mode still uses a normal textarea
- diff is shown in a separate preview panel
- the main editor does not itself become the visual diff surface

The target behavior is simpler than full track-changes editing:
- line-by-line diff, not character-by-character editing logic
- one unified stylized editor surface
- no separate live preview panel

## Required Behavior

Apply this to both:
- prompt detail editing in the `Prompts` tab
- sprint panel editing in sprint detail view

### Editing Model

- edit mode uses one stylized editor block
- unchanged lines remain visible
- no separate `Live Diff Preview` panel remains
- line numbers are visible

### Line Activation

When the user activates editing on an unchanged line:
- that line immediately becomes:
  - the original line shown in red struck-through form
  - a green editable replacement line directly below it
- the new green line is prefilled with the original text
- the user edits only the green replacement line from there

### Added / Deleted / Replaced Lines

- inserted lines appear as green editable lines in place
- deleted lines remain visible in red struck-through form
- full-line deletions remain visible until save/reset/restore
- each deleted line gets its own restore control
- restoring a deleted line returns it to a normal unchanged line
- if replacement lines exist for that deleted line, keep them unless they are blank

### Locked Lines

For prompts:
- registry-owned metadata stays inside the same editor block at the top
- those lines are locked and non-editable

For sprint panels:
- the sprint context header stays at the top of the same editor block
- it remains visible but is not part of the diffable editable body

Locked-line cue:
- each locked line shows a small lock icon on the far left

### Read-Only Saved View

After save:
- the main read-only view shows the stylized diff against the immediately previous saved version
- unchanged lines remain visible in that view
- archived history still exists separately and remains oldest-first

### Copy Behavior

Copy from either edit mode or read-only diff view must copy:
- the clean current body only
- never the red deleted lines or visual diff markup

### Save Confirmation

Replace the current text-only overwrite confirmation with an inline visual save-confirm state:
- the editor surface becomes temporarily non-editable during confirmation
- available actions:
  - `Confirm Save`
  - `Back to Editing`
  - `Copy`
- if save fails:
  - return to editable mode automatically
  - preserve the attempted changes
  - show an error message

### Resets / Cancel

- `Reset to Original` or `Reset to Saved` clears all diff markers immediately
- `Cancel` discards edits and returns to read-only saved view

## Relevant Files

At minimum inspect:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/server.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/prompt-registry-lib.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/sprint-registry-lib.mjs`
- the canonical prompt and sprint workflow docs if the behavior contract changes

## Constraints

- do not implement character-by-character tracked editing
- keep the implementation line-based
- do not regress localhost-only behavior for write actions
- do not break superseded prompt archive handling
- do not break sprint panel saved-body history handling
- do not let locked prompt metadata become editable

## Validation

- open an editable prompt and confirm edit mode uses one stylized line-diff editor with no separate live preview panel
- activate editing on one unchanged prompt line and confirm it becomes one red original line plus one green editable replacement line
- confirm prompt metadata lines stay visible, locked, and marked with lock icons
- save a prompt edit and confirm read-only view shows diff against the immediately previous saved version
- open a sprint panel editor and confirm it uses the same line-based stylized editing model while keeping the sprint header non-diffable and locked
- copy from edit mode and read-only mode and confirm clipboard text is the clean current saved body only
- confirm static `file://` mode still instructs the user to run `node server.mjs` and use `http://localhost:3001/dashboard`

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-667-F1
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-667-F1 --checklist "- [ ] Prompt detail editing uses one line-by-line stylized editor instead of a separate diff preview|- [ ] Locked prompt metadata remains visible and non-editable with lock cues|- [ ] Sprint panel editing uses the same line-based stylized model with locked header context|- [ ] Copy/save/read-only behavior still uses the clean current body and previous-version diff correctly|- [ ] Static HTML mode still routes users to the local dashboard server for edits"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-667-F1 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Prompt detail editing uses one line-by-line stylized editor instead of a separate diff preview|- [ ] Locked prompt metadata remains visible and non-editable with lock cues|- [ ] Sprint panel editing uses the same line-based stylized model with locked header context|- [ ] Copy/save/read-only behavior still uses the clean current body and previous-version diff correctly|- [ ] Static HTML mode still routes users to the local dashboard server for edits"
```

# CCP-668 — Add Skipped Prompt Lifecycle And Dashboard Control

## Summary

Add a first-class `skipped` prompt lifecycle state so queued prompts can be intentionally
removed from the runnable queue without being mistaken for active work later.

This must be bulletproof:
- skipped prompts must never appear as runnable queue candidates
- skipped prompts must never be startable/completable/reviewable
- if a user later asks an agent what prompts are available in queue, skipped prompts must
  never be included

## Scope

Implement the smallest complete workflow change needed to support skipping a prompt safely:

1. Add a first-class skipped prompt state in the prompt registry workflow.
2. Add a skip command and dashboard action.
3. Make skipped prompts non-runnable everywhere.
4. Remove skipped prompts from all queue / next-up / runnable surfaces.
5. Update all canonical prompt workflow docs that need to know this behavior exists.

## Required Behavior

### Registry / lifecycle model

- introduce `status: skipped`
- skipped prompts must also use `queueState: not-queued`
- add `skippedAt`
- optionally add `skipReason` if needed for clarity

Hard rules:
- skipped prompts are still real tracked prompt records
- skipped prompts are not superseded prompts
- skipped prompts are not runnable prompts

### Command behavior

Add a lifecycle command:

```sh
npm run prompt:skip -- <PROMPT_ID>
```

Expected behavior:
- confirms the prompt exists
- rejects reserved / superseded / already-reviewed / already-skipped prompts
- transitions the prompt to:
  - `status: skipped`
  - `queueState: not-queued`
  - `skippedAt: <now>`
- regenerates prompt workflow artifacts via the normal refresh path

### Dashboard behavior

In prompt detail view:
- add a `Skip This Prompt` button in the top-right action area
- this button should only appear for prompts that are still skippable
- clicking it must require an explicit confirmation step before mutating state

After skip:
- prompt must no longer show as queued
- prompt must no longer appear in Next Up / urgent queue / queue list surfaces
- prompt detail should clearly show that it was skipped and when

### Queue safety

Skipped prompts must never appear in:
- `CLAUDE_PROMPT_QUEUE.md`
- dashboard queued views
- next-up helpers
- urgent queue helpers
- any queue-derived “available prompt” surfaces

### Lifecycle safety

After a prompt is skipped, the following must fail cleanly:
- `npm run prompt:start -- <PROMPT_ID>`
- `npm run prompt:complete -- <PROMPT_ID>`
- `npm run prompt:review -- <PROMPT_ID> ...`

## Files Likely Relevant

- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/prompt-registry-lib.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/prompt-start.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/prompt-complete.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/prompt-review.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/server.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/package.json`

Update the canonical prompt workflow docs according to the existing prompt workflow-change
rules. At minimum, check and update the relevant canonical prompt docs plus:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/AGENTS.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`

## Constraints

- keep this scoped to prompt workflow / dashboard behavior
- do not invent a second state like “abandoned in progress” in this prompt
- do not allow skipped prompts to remain queryable as normal runnable work
- preserve existing superseded prompt protections
- preserve existing prompt edit behavior

## Validation

- create or identify a skippable prompt and skip it successfully
- confirm skipped prompt no longer appears in generated queue docs
- confirm skipped prompt no longer appears in dashboard queue / next-up surfaces
- confirm `prompt:start`, `prompt:complete`, and `prompt:review` reject the skipped prompt
- confirm prompt detail shows skipped state clearly
- run prompt workflow regeneration/audit successfully

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-668
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-668 --checklist "- [ ] Added a first-class skipped prompt lifecycle state|- [ ] Added a skip command and dashboard action with confirmation|- [ ] Skipped prompts no longer appear in queue / next-up / runnable surfaces|- [ ] Skipped prompts are rejected by prompt:start, prompt:complete, and prompt:review|- [ ] Canonical prompt workflow docs and agent docs were updated"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-668 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Added a first-class skipped prompt lifecycle state|- [ ] Added a skip command and dashboard action with confirmation|- [ ] Skipped prompts no longer appear in queue / next-up / runnable surfaces|- [ ] Skipped prompts are rejected by prompt:start, prompt:complete, and prompt:review|- [ ] Canonical prompt workflow docs and agent docs were updated"
```

# CCP-667-F2 — Fix Prompt Editor Flicker Before Entering Edit View

## Summary

Follow up on `CCP-667-F1`.

Right now, when the user clicks `Edit` in prompt detail, the UI briefly shows a strange
intermediate preview/state before it settles into what looks like the edit viewer.

On further inspection, the problem is worse than a visual flicker:
- the user may see an intermediate preview-like state with another apparent edit step
- even after pressing through that extra step, the resulting surface may still not behave like a
  real editable text editor
- the user should not have to click through a preview phase just to reach actual editable text

This should feel immediate and intentional:
- clicking `Edit` should transition directly into the edit viewer
- the detail panel should not briefly flash a partial preview or odd intermediate state

## Diagnosis Target

Inspect the current prompt detail edit transition in the generated dashboard implementation.

The likely issue is that the detail view is being fully re-rendered in a way that briefly
shows a non-editing state before the edit-state shell is mounted.

Confirm the exact cause in the current code before changing behavior.

## Required Fix

Implement the smallest safe fix so prompt detail edit mode enters cleanly.

Expected behavior:
- click `Edit`
- prompt detail goes straight into a real editable stylized text viewer
- no strange transient preview / flicker / intermediate read-only redraw appears first
- once edit mode is entered, the prompt body must actually accept text editing immediately

Constraints:
- keep this scoped to prompt detail edit-mode entry behavior
- do not redesign the whole editor model again
- do not regress locked metadata behavior from `CCP-667`
- do not regress the line-by-line stylized editor behavior from `CCP-667-F1`
- do not change sprint panel editing unless the same shared helper must be adjusted safely

## Files Likely Relevant

- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

If a shared helper must be touched for correctness, keep the change as small as possible.

## Validation

- open a prompt detail view
- click `Edit`
- confirm the UI goes directly into the stylized edit viewer with no strange intermediate preview
- confirm the resulting editor is actually editable as text immediately after entering edit mode
- confirm locked metadata still shows correctly
- confirm copy/save/cancel/reset still behave correctly
- confirm prompt detail still opens normally for non-editable prompts

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-667-F2
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-667-F2 --checklist "- [ ] Prompt detail enters edit mode directly without intermediate preview flicker|- [ ] Locked metadata still renders correctly in edit mode|- [ ] Copy/save/cancel/reset still work after the transition fix|- [ ] Non-editable prompt detail still renders normally"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-667-F2 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Prompt detail enters edit mode directly without intermediate preview flicker|- [ ] Locked metadata still renders correctly in edit mode|- [ ] Copy/save/cancel/reset still work after the transition fix|- [ ] Non-editable prompt detail still renders normally"
```

# CCP-667-F3 — Fix Sprint Panel Editor Flicker Before Entering Edit View

the sprint detail editable panels.

intermediate preview/state with another edit button before it settles into what looks like the
real editor.

On further inspection, the problem is worse than a visual flicker:
- the user may see an extra preview-like step before edit mode
- even after pressing through that extra step, the resulting surface may still not behave like a
  real editable text editor
- the user should not have to click through a preview phase just to reach actual editable text

This should feel immediate and intentional:
- clicking `Edit` should go straight into the stylized sprint panel editor
- the sprint detail view should not flash a preview-like intermediate state first

## Required Fix

Implement the smallest safe fix so sprint panel edit mode enters cleanly.

Expected behavior:
- click `Edit` on a sprint panel
- that panel goes directly into a real editable stylized text viewer
- no extra preview frame or duplicate edit step appears first
- once edit mode is entered, the panel body must actually accept text editing immediately

Constraints:
- keep this scoped to sprint panel edit-mode entry behavior
- do not redesign the sprint panel editor again
- do not regress saved-body history, cancel/reset, save confirm, or localhost-only behavior
- do not regress the prompt-side fix from `CCP-667-F2`
- only touch shared helpers if that is the smallest safe way to fix the sprint side

# CCP-667-F4 — Fix Stylized Editor So It Is Actually Editable

## Summary

Follow up on `CCP-667-F1`.

After entering the stylized editor on prompt detail and sprint detail panels, the resulting
surface is still not behaving like a real editable text editor.

This is distinct from the transition/flicker bug:
- the user may click `Edit`
- the UI may enter what looks like the editor
- but the user still cannot actually edit the text as expected

This prompt is specifically about making the stylized editor truly editable.

## Scope

Fix the actual text-editability of the stylized line-based editor for both:
- prompt detail editing in the `Prompts` tab
- sprint panel editing in sprint detail view

The goal is not to redesign the editor again.

The goal is:
- when the user enters edit mode, the editor behaves like a real editable text surface
- text input, cursor placement, line activation, and text replacement work as intended

## Required Behavior

- prompt detail edit mode must allow real text editing immediately
- sprint panel edit mode must allow real text editing immediately
- activated editable lines must accept typing naturally
- line insertion / replacement behavior must still follow the intended line-based editor model
- locked metadata/header lines must remain non-editable

## Constraints

- keep this scoped to actual text editability of the current stylized editor
- do not redesign the prompt/sprint workflow
- do not remove locked metadata/header behavior
- do not regress copy/save/cancel/reset
- do not regress saved diff/history behavior

## Files Likely Relevant

- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

## Validation

- open an editable prompt and confirm typed text actually changes the active editable line
- open an editable sprint panel and confirm typed text actually changes the active editable line
- confirm locked lines remain non-editable
- confirm save/cancel/reset still work
- confirm prompt detail and sprint detail still render normally outside edit mode

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-667-F4
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-667-F4 --checklist "- [ ] Prompt detail stylized editor is actually editable as text|- [ ] Sprint panel stylized editor is actually editable as text|- [ ] Locked metadata/header lines remain non-editable|- [ ] Save/cancel/reset still work after the editability fix"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-667-F4 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Prompt detail stylized editor is actually editable as text|- [ ] Sprint panel stylized editor is actually editable as text|- [ ] Locked metadata/header lines remain non-editable|- [ ] Save/cancel/reset still work after the editability fix"
```

# CCP-670 — Fix New Research username autofocus in opponent research

Read and follow:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/CLAUDE.md`

Prompt ID: CCP-670
Task ID: CCP-670
Short task title: Fix New Research username autofocus in opponent research
Source document: User request
Source step: Opponent Research New Research username autofocus bug

## Context

Bug report:
- when the user clicks `New Research` on the Opponent Research page, the username text box
  in the details form should auto-select for immediate typing
- right now the user has to click into the field manually

Expected behavior:
- clicking `New Research` or `Start New Research` should open the details form
- the username input should immediately receive focus
- the text should be selected so the user can start typing over any existing value

Current repo-grounded ownership:
- `src/openings/view.ts` renders:
  - the `New Research` button
  - the empty-state `Start New Research` button
  - the details-step username input
- current code inspection shows the username input has no insert/postpatch focus-management hook

## Scope

This is a small bug-fix prompt.

Keep it scoped to:
- the opponent-research details-step username field focus/select behavior

Do not bundle:
- broader import-flow redesign
- unrelated openings styling changes
- PGN-upload behavior changes unless they are strictly necessary for a safe focus fix

## Required workflow

1. Inspect the actual current Patzer code first.
2. Confirm the details-step render path and which input should receive focus.
3. Identify the smallest safe fix.
4. Implement only that fix.
5. Validate with build and a task-specific manual check.

## Before coding, answer explicitly

Provide:
- prompt id
- task id
- source document
- source step
- relevant Patzer Pro files
- relevant Lichess files, if any
- exact diagnosis
- exact small step to implement
- why that step is safely scoped

If Lichess has no meaningful direct equivalent for this Opponent Research import form behavior,
say so clearly instead of forcing a parity claim.

## Validation

Run:

```sh
npm run build
```

Then verify:
- clicking `New Research` focuses the username field immediately
- clicking `Start New Research` from the empty state also focuses the username field immediately
- existing username text, if present, is selected for replacement
- typing still updates state correctly
- no console/runtime errors appear

# CCP-671 — Fix Line Editor Newline Escaping Mismatch

Prompt ID: CCP-671
Task ID: CCP-671
Source Document: docs/internal/BUG_LINE_EDITOR_NEWLINE_MISMATCH.md

## Context

The dashboard inline editor in `generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs` has a systematic escaping bug.
When the generator writes JS functions into the HTML `<script>` block, it uses `'\\\\n'` (4 backslashes + n)
where it should use `'\\n'` (2 backslashes + n). This means that in the browser, `split('\\n')` tries to
split on literal backslash-n (2 chars) instead of newline (char code 10). The result: the entire prompt
body renders as a single `<input>` element, and locked header lines are concatenated into one row.

The full bug analysis with hex-verified evidence is in `docs/internal/BUG_LINE_EDITOR_NEWLINE_MISMATCH.md`.

## Task

Fix the escaping mismatch in `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs` at the specific lines identified
in the bug doc. Then regenerate `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` and verify the editor works.

### Affected lines (from bug doc)

All of these use `'\\\\n'` and need to become `'\\n'`:

| Function | Issue |
|---|---|
| `normalizePromptBody` | regex and replacement strings |
| `lockedPromptMetadataLines` | `join(...)` |
| `splitPromptEditorBody` | `split(...)` and `join(...)` |
| `composePromptEditorBody` | separator string |
| `createLineEditorState` | `split(...)` |
| `cleanLineEditorBody` | `join(...)` |
| `renderLineEditor` | `split(...)` for locked lines |
| `buildPromptDiffOps` | `split(...)` calls |
| `summarizePromptDiff` | `join(...)` |

Also fix `normalizePromptBody` regex: `/\\\\r\\\\n/g` → `/\\r\\n/g` and `/\\\\s+$/` → `/\\s+$/`.

### Do NOT change

- Lines in the audit/task display string builders (those embed `\\\\n` into HTML content strings,
  not JS split/join operations — they are correct as-is)
- The `sprintPanelContextLines` join which already uses the correct `'\\n'`

### Files to touch

1. `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs` — fix escaping
2. `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` — regenerated output (via `npm run dashboard:generate`)

## Validation

After the fix:
1. Run `npm run dashboard:generate` to regenerate dashboard.html
2. Open `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` in a browser served through `node server.mjs`
3. Open any editable prompt (status: created, queueState: queued-pending)
4. Click Edit — the locked header should render as separate lines (not concatenated)
5. The editable body should render as multiple individual line inputs (not one giant input)
6. Verify sprint panel editing also renders lines correctly
7. Hex-check a representative `split(` in the generated dashboard.html to confirm single backslash before n

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-671
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-671 --checklist "- [ ] Locked header renders as separate lines|- [ ] Editable body renders as multiple line inputs|- [ ] Sprint panel editor also renders correctly|- [ ] Hex-verified split uses single backslash-n in generated HTML|- [ ] Audit/task string builders were NOT changed"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-671 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Locked header renders as separate lines|- [ ] Editable body renders as multiple line inputs|- [ ] Sprint panel editor also renders correctly|- [ ] Hex-verified split uses single backslash-n in generated HTML|- [ ] Audit/task string builders were NOT changed"
```

# CCP-672 — Replace Line Editor With Textarea Plus Diff-On-Confirm

Prompt ID: CCP-672
Task ID: CCP-672
Source Document: (audit conversation, 2026-04-02)

## Context

The dashboard's inline prompt/sprint editor uses a custom 4-state line-by-line editor (~350 LOC client-side)
with context/removed/replacement/added line tracking, pairKey pairing, and per-line `<input>` elements.
This is overengineered for editing prompt bodies. It should be replaced with a standard `<textarea>`.

CCP-671 must be completed first (fixes the escaping bug that prevents the current editor from working at all).

## Task

In `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`, replace the line editor with a textarea-based editor.
Both prompt editing and sprint panel editing must use the same approach.

### Remove (from the generated JS)

- `createLineEditorState()` — 4-state line array creation
- `cleanLineEditorBody()` — filter/join from line array
- `activateLineEditorLine()` — paired removal/replacement logic
- `deleteContextLine()` — context→removed transition
- `updateLineEditorText()` — per-line text mutation
- `insertLineEditorLineAfter()` — add line after
- `removeEditableLine()` — remove added/replacement
- `restoreRemovedLine()` — restore with pairKey handling
- `renderLineEditor()` — per-line HTML grid rendering
- `nextLineEditorId()` / `lineEditorUid` — ID generation
- All line-level event handlers: `activate`, `delete-context`, `restore`, `remove-editable`, line `input` events, Enter-key line splitting
- All `.line-editor-*` CSS classes

### Replace with

**Edit state** (for both prompts and sprint panels):
```
{
  original: string,   // body text when editing started
  current: string,    // current textarea contents
  confirming: boolean // false=editing, true=showing diff confirmation
}
```

**Editing view:**
- Locked metadata rendered as read-only styled text above the textarea (same split logic as today)
- A `<textarea>` containing the editable body portion
- Buttons: "Revert to Original" (resets textarea to `original`), "Save" (enters confirmation), "Cancel" (exits edit mode, warns if changed)

**Confirmation view (when `confirming: true`):**
- Diff display using the existing `buildPromptDiffOps()` LCS algorithm (already fixed by CCP-671)
- Color coding: green for added lines, red for removed lines, neutral for context
- Buttons: "Confirm Save" (sends to server), "Back to Edit" (returns to textarea), "Decline / Revert" (reverts to original and exits edit mode)

**Behavior:**
- "Revert to Original" is available at any time during editing — resets textarea to `original`
- "Decline" in the confirmation step reverts to original and exits edit mode entirely
- The `buildPromptDiffOps` function stays (it's a clean ~30-line LCS, not part of the line editor)
- The `renderPromptDiffHtml` and `summarizePromptDiff` functions stay (they render the diff output)

### Files to touch

1. `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs` — editor rewrite
2. `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` — regenerated output

## Validation

1. Open an editable prompt in the dashboard (served via `node server.mjs`)
2. Click Edit — locked header is read-only, body appears in a textarea
3. Make changes in the textarea
4. Click "Revert to Original" — textarea resets to the original body
5. Make changes again, click "Save" — diff confirmation appears with green/red lines
6. Click "Back to Edit" — returns to textarea with changes preserved
7. Click "Save" again, then "Decline / Revert" — reverts to original, exits edit mode
8. Click "Save" again, then "Confirm Save" — server saves, prompt refreshes
9. Repeat steps 2-8 for a sprint panel editor — behavior should be identical
10. Verify no `.line-editor-*` CSS or line state functions remain in the generated HTML

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-672
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-672 --checklist "- [ ] Prompt editor uses textarea|- [ ] Sprint panel editor uses textarea|- [ ] Locked metadata is read-only above textarea|- [ ] Revert to Original works during editing|- [ ] Diff shows on save confirmation|- [ ] Decline in confirmation reverts to original|- [ ] Confirm Save writes to server correctly|- [ ] No line-editor state functions remain|- [ ] No .line-editor CSS remains"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-672 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Prompt editor uses textarea|- [ ] Sprint panel editor uses textarea|- [ ] Locked metadata is read-only above textarea|- [ ] Revert to Original works during editing|- [ ] Diff shows on save confirmation|- [ ] Decline in confirmation reverts to original|- [ ] Confirm Save writes to server correctly|- [ ] No line-editor state functions remain|- [ ] No .line-editor CSS remains"
```

# CCP-673 — Unify Sprint Panel Archival To RETIRED-V# Pattern

Prompt ID: CCP-673
Task ID: CCP-673
Source Document: (audit conversation, 2026-04-02)

## Context

Prompt editing archives old versions using the `RETIRED-PROMPT-DONOTRUN-REFERENCE-ONLY-<ID>-V#` pattern
in the prompt registry. Sprint panel editing uses a different approach: a `panelNotes.history` array
on the sprint record. This inconsistency should be unified — sprint panels should use the same
RETIRED-V# archival pattern.

CCP-672 must be completed first (the textarea editor must be in place).

## Task

Migrate sprint panel archival from `panelNotes.history` to the RETIRED-V# pattern used by prompts.

### Current sprint panel archival (to remove)

In `scripts/sprint-registry-lib.mjs`, sprint panel saves store previous versions in:
```
sprint.panelNotes[panel].history = [
  { body: "old version", archivedAt: "2026-04-02T..." },
  ...
]
```

### New sprint panel archival (to implement)

When a sprint panel body is saved:
1. Create a new entry in the sprint registry with a retired reference ID pattern:
   `RETIRED-SPRINT-PANEL-DONOTRUN-REFERENCE-ONLY-<SPRINT_ID>-<PANEL>-V#`
2. The retired entry stores:
   - `sprintId` — which sprint it belongs to
   - `panel` — which panel (audit, mismatch, nextPhase, appendRequest)
   - `archivedBody` — the previous panel body
   - `archivedAt` — timestamp
   - `supersededFromSprintId` — the originating sprint ID
   - `retiredReferenceOnly: true`
   - `hiddenFromDashboard: true`
3. The active sprint `panelNotes[panel]` keeps:
   - `currentBody` — the new saved body
   - `lastEditedAt` — timestamp
   - `supersededVersionIds` — array of retired reference IDs (like prompts do)
4. Remove the `history` array from `panelNotes[panel]`

### Files to touch

1. `scripts/sprint-registry-lib.mjs` — archival logic change
2. `docs/mini-sprints/sprint-registry.json` — will gain retired entries on next save

### Migration

Existing `panelNotes.history` entries do not need to be migrated. They can be left in place
as inert data. New saves going forward will use the RETIRED-V# pattern.

## Validation

1. Start `node server.mjs`
2. Open a sprint in the dashboard
3. Edit a sprint panel body and save
4. Check `sprint-registry.json` — a `RETIRED-SPRINT-PANEL-DONOTRUN-REFERENCE-ONLY-*` entry should exist
5. The active sprint's `panelNotes[panel]` should have `supersededVersionIds` array, no new `history` entries
6. Edit and save the same panel again — V2 should be created
7. Verify retired entries have `hiddenFromDashboard: true` and don't appear in the sprint list

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-673
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-673 --checklist "- [ ] Sprint panel save creates RETIRED-V# entry|- [ ] Active panel has supersededVersionIds array|- [ ] No new history array entries on save|- [ ] Second save creates V2|- [ ] Retired entries have hiddenFromDashboard: true|- [ ] Retired entries do not appear in sprint list"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-673 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Sprint panel save creates RETIRED-V# entry|- [ ] Active panel has supersededVersionIds array|- [ ] No new history array entries on save|- [ ] Second save creates V2|- [ ] Retired entries have hiddenFromDashboard: true|- [ ] Retired entries do not appear in sprint list"
```

# CCP-674 — Update Server Endpoints For Textarea Editor And Unified Archival

Prompt ID: CCP-674
Task ID: CCP-674
Source Document: (audit conversation, 2026-04-02)

## Context

The server endpoints `/api/prompt-edit` and `/api/sprint-panel-note` currently expect payloads
shaped by the old line editor. After CCP-672 (textarea editor) and CCP-673 (unified archival),
the server handlers need to be updated to match the simplified client and the new sprint archival model.

CCP-672 and CCP-673 must be completed first.

## Task

Update the server-side handlers in `server.mjs` to work with the new textarea-based editor
and the unified RETIRED-V# archival for sprint panels.

### `/api/prompt-edit` (handlePromptEditSave)

This endpoint is likely already compatible since it receives `{ promptId, body }` — the textarea
just sends the same payload. However:
1. Verify it still works end-to-end with the new textarea client
2. Confirm the superseded archival still creates RETIRED-PROMPT-DONOTRUN-REFERENCE-ONLY entries
3. Remove any code that referenced line editor state if present
4. Ensure `lastEditedAt` is still set on the active prompt

### `/api/sprint-panel-note` (handleSprintPanelNoteWrite)

This endpoint needs updating to use the new archival pattern from CCP-673:
1. On save, call the updated `sprint-registry-lib` archival function that creates RETIRED-V# entries
2. Stop writing to `panelNotes.history` array (the lib change from CCP-673 handles this)
3. Ensure the response returns the retired entry ID so the client can confirm archival
4. Ensure `lastEditedAt` is still set

### `/api/sprint-panel-note/clear`

Verify that clearing a saved panel note still works correctly after the archival change.
Clearing should not create a retired entry — it just removes the saved override.

### Files to touch

1. `server.mjs` — update handlers

## Validation

1. Start `node server.mjs`
2. Edit and save a prompt from the dashboard — verify server returns `{ok: true, prompt, archivedId}`
3. Edit and save a sprint panel — verify server returns the retired entry ID
4. Clear a sprint panel note — verify it falls back to the generated default
5. Check `prompt-registry.json` for correct RETIRED prompt entry after prompt save
6. Check `sprint-registry.json` for correct RETIRED sprint panel entry after sprint save
7. Verify no references to line editor state remain in server.mjs

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-674
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-674 --checklist "- [ ] Prompt edit save works end-to-end with textarea client|- [ ] Sprint panel save creates RETIRED-V# entry via server|- [ ] Sprint panel clear works correctly|- [ ] Server responses include archived/retired entry IDs|- [ ] No line editor references remain in server.mjs"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-674 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Prompt edit save works end-to-end with textarea client|- [ ] Sprint panel save creates RETIRED-V# entry via server|- [ ] Sprint panel clear works correctly|- [ ] Server responses include archived/retired entry IDs|- [ ] No line editor references remain in server.mjs"
```

# CCP-675 — Update Dashboard And Workflow Docs For Textarea Editor And Unified Archival

Prompt ID: CCP-675
Task ID: CCP-675
Source Document: (audit conversation, 2026-04-02)

## Context

After CCP-671 through CCP-674, the dashboard editor has been replaced (line editor → textarea)
and sprint panel archival has been unified to the RETIRED-V# pattern. The workflow documentation
must be updated to reflect these changes.

CCP-671 through CCP-674 must be completed first.

## Task

Update all affected documentation to reflect:
1. The editor is now a textarea (not a line-by-line editor)
2. Sprint panels use the same RETIRED-V# archival pattern as prompts
3. The editing flow is: Edit → textarea (with Revert to Original) → Save → diff confirmation → Confirm/Decline
4. Decline in the confirmation step reverts to the original body

### Files to update

Check each file below. Update only sections that reference dashboard editing, sprint panel editing,
or archival behavior. Do not rewrite unrelated sections.

1. **`docs/prompts/PROMPT_REGISTRY_README.md`**
   - "Dashboard Prompt Editing" section — confirm it describes textarea, not line editor
   - Add note that prompt editing requires the local server (currently only sprint docs say this)

2. **`docs/prompts/PROMPT_CREATION_PROCESS.md`**
   - "Dashboard Edit Rule" section — confirm it reflects the textarea approach

3. **`docs/prompts/PROMPT_REVIEW_PROCESS.md`**
   - "Dashboard Prompt Editing" section — confirm it reflects the textarea approach

4. **`docs/mini-sprints/SPRINT_REGISTRY_README.md`**
   - "Sprint dashboard prompt panels" section — update to describe:
     - textarea editing (not line editor)
     - RETIRED-V# archival (not `panelNotes.history`)
     - `supersededVersionIds` on active panel notes

5. **`docs/mini-sprints/SPRINT_PROGRESS_PROCESS.md`**
   - "Editable Sprint Prompt Panels" section — update:
     - editing UX description (textarea, not per-line inputs)
     - archival description (RETIRED-V# pattern)
     - panel note persistence rules

6. **`CLAUDE.md`**
   - Check "sprint detail prompt panels" rules — update if they reference old editor behavior
   - Check "dashboard prompt editing" rules — update if needed

7. **`AGENTS.md`**
   - Check "dashboard prompt editing" rules — keep in sync with CLAUDE.md

8. **`docs/prompts/PROMPT_USER_GUIDE.md`**
   - "Edit An Existing Unrun Prompt" section — confirm it doesn't reference line editor specifics

### Do NOT change

- Prompt lifecycle rules (reserve, create, start, complete, review, skip)
- Sprint lifecycle rules (create, seed, backfill, audit)
- Prompt taxonomy rules
- Any unrelated documentation sections

## Validation

1. Read each updated file and verify the editing descriptions match the actual textarea implementation
2. Verify no references to "line editor", "line state", "pairKey", or "4-state" remain in any doc
3. Verify sprint archival docs describe RETIRED-V# pattern, not `panelNotes.history`
4. Verify CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md are in sync on dashboard editing rules
5. Verify the prompt docs mention that dashboard editing requires the local server

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-675
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-675 --checklist "- [ ] PROMPT_REGISTRY_README updated for textarea and server requirement|- [ ] PROMPT_CREATION_PROCESS Dashboard Edit Rule updated|- [ ] PROMPT_REVIEW_PROCESS Dashboard Editing updated|- [ ] SPRINT_REGISTRY_README updated for RETIRED-V# archival|- [ ] SPRINT_PROGRESS_PROCESS Editable Sprint Panels updated|- [ ] CLAUDE.md dashboard rules checked and updated|- [ ] AGENTS.md dashboard rules in sync with CLAUDE.md|- [ ] PROMPT_USER_GUIDE checked|- [ ] No references to line editor remain in docs"
```

# CCP-676 — Manager: Dashboard Editor Simplification

Prompt ID: CCP-676
Task ID: CCP-676
Source Document: (audit conversation, 2026-04-02)

## Summary

This manager prompt orchestrates the dashboard editor simplification work. The current dashboard
uses a custom 4-state line-by-line editor that is overengineered and has a critical escaping bug
that prevents it from working. This manager replaces it with a textarea-based editor, unifies
sprint panel archival to match prompts, and updates all documentation.

## Child Prompts (execute in this order)

1. **CCP-671** — Fix line editor newline escaping mismatch (bugfix)
2. **CCP-672** — Replace line editor with textarea plus diff-on-confirm (feature)
3. **CCP-673** — Unify sprint panel archival to RETIRED-V# pattern (feature)
4. **CCP-674** — Update server endpoints for textarea editor and unified archival (feature)
5. **CCP-675** — Update dashboard and workflow docs for textarea editor and unified archival (docs)

## Execution Order And Dependencies

- CCP-671 must complete before CCP-672 (the escaping fix is needed for the diff algorithm to work)
- CCP-672 must complete before CCP-673 (textarea editor must be in place before changing archival)
- CCP-672 and CCP-673 must complete before CCP-674 (server must match new client and archival)
- CCP-671 through CCP-674 must complete before CCP-675 (docs must reflect final implementation)

## Manager Rules

- Execute children in the exact listed order
- Run `prompt:start` and `prompt:complete` for each child prompt
- Do not reorder prompts
- Do not create new prompts during execution unless explicitly required
- Do not skip any child prompt

## Lifecycle

Before executing any child prompts, mark this manager as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-676
```

After all child prompts are complete, mark the manager as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-676 --checklist "- [ ] CCP-671 completed (escaping fix)|- [ ] CCP-672 completed (textarea editor)|- [ ] CCP-673 completed (unified archival)|- [ ] CCP-674 completed (server endpoints)|- [ ] CCP-675 completed (doc updates)|- [ ] All child prompt checklists verified"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-676 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] CCP-671 completed (escaping fix)|- [ ] CCP-672 completed (textarea editor)|- [ ] CCP-673 completed (unified archival)|- [ ] CCP-674 completed (server endpoints)|- [ ] CCP-675 completed (doc updates)|- [ ] All child prompt checklists verified"
```

# CCP-677 — Fix Sprint Grid Updated-At Accuracy And Add Created-At Timestamp

Prompt ID: CCP-677
Task ID: CCP-677
Source Document: user request 2026-04-02

## Context

The sprint grid currently shows "updated X minutes ago" on every sprint card. This timestamp
is inaccurate because `sprint.updatedAt` gets stamped on the entire sprint record every time
the registry is mutated — not just when the sprint itself was meaningfully changed. The result
is all sprints showing the same recent timestamp even if they haven't been touched.

Additionally, there is no "created at" timestamp visible on sprint cards, which would help
orient the user when reviewing the grid.

## Task

Fix the sprint grid to show accurate per-sprint timestamps, and add a created-at timestamp
to each card.

### 1. Fix the updated-at timestamp

The root cause is that `sprint.updatedAt` is set to `new Date().toISOString()` at the end
of every `mutateSprintRegistryLocked` call in `server.mjs`, even when the sprint being
mutated is a different sprint (e.g., a RETIRED panel archival entry is being added).

Fix: only stamp `sprint.updatedAt` on the sprint that was actually changed in the mutation,
not on every sprint that goes through the loop.

In `server.mjs` in `handleSprintPanelNoteWrite`, the line:
```js
sprint.updatedAt = new Date().toISOString();
```
is at the end of the mutation callback and applies to the active sprint being saved. That
part is fine. Check that no other code path is globally stamping unrelated sprints.

Also check `scripts/sprint-registry-lib.mjs` — if `recomputeSprintRegistryState` or any
normalization function is touching `updatedAt` on sprints that were not actually changed,
that must be removed.

### 2. Add created-at timestamp to sprint cards

In `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`, in the sprint card rendering function
(search for `sprint-card` CSS class or the function that builds the card HTML):

- Add a `createdAt` display below the `updatedAt` display on each sprint card
- Format: `Created: <date>` using the same `fmtDate` helper already used for other dates
- Style: small, centered, muted text below the existing "Updated X ago" line
- Only render the created-at line if `sprint.createdAt` is present

### Files to touch

1. `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs` — add created-at to sprint card
2. `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` — regenerated output (via `npm run dashboard:generate`)
3. `server.mjs` — verify/fix updatedAt stamping scope (if needed)

## Validation

1. Open the sprint grid in the dashboard (served via `node server.mjs`)
2. Verify each sprint card shows a "Created: <date>" below the updated timestamp
3. Edit a sprint panel body and save — only that sprint's updatedAt should change
4. Other sprints should still show their previous updatedAt (not all reset to now)
5. Verify the created-at text is small, centered, and muted on each card

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-677
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-677 --checklist "- [ ] Created-at timestamp visible on sprint cards|- [ ] Created-at is small, centered, muted text|- [ ] Saving a sprint panel only updates that sprint's updatedAt|- [ ] Other sprints retain their previous updatedAt after a save"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-677 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Created-at timestamp visible on sprint cards|- [ ] Created-at is small, centered, muted text|- [ ] Saving a sprint panel only updates that sprint's updatedAt|- [ ] Other sprints retain their previous updatedAt after a save"
```

# CCP-678 — Add Sprint Recompute To Dashboard Refresh Button

Prompt ID: CCP-678
Task ID: CCP-678
Source Document: user request 2026-04-02

## Context

The dashboard Refresh button currently calls `fetchLiveData()` to reload prompt and sprint
data from the server. However, it does not trigger `sprint:recompute`, which means the sprint
registry state (task execution rollups, phase rollups, sprint rollups) can drift from reality
without the user noticing. When the user clicks Refresh, they expect a fully up-to-date view
of sprint progress — not a stale snapshot.

## Task

Add a `/api/sprint-recompute` POST endpoint to `server.mjs` that runs
`recomputeSprintRegistryState()` and `refreshSprintArtifacts()`. Then update the live-mode
Refresh button handler in `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs` to call this endpoint
before `fetchLiveData()` so that every manual refresh also syncs sprint state.

### 1. Add the server endpoint

In `server.mjs`, add a handler for `POST /api/sprint-recompute`:

- Call `recomputeSprintRegistryState()` inside `mutateSprintRegistryLocked` (same pattern as
  other sprint mutation endpoints)
- Call `refreshSprintArtifacts()` after the mutation to regenerate SPRINT_STATUS.md and
  dashboard HTML
- Return `{ ok: true }` on success
- Return a 500 JSON error on failure

### 2. Update the dashboard Refresh button handler

In `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`, find the Refresh button click handler in the
live-mode JS. It currently calls `fetchLiveData()`. Change it to:

1. Show a loading/working state on the button (e.g., button text → "Refreshing…", disabled)
2. POST to `/api/sprint-recompute`
3. On success or failure, call `fetchLiveData()` as before
4. Restore button state after `fetchLiveData()` resolves

The sprint recompute failure must NOT block the page refresh — if the POST fails, log a
console warning but still proceed with `fetchLiveData()`.

### Files to touch

1. `server.mjs` — add `/api/sprint-recompute` endpoint
2. `scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs` — update Refresh button handler
3. `docs/prompts/dashboard.html` — regenerated output (via `npm run dashboard:generate`)

## Validation

1. Open the dashboard in live mode (`node server.mjs`)
2. Click Refresh — observe button briefly shows "Refreshing…"
3. Confirm the sprint registry state is recomputed (check terminal output for recompute log)
4. Confirm the dashboard reloads with updated sprint data
5. Confirm that if the server is not running (static mode), behavior is unchanged

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-678
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-678 --checklist "- [ ] /api/sprint-recompute endpoint added to server.mjs|- [ ] Refresh button calls sprint-recompute before fetchLiveData|- [ ] Sprint recompute failure does not block page refresh|- [ ] Button shows Refreshing… state during operation|- [ ] dashboard.html regenerated"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-678 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] /api/sprint-recompute endpoint added to server.mjs|- [ ] Refresh button calls sprint-recompute before fetchLiveData|- [ ] Sprint recompute failure does not block page refresh|- [ ] Button shows Refreshing… state during operation|- [ ] dashboard.html regenerated"
```

# CCP-679 — Opponents Left-Panel Label Truncation and Filter Default Fixes

## Summary

Fix truncated tool labels in the left-panel nav of the Opponents opening tree page, and change the default time filter to "All Time" in Prep Report and any other surfaces that use that filter.

## Context

The left-hand panel on the Opponents page contains tool nav labels that get cut off due to space constraints. The labels need to be shortened to fit cleanly. Additionally the Prep Report tool defaults to a time filter that is not "All Time" — that should be corrected.

## Tasks

### 1. Shorten truncated left-panel nav labels

| Current label   | Replace with |
|-----------------|--------------|
| Opening Tree    | Tree         |
| Repertoire      | Repertoire (fix truncation — shorten or wrap as needed) |
| Prep Report     | Report       |

Find where these labels are rendered in the Opponents/openings left-panel nav and apply the shorter strings.

### 2. Set default time filter to "All Time" in Prep Report

In the Prep Report tool (and any other surface on the Opponents page that uses a time-range filter), change the default selected value from whatever it currently is to "All Time".

## Scope

- Touch only the files that render the left-panel nav labels and the Prep Report default filter value
- Do not refactor surrounding code
- Maximum 1–3 files

## Validation

- [ ] Left panel nav labels are no longer cut off: "Tree", short "Repertoire" or equivalent, "Report"
- [ ] Prep Report default filter shows "All Time" on first load
- [ ] Any other time-range filter on the Opponents page also defaults to "All Time"
- [ ] No other visual regressions in the left panel or Opponents tool views

## Lifecycle

```sh

```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh

```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:

# CCP-680 — Learn From Your Mistakes: Eval Diff Showing Blank

## Summary

In Learn From Your Mistakes mode, the eval diff between the move played in the game and the best move is displaying as blank. Find the source of the missing value and fix it so the eval diff renders correctly.

## Context

The Learn From Your Mistakes surface is expected to show the evaluation difference between:
- the move the user played in the game
- the best move available at that position

This delta is used to communicate how much was lost by playing the inferior move. It is currently rendering as blank/empty instead of showing the centipawn or mate-distance value.

## Tasks

1. Locate the code responsible for computing or rendering the eval diff in the Learn From Your Mistakes view
2. Determine whether the value is:
   - not being computed (missing logic)
   - computed but not passed through to the view
   - passed through but formatted/rendered incorrectly (e.g. undefined, NaN, empty string)
3. Fix the root cause so the eval diff value displays correctly

## Scope

- Touch only the files involved in computing and rendering the eval diff in Learn From Your Mistakes
- Do not refactor the surrounding analysis or engine layers
- Maximum 1–3 files

## Validation

- [ ] Eval diff between move played and best move is visible and non-blank in Learn From Your Mistakes
- [ ] Value is correct (centipawns or mate notation, matching what the engine reported)
- [ ] No regression in surrounding Learn From Your Mistakes feedback (move quality label, solution reveal, etc.)

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-680
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-680 --checklist "- [ ] Eval diff is visible and non-blank in Learn From Your Mistakes|- [ ] Value is correct (centipawns or mate notation)|- [ ] No regression in surrounding LFYM feedback UI"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-680 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Eval diff is visible and non-blank in Learn From Your Mistakes|- [ ] Value is correct (centipawns or mate notation)|- [ ] No regression in surrounding LFYM feedback UI"
```

# CCP-681 — Learn From Your Mistakes: Feedback UI Text Overflow and Layout Fix

## Summary

After a move is played in Learn From Your Mistakes mode, the feedback UI has text that overflows or appears displaced outside its dedicated container box. Audit how the feedback elements are structured and styled so all UI elements sit cleanly together inside their intended area.

## Context

The feedback panel shown after a move is played in Learn From Your Mistakes is expected to contain all its text and controls inside a bounded box. Currently, text content is escaping that box and appearing displaced. This is a layout/styling bug — the content is present but positioned incorrectly.

## Tasks

1. Locate the code that renders the Learn From Your Mistakes feedback panel (the box that appears after a move is played)
2. Identify which text or elements are overflowing or escaping the container
3. Inspect the CSS/styles applied to the feedback box and its children — check for:
   - missing `overflow: hidden` or `overflow: auto`
   - absolute or fixed positioning pushing elements out of flow
   - missing `flex` or `block` containment on the parent
   - text that lacks wrapping constraints (`white-space`, `word-break`, `max-width`)
   - margin or padding applied to the wrong element causing visual displacement
4. Fix the layout so all feedback elements (move quality label, eval diff, description text, action buttons) sit together inside the feedback box without overflowing

## Scope

- Touch only the feedback panel rendering and its associated styles
- Do not change the content or logic of the feedback — only the layout and containment
- Maximum 1–3 files

## Validation

- [ ] After playing a move in Learn From Your Mistakes, all feedback text stays inside the feedback box
- [ ] No text or UI elements appear displaced or outside the intended container area
- [ ] Move quality label, eval diff, and any action buttons are visually grouped and contained
- [ ] Layout holds across different feedback states (correct, incorrect, best move, good move, etc.)
- [ ] No regression in board layout or surrounding analysis UI

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-681
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-681 --checklist "- [ ] All feedback text stays inside the feedback box after a move|- [ ] No text or UI elements appear displaced or outside their container|- [ ] Move quality label, eval diff, and action buttons are visually grouped|- [ ] Layout holds across correct, incorrect, best move, and good move feedback states|- [ ] No regression in board layout or surrounding analysis UI"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-681 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] All feedback text stays inside the feedback box after a move|- [ ] No text or UI elements appear displaced or outside their container|- [ ] Move quality label, eval diff, and action buttons are visually grouped|- [ ] Layout holds across correct, incorrect, best move, and good move feedback states|- [ ] No regression in board layout or surrounding analysis UI"
```

# CCP-682 — Learn From Your Mistakes: Hide Live Eval During Puzzle Attempt

## Summary

In Learn From Your Mistakes mode, the engine's live evaluation is currently visible while the user is attempting to find the correct move. This spoils the puzzle. The live eval should be hidden while the user is deciding, then cleared automatically when the user moves on to the next mistake.

## Context

In Learn From Your Mistakes, each position is presented as a puzzle — the user is supposed to find the best move without assistance. Displaying the live engine eval while the user is in the attempt phase gives away the answer (positive eval = they need to find a winning move; large swing = something dramatic is happening). This is a spoiler.

The correct behavior (matching Lichess puzzle UX):
- **Before a move is made:** live eval is hidden — no eval bar value, no centipawn number visible
- **After a move is made:** the feedback panel can show the eval diff from the result
- **When advancing to the next mistake:** the eval display resets/clears so it does not carry over

## Tasks

1. Locate where the live engine eval is rendered during the Learn From Your Mistakes session
2. Add a gate that suppresses the eval display while the player has not yet made their move attempt
3. Ensure the suppression clears correctly when:
   - the user makes a move (eval can then be shown as part of feedback if needed)
   - the session advances to the next mistake position (eval resets, hidden again until next attempt)

## Scope

- Touch only the code responsible for showing/hiding the eval during Learn From Your Mistakes
- Do not change eval behavior on the main analysis board outside of LFYM mode
- Maximum 1–3 files

## Validation

- [ ] Live eval is not visible while the user is in the attempt phase (before making a move)
- [ ] Eval display clears when advancing to the next mistake position
- [ ] Eval is not accidentally hidden outside of Learn From Your Mistakes mode (main analysis board unaffected)
- [ ] No regression in the feedback panel or the eval bar in normal analysis mode

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-682
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-682 --checklist "- [ ] Live eval hidden during LFYM attempt phase (before move is made)|- [ ] Eval clears when advancing to next mistake|- [ ] Main analysis board eval unaffected outside LFYM mode|- [ ] No regression in feedback panel or eval bar in normal analysis"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-682 --errors "brief description of what went wrong" --checklist "- [ ] Live eval hidden during LFYM attempt phase (before move is made)|- [ ] Eval clears when advancing to next mistake|- [ ] Main analysis board eval unaffected outside LFYM mode|- [ ] No regression in feedback panel or eval bar in normal analysis"
```

# CCP-683 — Fix evalView TypeScript Strict Errors

## Summary

`src/analyse/evalView.ts` has 8 pre-existing TypeScript strict-mode errors that cause `npx tsc --noEmit` to fail. These were surfaced during CCP-681 validation. Fix them without changing runtime behavior.

## Errors

All errors are in `src/analyse/evalView.ts`:

- Line 281: `Argument of type 'string | undefined' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'`
- Lines 289, 310, 311: `'summary' is possibly 'null'`
- Lines 319, 320, 321: `'worst' is possibly 'null'`

## Tasks

1. Read `src/analyse/evalView.ts` around lines 281 and 289–321 to understand the exact context
2. For line 281: add a null/undefined guard or non-null assertion where appropriate, matching the existing code's intent
3. For the `summary` null cases (289, 310, 311): add guards that match the surrounding logic — do not change what is rendered, only make the access type-safe
4. For the `worst` null cases (319, 320, 321): same approach — guard or assert where the value is provably non-null at that code path

## Scope

- Touch only `src/analyse/evalView.ts`
- Do not change rendered output or runtime behavior
- Do not refactor surrounding logic
- Maximum 1 file

## Validation

- [ ] `npx tsc --noEmit` passes with no errors in `evalView.ts`
- [ ] `npm run build` passes
- [ ] No change in rendered eval summary output in the browser

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-683
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-683 --checklist "- [ ] npx tsc --noEmit passes with no errors in evalView.ts|- [ ] npm run build passes|- [ ] No change in rendered eval summary output in the browser"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:

# CCP-684 — Add Per-Phase Prompt Generation Copy Buttons To Sprint Detail

## Summary

Add a large copy action to each phase shown in sprint detail view:

- button text: `Generate Next Prompts Template`
- behavior: copy the prompt-generation instructions for that exact phase

This must not be implemented as a cosmetic copy button that reuses the current sprint-level
next-phase template for every phase.

Right now the sprint system reliably generates a template only for the sprint's current
`nextAvailablePhase`. That is not sufficient for this feature because the user wants a button on
every listed phase, and the copied template must stay accurate for the exact phase the button
belongs to.

## Goal

Make every phase in sprint detail view capable of copying a phase-specific prompt-generation
template that is accurate for that phase's current task/linkage state.

## Required Behavior

For every phase shown in sprint detail view:
- show a prominent copy button labeled `Generate Next Prompts Template`
- clicking the button copies a phase-specific template for that exact phase
- the template must be derived from current sprint registry truth, not hardcoded prose
- the template must include:
  - sprint id
  - sprint title
  - source sprint doc
  - exact phase id
  - exact phase title
  - tasks in that phase still needing prompts
  - tasks in that phase already covered by linked prompts
  - linked prompt ids for already-covered tasks
  - rules telling the agent not to recreate already-covered prompts unless needed
  - rules telling the agent to use exact sprint linkage fields when creating prompts

## Accuracy Rules

The copied template must be accurate for the selected phase, not only the next available phase.

Use sprint registry data as source of truth for:
- phase/task structure
- task current state
- linked prompt ids

If a phase has no tasks needing prompts:
- still allow the phase button to exist
- but the copied template must say that no tasks in that phase currently need prompts
- and it must still list already-covered tasks for audit/reference context

If a phase is not yet ready because an earlier phase is incomplete:
- the template should still reflect the exact phase state truthfully
- but it should make that dependency clear instead of pretending the phase is currently ready

## Implementation Notes

This likely requires backend generation changes, not just dashboard rendering changes.

Relevant code paths likely include:
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/sprint-registry-lib.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/scripts/generate-prompt-dashboard.mjs`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/prompts/dashboard.html`

Current limitation to address:
- `buildNextPromptsTemplate(...)` currently works only from `sprint.nextAvailablePhase`
- sprint detail currently exposes only the sprint-level next-phase action panel, not per-phase templates

Preferred approach:
- add a reusable phase-specific template builder in sprint registry/dashboard data
- attach a generated template to each phase record in sprint detail data
- render the per-phase copy button from that phase-specific data

## Validation

- open a sprint detail view and confirm every listed phase shows a `Generate Next Prompts Template` copy button
- click the button for the sprint's current next available phase and confirm the copied template matches that phase's current task/linkage state
- click the button for a later not-ready phase and confirm the copied template references that exact phase and accurately reflects its current readiness/task state
- click the button for a completed or already-covered phase and confirm the copied template does not invent missing prompt work
- confirm the existing sprint-level prompt-generation panels still render correctly and do not regress

## Lifecycle

Before making any changes, mark this prompt as started:
```sh
npm run prompt:start -- CCP-684
```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:
```sh
npm run prompt:complete -- CCP-684 --checklist "- [ ] Every sprint detail phase now exposes a Generate Next Prompts Template copy button|- [ ] Per-phase copied templates are generated from exact phase state rather than reused sprint-level next-phase text|- [ ] Not-ready and already-covered phases copy truthful templates instead of misleading ready-to-run instructions|- [ ] Existing sprint detail prompt-generation panels still work after the per-phase addition"
```

If errors or issues were encountered during execution, use `--errors` instead:


# CCP-685 — Fix LFYM Eval Boxes And Puzzle Orientation Regressions

## Summary

Two user-facing regressions need to be fixed in the Learn From Your Mistakes / puzzle flow:

1. In LFYM feedback, the dual eval boxes are still not showing the intended engine-backed
   difference cleanly. The user is seeing output like:

   ```html
   <div class="retro-eval-box retro-eval-box--near"><span class="retro-eval-box__label">vs Engine Best</span><span class="retro-eval-box__value">-0.0</span></div>
   ```

   That is effectively blank/incorrect for the intended UX. The box should show a truthful,
   non-empty eval difference based on the move played versus the best move.

2. When opening a puzzle round, the board orientation is wrong. Puzzle orientation should be from
   the perspective of the user whose puzzles are being solved, not an arbitrary/default side.

## Context

Expected LFYM behavior:
- after a move is judged in LFYM, the feedback area should show meaningful eval comparison values
- the values should be based on the actual engine/review data available for:
  - the played move
  - the engine best move
- the UI should not show effectively empty values like `-0.0` when a real difference exists

Expected puzzle behavior:
- when a puzzle round opens, the board orientation should place the solving user's side at the
  bottom
- this should hold for both locally saved puzzle rounds and any compatible imported/derived puzzle
  flow that uses the same runtime owner

## Scope

Keep this prompt tightly scoped to:
- LFYM dual eval-box value computation / display correctness
- puzzle-round board orientation ownership and application

Do not bundle:
- broader retrospection redesign
- broader puzzle UX redesign
- unrelated board theme/orientation polish
- new puzzle metadata features
- unrelated ceval or review-queue refactors

## Required workflow

1. Inspect the actual current Patzer code first.
2. Locate the current LFYM dual-eval-box computation/render path.
3. Locate the current puzzle-round orientation owner and open-round path.
4. Inspect the relevant Lichess retrospection and puzzle UX sources before deciding how to fix it.
5. Compare:
   - how Patzer currently computes/renders these values
   - how Patzer currently sets puzzle orientation
   - what Lichess confirms about equivalent ownership/behavior
6. Identify the smallest safe implementation step.
7. Implement only that step.
8. Validate with build plus task-specific manual checks.

## Before coding, answer explicitly

Provide:
- prompt id
- task id
- source document
- source step
- relevant Patzer Pro files
- relevant Lichess files, if any
- exact diagnosis
- exact small step to implement
- why that step is safely scoped

If Lichess does not expose an exact equivalent for one of these details, say so clearly instead of
claiming parity that the source does not prove.

## Likely relevant Patzer files

- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/analyse/retroView.ts`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/analyse/retroCtrl.ts`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/analyse/retroMoveHandler.ts`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/analyse/evalDiff.ts`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/puzzles/ctrl.ts`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/puzzles/view.ts`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/board/index.ts`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/main.ts`

## Mandatory reference set

Because this touches LFYM and puzzle runtime behavior, inspect these before implementation:

- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/reference/lichess-retrospection/README.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/reference/lichess-retrospection-ux/README.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/reference/lichess-puzzle-ux/README.md`

And inspect the relevant upstream files they point to, especially:

- `~/Development/lichess-source/lila/ui/analyse/src/retrospect/retroCtrl.ts`
- Lichess puzzle / board-orientation ownership files relevant to opening a solving round

## Validation

Run:

```sh
npm run build
```

Then verify:
- in LFYM, the dual eval boxes no longer show effectively blank values like `-0.0` when a real
  engine/review difference exists
- the displayed LFYM eval-box values are truthful relative to the move played vs engine best
- opening a puzzle round shows the board from the solving user's perspective
- puzzle orientation remains correct after opening/reopening a puzzle round
- no regression in surrounding LFYM feedback or puzzle round behavior
- no console/runtime errors appear

# CCP-680-F1 — Audit LFYM Dual Eval Box Data Flow

## Summary

The prior LFYM eval-display fixes did not solve the real regression. After a move is played in
Learn From Your Mistakes, the UI can still show output like:

- `+1.5`
- `vs Engine Best`
- `—`
- `vs Move Played`
- `—`
- `Chosen because: Missed opportunity`
- `The move played gave up a significant advantage.`

That means the dual eval boxes are rendering, but their underlying comparison values are missing
at the point of render.

This prompt is an audit/discovery prompt, not a blind implementation prompt. The goal is to
identify exactly where the LFYM dual-box data path breaks so the next fix prompt can target the
real seam.

## Problem framing

Current evidence suggests the problem is no longer just formatting:

- both `vs Engine Best` and `vs Move Played` can render as `—`
- that implies one or more of the required solving snapshot values are absent at render time:
  - played-move eval
  - game-move eval
  - engine-best eval
- the break could be in:
  - snapshot computation
  - retro state transition timing
  - ceval-backed data availability
  - render-state selection
  - a mismatch between review-backed and live-eval-backed LFYM flows

Do not guess. Prove the broken seam.

## Scope

Keep this prompt tightly scoped to:

- Learn From Your Mistakes dual eval-box value sourcing
- the data path from move judgment to retro feedback rendering
- identifying the exact missing/null/undefined values and why they are absent
- producing the smallest safe next-step fix recommendation

Do not bundle:

- a broad LFYM redesign
- generic retrospection cleanup
- puzzle product work
- unrelated engine review queue work
- styling-only changes

## Required workflow

1. Inspect the actual Patzer code first.
2. Inspect the relevant mandatory Lichess retrospection references before concluding anything.
3. Trace the full Patzer data path for the dual eval boxes:
   - move played
   - move judged
   - snapshot populated
   - feedback state selected
   - view rendered
4. Identify exactly which values are missing when the boxes show `—`.
5. Determine whether the break is:
   - missing computation
   - missing propagation
   - wrong state timing
   - wrong render-state selection
   - or a mismatch between two LFYM evaluation sources
6. Do not implement a speculative fix unless the broken seam is proven.
7. If the seam is proven and the smallest safe fix is obvious, you may include a recommended
   follow-up implementation step, but this prompt’s main deliverable is the audit result.

## Mandatory reference set

Read these before auditing:

- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/reference/lichess-retrospection/README.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/reference/lichess-retrospection-ux/README.md`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/docs/reference/lichess-puzzle-ux/README.md`

Inspect the relevant linked Lichess source files directly, especially the retrospection / LFYM
state and feedback paths.

If Lichess does not expose the exact same dual-box UI pattern, say so clearly. Use Lichess to
confirm the intended evaluation-state ownership and timing, not to invent unsupported parity claims.

## Likely relevant Patzer files

You must confirm the actual ownership in code, but likely files include:

- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/analyse/retroView.ts`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/analyse/retroCtrl.ts`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/analyse/retroMoveHandler.ts`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/analyse/retro.ts`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/analyse/evalDiff.ts`
- `/Users/leftcoast/Development/PatzerPatzer/src/engine/ctrl.ts`

## Before coding, answer explicitly

Provide:

- prompt id
- task id
- parent prompt id
- source document
- source step
- relevant Patzer Pro files
- relevant Lichess files
- exact diagnosis
- exact missing data seam
- exact smallest safe follow-up step
- why that step is correctly scoped

## Required audit output

Your audit must answer all of these explicitly:

1. What exact values do the dual eval boxes require at render time?
2. Which of those values are actually missing when the UI shows `—`?
3. Where should those values have been populated?
4. At what step are they lost, skipped, or never produced?
5. Is the regression in:
   - move judgment
   - ceval availability
   - snapshot construction
   - feedback state transition
   - or rendering?
6. What is the smallest safe implementation step to fix it?
7. Which file or files should that fix touch?

If you cannot prove the seam, say that clearly and stop at the audit result rather than faking a fix.

## Validation

Run:

```sh
npm run build
```

Also perform code-path validation sufficient to support the audit conclusions.

Then report:

- whether the broken seam was proven
- what values are missing
- where they go missing
- whether a fix should be a view fix, controller fix, or engine/ceval timing fix
- whether build passed
- whether there are console/runtime concerns implied by the current code path

## Lifecycle

```sh

```

After all work is complete, mark it as done:

